title.zh: 卷四十三 朱樂何列傳
title.en: Volume 43: Biographies of Zhu, Yue, He
title.raw: 卷四十三 朱樂何列傳 Volume 43: Biographies of Zhu, Yue, He

sentences.zh: 朱暉字文季，南陽宛人也。

translations.literal: Zhu Hui, courtesy name Wenji, was a man of Wan in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui, whose courtesy name was Wenji, came from Wan in Nanyang.

sentences.zh: [一]家世衣冠。

translations.literal: [Note one] For generations his family had worn caps and gowns (been officials).
translations.idiomatic: [Annotation 1] His family had held office for generations.

sentences.zh: 暉早孤，有氣決。

translations.literal: Hui was orphaned early and had spirit and decisiveness.
translations.idiomatic: He lost his father while still young, yet showed both courage and firm resolve.

sentences.zh: 年十三，王莽敗，天下亂，與外氏家屬從田閒奔入宛城。

translations.literal: At age thirteen, when Wang Mang was defeated and the realm was in chaos, together with his maternal kin he fled from the fields into Wan city.
translations.idiomatic: At thirteen, after Wang Mang's fall threw the empire into turmoil, he fled the countryside with his mother's family and took refuge inside the walls of Wan.

sentences.zh: [二]道遇腢賊，白刃劫諸婦女，略奪衣物。

translations.literal: [Note two] On the road they met bandits who, with naked blades, seized the women and plundered clothing.
translations.idiomatic: [Annotation 2] Along the way they ran into robbers who threatened the women at sword point and stripped them of their garments.

sentences.zh: 昆弟賓客皆惶迫，伏地莫敢動。

translations.literal: His brothers and retainers were all terrified and pressed flat to the ground, none daring to move.
translations.idiomatic: His kinsmen and followers cowered on the ground in panic, too frightened to stir.

sentences.zh: 暉拔□前曰：「財物皆可取耳，諸母衣不可得。

translations.literal: Hui drew [his weapon] and stepped forward, saying, 'You may take all the goods, but you cannot have the mothers' clothing.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui stepped forward with blade drawn and said, 'Take whatever valuables you like, but you will not have these women's clothes.

sentences.zh: 今日朱暉死日也！

translations.literal: Today is the day Zhu Hui dies!'
translations.idiomatic: If you insist, then today is the day I die!'

sentences.zh: 」賊見其小，壯其志，笑曰：「童子內刀。

translations.literal: ' The bandits saw he was small, admired his resolve, laughed and said, 'Boy, sheath your knife.
translations.idiomatic: The robbers saw how young he was yet respected his courage; they laughed and told him, 'Put the blade away, lad.

sentences.zh: 」遂捨之而去。

translations.literal: ' Then they released him and departed.
translations.idiomatic: They let him go and moved on.

sentences.zh: 注[一]東觀記曰「其先宋微子之後也，以國氏姓。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Eastern Han Records says, 'His ancestors were posterity of Weizi of Song, and took their surname from the state.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: The Eastern Han Records states that his line descended from Weizi of Song and bore the state's name as their clan.

sentences.zh: 周衰，諸侯滅宋，罐碭，易姓為朱，後徙於宛」也。

translations.literal: When the Zhou declined, the feudal lords destroyed Song; [they fled] to Dang, changed their surname to Zhu, and later moved to Wan.'
translations.idiomatic: When Zhou power waned and the states wiped out Song, the family fled to Dang, adopted the surname Zhu, and eventually resettled in Wan.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]東觀記曰「暉外祖父孔休，以德行稱於代」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Eastern Han Records says, 'Hui's maternal grandfather Kong Xiu was famed in his generation for virtue and conduct.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: The Eastern Han Records adds that his maternal grandfather Kong Xiu was celebrated in his day for moral character.

sentences.zh: 初，光武與暉父岑俱學長安，有舊故。

translations.literal: In the beginning, Guangwu and Hui's father Cen had studied together in Chang'an and had an old friendship.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, Emperor Guangwu and Zhu Hui's father, Zhu Cen, had been fellow students in Chang'an and were old friends.

sentences.zh: 及即位，求問岑，時已卒，乃召暉拜為郎。

translations.literal: When he ascended the throne he inquired after Cen, but by then Cen had already died, so he summoned Hui and appointed him gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: After he took the throne he asked after Zhu Cen, only to learn he had died; he then called Zhu Hui to court and gave him a post as a gentleman.

sentences.zh: 暉尋以病去，卒業於太學。

translations.literal: Hui soon left on account of illness and completed his studies at the Imperial Academy.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui soon resigned because of ill health and finished his training at the Imperial Academy.

sentences.zh: 性矜嚴，進止必以禮，諸儒稱其高。

translations.literal: By nature he was stern and dignified; in advancing and withdrawing he invariably followed ritual, and the scholars praised his loftiness.
translations.idiomatic: He was austere and punctilious, and every move he made conformed to propriety, which won him the respect of the scholarly community.

sentences.zh: 永平初，顯宗舅新陽侯陰就慕暉賢，自往候之，暉避不見。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Yongping, the Exalted Ancestor's maternal uncle, Marquis of Xinyang Yin Jiu, admiring Hui's worthiness, went in person to visit him, but Hui avoided him and would not meet.
translations.idiomatic: Early in the Yongping era, Yin Jiu, the Marquis of Xinyang and maternal uncle of Emperor Ming, admired Zhu Hui's reputation and called on him in person; Zhu Hui declined to receive him.

sentences.zh: 復遣家丞致禮，[一] 暉遂閉門不受。

translations.literal: He again sent his household steward to present gifts; [note one] Hui then barred his gate and would not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Jiu sent his household steward with gifts a second time, yet Zhu Hui shut his door and refused the offerings.

sentences.zh: 就聞，歎曰：「志士也，勿奪其節。

translations.literal: When Jiu heard of it, he sighed and said, 'A man of purpose; do not wrest his integrity from him.
translations.idiomatic: When Yin Jiu heard this, he sighed, 'He is a man of principle; no one should force him to compromise his integrity.

sentences.zh: 」後為郡吏，太守阮況嘗欲市暉*

translations.literal: ' Later he became a clerk in the commandery; the grand administrator Ruan Kuang once wished to buy from Hui
translations.idiomatic: Later, as a commandery clerk, he served under Grand Administrator Ruan Kuang, who once tried to buy from him

translations.literal: (an ox)
translations.idiomatic: an ox

translations.literal: **[a maidservant]*; Hui did not consent.
translations.idiomatic: and a maidservant; Zhu Hui refused.

sentences.zh: [二]及況卒，暉乃厚贈送其家。

translations.literal: [Note two] When Kuang died, Hui then sent generous gifts to his family.
translations.idiomatic: [Annotation 2] After Ruan Kuang died, Zhu Hui sent his family a substantial funeral gift.

sentences.zh: 人或譏焉，暉曰：「前阮府君有求於我，所以不敢聞命，誠恐以財貨□君。

translations.literal: Some mocked him for it. Hui said, 'Formerly Lord Ruan had a request of me; therefore I did not dare obey his command—I truly feared that by goods and money I would [stain] the lord.
translations.idiomatic: People criticized him for this. Zhu Hui replied, 'When Lord Ruan wanted those things from me, I dared not comply because I was afraid accepting payment would compromise him.

sentences.zh: 今而相送，明吾非有愛也。

translations.literal: Now in sending this gift, I make clear that I had no stinginess.'
translations.idiomatic: By contributing now, I show I was never begrudging—only careful.'

sentences.zh: 」驃騎將軍東平王蒼聞而辟之，甚禮敬焉。

translations.literal: ' When General of Agile Cavalry, the Prince of Dongping Cang, heard of it, he summoned him and treated him with great courtesy and respect.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Cang of Dongping, the general of agile cavalry, heard the story and recruited him, honoring him with exceptional deference.

sentences.zh: 正月朔旦，蒼當入賀。

translations.literal: On the new year's morning of the first month, Cang was to enter to offer congratulations.
translations.idiomatic: On New Year's Day of the first month, Prince Cang was due at court for the congratulatory audience.

sentences.zh: 故事，少府給璧。

translations.literal: By precedent, the privy treasurer supplied the jade disk.
translations.idiomatic: By established practice the privy treasurer provided the ceremonial jade.

sentences.zh: 是時陰就為府卿，貴驕，吏□不奉法。

translations.literal: At this time Yin Jiu was chief of the office, noble and arrogant, and the clerks [acted wrongly] and did not uphold the law.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Jiu was then chief of the privy treasury; swollen with pride at his rank, his subordinates flouted regulations.

sentences.zh: 蒼坐朝堂，漏且盡，而求璧不可得，顧謂掾屬曰：「若之何？

translations.literal: Cang sat in the court hall; the clepsydra was nearly exhausted, yet the jade disk could not be obtained. He looked around and said to his clerks, 'What is to be done?
translations.idiomatic: Prince Cang waited in the court until the water clock was almost dry, but the jade still had not arrived. Turning to his staff he asked, 'What can we do?

sentences.zh: 」暉望見少府主簿持璧，即往紿之曰：[三]「我數聞璧未嘗見，試請觀之。

translations.literal: ' Hui saw from afar the privy treasurer's chief clerk holding the disk; he went at once and deceived him, saying, [three] 'I have often heard of the disk but never seen it; I beg to try to look at it.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui spotted the chief clerk of the privy treasury carrying the jade. He walked up and coaxed him, [3] saying, 'I have heard so much about that disk but never laid eyes on it—may I have a look?

sentences.zh: 」主簿以授暉，暉顧召令史奉之。

translations.literal: ' The chief clerk handed it to Hui; Hui looked back and summoned a clerk to present it.
translations.idiomatic: The clerk handed it over; Zhu Hui immediately signaled an aide to carry it away to the prince.

sentences.zh: [四]主簿大驚，遽以白就。

translations.literal: [Four] The chief clerk was greatly alarmed and hastily reported to Jiu.
translations.idiomatic: [4] The chief clerk panicked and rushed to tell Yin Jiu.

sentences.zh: 就曰：「朱掾義士，勿復求。

translations.literal: Jiu said, 'Clerk Zhu is a man of righteousness; do not seek it further.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Jiu said, 'Zhu Hui is a man of honor; do not ask for it back.

sentences.zh: 」更以它璧朝。

translations.literal: ' They used another disk for the court appearance.
translations.idiomatic: They substituted another jade for the audience.

sentences.zh: 蒼既罷，召暉謂曰：「屬者掾自視孰與藺相如？」

translations.literal: When Cang had finished, he summoned Hui and said, 'Just now, how do you yourself compare to Lin Xiangru?'
translations.idiomatic: Afterward Prince Cang called Zhu Hui in and asked, 'How do you think your exploit today measures against Lin Xiangru's?

sentences.zh: [五]帝聞壯之。

translations.literal: [Five] The emperor heard and thought him heroic.
translations.idiomatic: [5] When the emperor heard the tale, he admired Zhu Hui's boldness.

sentences.zh: 及當幸長安，欲嚴宿□，故以暉為衛士令。

translations.literal: When he was about to visit Chang'an and wished to tighten night watch [duties], therefore he made Hui captain of the guard.
translations.idiomatic: Planning a journey to Chang'an, the emperor wanted the palace watch strengthened, so he appointed Zhu Hui captain of the guards.

sentences.zh: 再遷臨淮太守。

translations.literal: He was transferred twice to be grand administrator of Linhuai.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted twice, eventually becoming grand administrator of Linhuai.

sentences.zh: 注[一]續漢志曰：「諸侯家丞，秩三百石。」

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Continued Han Treatises says, 'Stewards of feudal lords, rank salary three hundred bushels.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: The Continued Han Treatises notes that stewards of imperial princes held rank at three hundred bushels.

sentences.zh: 注[二]東觀記曰：「暉為*

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Eastern Han Records says, 'Hui was
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: The Eastern Han Records relates that Zhu Hui served as

translations.literal: (clerk)
translations.idiomatic: a clerk

sentences.zh: *督郵，況當歸女，欲買暉婢，暉不敢與。

translations.literal: *supervising secretary; Kuang was about to marry off a daughter and wished to buy Hui's maidservant; Hui did not dare give her.
translations.idiomatic: supervising secretary when Ruan Kuang, preparing to marry off his daughter, wanted to buy Zhu Hui's maidservant; Zhu Hui dared not sell her.

sentences.zh: 後況卒，暉送其家金三斤。」

translations.literal: Later when Kuang died, Hui sent his family three jin of gold.'
translations.idiomatic: After Ruan Kuang died, Zhu Hui sent his household three jin of gold.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]紿，欺也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Dai means deceive.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Dai means to deceive or trick.

sentences.zh: 注[四]奉之於蒼。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Presented it to Cang.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: That is, he had it delivered to Prince Cang.

sentences.zh: 注[五]屬，向也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Shu means 'just now' / toward.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: Shu here means 'a moment ago' or 'just then.'

sentences.zh: 與猶如也。

translations.literal: Yu is like ru ('like').
translations.idiomatic: Yu here has the same force as ru, 'like' or 'as.'

sentences.zh: 史記曰，藺相如，趙人也。

translations.literal: The Records of the Historian says: Lin Xiangru was a man of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: The Records of the Grand Historian identifies Lin Xiangru as a native of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 趙惠文王時得楚和氏璧，秦昭王欲以十五城易之，趙王使相如奉璧入秦。

translations.literal: In the time of King Huiwen of Zhao he obtained the He clan disk from Chu; King Zhaoxiang of Qin wished to exchange fifteen cities for it; the king of Zhao sent Xiangru to bear the disk into Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Under King Huiwen of Zhao the state acquired the He-family jade from Chu. King Zhaoxiang of Qin offered fifteen cities in trade, so the king of Zhao dispatched Lin Xiangru to carry the disk into Qin.

sentences.zh: 秦王大喜，無意償趙城。

translations.literal: The king of Qin was greatly pleased and had no intention of compensating Zhao with cities.
translations.idiomatic: The Qin ruler was delighted but never intended to hand over the promised towns.

sentences.zh: 相如乃前曰：「璧有瑕，願指示王。

translations.literal: Xiangru then stepped forward and said, 'The disk has a flaw; I wish to point it out to the king.
translations.idiomatic: Lin Xiangru stepped forward and said, 'There is a blemish on the jade; allow me to show Your Majesty.

sentences.zh: 」相如因持璧卻立倚柱，怒發上衝冠，曰：「臣觀大王無償趙城色，故臣復取璧。

translations.literal: ' Xiangru therefore took the disk, stepped back, stood leaning on a pillar; his hair in anger rose up to strike his cap, and he said, 'Your servant observed that the great king had no intention of compensating Zhao with cities; therefore your servant took back the disk.
translations.idiomatic: He took the jade, retreated to a pillar, and with hair bristling against his cap declared, 'I saw that Your Majesty had no intention of yielding those cities to Zhao, so I took the disk back.

sentences.zh: 大王必欲急臣，臣今頭與璧俱碎於柱矣。

translations.literal: If the great king must press your servant, then now your servant's head and the disk together will be shattered on the pillar.'
translations.idiomatic: If you force me, my skull and this jade will both shatter against this pillar!'

sentences.zh: 」相如持其璧睨柱，欲以擊柱。

translations.literal: ' Xiangru held the disk, glanced sidelong at the pillar, intending to strike the pillar with it.
translations.idiomatic: He held the disk, eyed the pillar, and made as if to smash the jade against it.

sentences.zh: 秦王恐其璧破，乃謝之。

translations.literal: The king of Qin feared the disk would break and apologized to him.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing the jade would be ruined, the king of Qin apologized.

sentences.zh: 暉好節□，有所拔用，皆厲行士。

translations.literal: Hui loved integrity [and propriety]; those whom he promoted and employed were all men of stern conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui valued integrity; everyone he singled out for office was a man of upright, rigorous character.

sentences.zh: 其諸報怨，以義犯率，皆為求其理，多得生濟。

translations.literal: In all cases of answering resentment, of offending the law for righteousness's sake, he all sought their principles for them, and many obtained life and relief.
translations.idiomatic: When people struck back at wrongs or broke the letter of the law for a just cause, he worked their cases through proper channels and often saved them from execution.

sentences.zh: 其不義之囚，實時僵仆。

translations.literal: As for prisoners who were unjust, in fact promptly they stiffened and fell (died).
translations.idiomatic: Prisoners whose guilt was genuine died on schedule.

sentences.zh: [一]吏人畏愛，為之歌曰：「強直自遂，南陽朱季。

translations.literal: [One] Officials and people feared and loved him; for him they made a song: 'Strong and straight, following his own course—Zhu Ji of Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The clerks and common folk both feared and loved him, and sang: 'Firm and upright, he goes his own way—Zhu Ji from Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 吏畏其威，人懷其惠。

translations.literal: Officials fear his awesomeness; people cherish his kindness.'
translations.idiomatic: Clerks dread his sternness; the people remember his kindness.'

sentences.zh: 」[二]數年，坐法免。

translations.literal: ' [Two] After several years, he was dismissed for violating the law.
translations.idiomatic: [2] A few years later he lost his post for a legal infraction.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]僵，偃；

translations.literal: [Three] Commentary [one]: Jiang means yan;
translations.idiomatic: [3] Commentary 1: Jiang means to fall prostrate;

sentences.zh: 僕，踣也。

translations.literal: pu means stumble and fall.
translations.idiomatic: pu means to collapse or topple.

sentences.zh: 注[二]東觀記曰：「建武十六年，四方牛大疫，臨淮獨不，鄰郡人多牽牛入界。」

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Eastern Han Records says, 'In the sixteenth year of Jianwu, cattle in the four directions had a great plague; Linhuai alone did not; people of neighboring commanderies often led cattle into its borders.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: The Eastern Han Records records that in Jianwu 16 a severe cattle plague swept the empire while Linhuai was spared, so herdsmen from neighboring commanderies drove their stock across its border.

sentences.zh: 注[三]東觀記曰：「坐考長吏囚死獄中，州奏免官。」

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The Eastern Han Records says, 'He sat in judgment because a senior official's prisoner died in jail; the province memorialized and he was dismissed from office.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: The Eastern Han Records adds that he was removed from office after a prisoner died under interrogation ordered by a senior official he was examining, and the province reported the case.

sentences.zh: 暉剛於為吏，見忌於上，所在多被劾。

translations.literal: Hui was rigid in serving as an official, was envied by superiors, and wherever he was often suffered impeachment.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui's uncompromising style as an official earned him enemies among his superiors, and he was frequently impeached wherever he served.

sentences.zh: 自去臨淮，屏居野澤，布衣蔬食，不與邑裡通，鄉黨譏其介。

translations.literal: From the time he left Linhuai, he lived in seclusion in marshes and wilds, cloth garments and vegetable food, not communicating with the village; his township fellows mocked his aloofness.
translations.idiomatic: After leaving Linhuai he withdrew to the countryside, dressed in plain cloth and lived simply, cut himself off from neighbors, and local opinion ridiculed him for being too standoffish.

sentences.zh: [一]建初中，南陽大饑，米石千餘，暉盡散其家資，以分宗裡故舊之貧羸者，鄉族皆歸焉。

translations.literal: [One] In the middle of Jianchu, Nanyang had a great famine; rice was over a thousand cash per shi; Hui completely dispersed his household property to divide among clansmen, village, and old acquaintances who were poor and weak; the township and clan all turned to him.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Midway through the Jianchu era Nanyang suffered a terrible famine—grain cost more than a thousand cash a bushel—yet Zhu Hui gave away his entire fortune to clansmen, neighbors, and old friends who were starving, and the whole community came to rely on him.

sentences.zh: 初，暉同縣張堪素有名稱，嘗於太學見暉，甚重之，接以友道，乃把暉臂曰：「欲以妻子托朱生。

translations.literal: In the beginning, Zhang Kan of Hui's same county had always had a good reputation; he once saw Hui at the Imperial Academy, greatly esteemed him, received him with the way of friendship, then grasped Hui's arm and said, 'I wish to entrust wife and children to Master Zhu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Kan of the same county had long enjoyed a fine name. When he met Zhu Hui at the Imperial Academy he thought the world of him, treated him as a true friend, took his arm, and said, 'I want to leave my wife and children in your care, Zhu.

sentences.zh: 」暉以堪先達，舉手未敢對，自後不復相見。

translations.literal: ' Hui because Kan was senior raised his hand and did not dare reply; from then on they did not again meet each other.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui, out of deference to Zhang Kan as his senior, raised his hand in salute but could not bring himself to answer, and the two never met again.

sentences.zh: 堪卒，暉聞其妻子貧困，乃自往候視，厚賑贍之。

translations.literal: When Kan died, Hui heard his wife and children were in poverty, then went in person to visit and look after them, generously relieving and supporting them.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Kan died, Zhu Hui learned that his family was destitute, called on them in person, and gave generous support.

sentences.zh: 暉少子頡怪而問曰：「大人不與堪為友，平生未曾相聞，子孫竊怪之。

translations.literal: Hui's youngest son Jie wondered and asked, 'The great man was not friends with Kan, in life never had mutual communication; we children privately find it strange.
translations.idiomatic: His youngest son, Zhu Jie, asked in puzzlement, 'You were never close to Zhang Kan and scarcely exchanged a word with him in all those years—we find this hard to understand.

sentences.zh: 」暉曰：「堪嘗有知己之言，吾以信於心也。

translations.literal: ' Hui said, 'Kan once had words of one who knows me; I by trust stored it in my heart.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui replied, 'He once spoke to me as a true friend would; I pledged myself in silence.'

sentences.zh: 」[二]暉又與同郡陳揖交善，揖早卒，有遺腹子友，暉常哀之。

translations.literal: ' [Two] Hui also was on good terms with Chen Yi of the same commandery; Yi died early, having a posthumous son You; Hui always pitied him.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Zhu Hui was also close to Chen Yi of the same commandery. When Chen died young, leaving a son, Chen You, born after his death, Zhu Hui watched over the boy with constant sympathy.

sentences.zh: 及司徒桓虞為南陽太守，召暉子駢為吏，暉辭駢而薦友。

translations.literal: When Minister of Education Huan Yu became grand administrator of Nanyang, he summoned Hui's son Pian to be a clerk; Hui declined Pian and recommended You.
translations.idiomatic: When Huan Yu became grand administrator of Nanyang, he offered a clerkship to Zhu Hui's son Zhu Pian; Zhu Hui declined on his son's behalf and recommended Chen You instead.

sentences.zh: 虞歎息，遂召之。

translations.literal: Yu sighed and then summoned him.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Yu was moved and summoned Chen You.

sentences.zh: 其義烈若此。

translations.literal: His righteous ardor was like this.
translations.idiomatic: Such was the steadfastness of his sense of honor.

sentences.zh: 注[一]介，特也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Jie means peculiar / alone.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: Jie means singular or aloof.

sentences.zh: 言不與觿同。

translations.literal: The saying is he did not join with the common belt-hooks (the crowd).
translations.idiomatic: That is, he did not mingle with the ordinary run of people.

sentences.zh: 注[二]以堪先托妻子，心已許之，故言信於心也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Because Kan first entrusted wife and children, in heart he had already promised him; therefore it says trust in the heart.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Zhang Kan had entrusted his family in spirit if not in words, and Zhu Hui had already given his word in his heart.

sentences.zh: 元和中，肅宗巡狩，告南陽太守問暉起居，召拜為尚書僕射。

translations.literal: In the middle of Yuanhe, the Solemn Ancestor on tour announced to the grand administrator of Nanyang to ask after Hui's well-being, summoned and appointed him vice director of the secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: During the Yuanhe era, Emperor Zhang, on an imperial progress, sent word through the grand administrator of Nanyang to inquire after Zhu Hui's health, then summoned him and appointed him vice director of the secretariat.

sentences.zh: 歲中遷太山太守。

translations.literal: Within the year he was transferred to be grand administrator of Mount Tai.
translations.idiomatic: That same year he was promoted to grand administrator of Taishan.

sentences.zh: 暉上疏乞留中，詔許之。

translations.literal: Hui submitted a memorial begging to remain at court; an edict permitted it.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui petitioned to stay at the capital rather than take up the provincial post, and the throne granted his request.

sentences.zh: 因上便宜，陳密事，深見嘉納。

translations.literal: Thereupon he presented convenient proposals, stated secret matters, and was deeply praised and accepted.
translations.idiomatic: He followed with practical policy suggestions and confidential memorials, which the court warmly endorsed.

sentences.zh: 詔報曰：「補公家之闕，[一]不累清白之素，斯善美之士也。

translations.literal: An edict replied, saying: 'To remedy the court's deficiencies, [one] not dragging down the plainness of purity—such is a good and fine gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor replied in an edict: 'You supply what the state lacks [1] without tarnishing your reputation for integrity—that is the mark of an admirable man.

sentences.zh: 俗吏苟合，阿意面從，進無謇謇之志，卻無退思之念，[二]患之甚久。

translations.literal: Vulgar clerks agree hastily, fawn and follow to the face; advancing they have no jianjian resolve, retreating they have no thought of withdrawing to reflect—[two] I have worried about this very long.
translations.idiomatic: Mediocre officials trim their sails, nod along to please their betters, show no loyal frankness in office, and never pause to mend their faults on their own [2]; I have long deplored that habit.

sentences.zh: 惟今所言，適我願也。

translations.literal: Only what you have now said suits my wish.
translations.idiomatic: What you have just said is exactly what I hoped to hear.

sentences.zh: 生其勉之！

translations.literal: Sir, exert yourself therein!'
translations.idiomatic: Take this to heart, good sir, and strive all the harder!'

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]詩曰：「袞職有闕，仲山甫補之。」

translations.literal: ' Commentary [one]: The Classic of Poetry says, 'Where the ceremonial robe's office had a gap, Zhong Shanfu mended it.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: The Book of Songs says, 'Where the king's robe was wanting, Zhong Shanfu made it whole.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]易蹇卦艮下坎上，艮為山，坎為水，山上有水，蹇難之象也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Changes' hexagram Jian: Gen below, Kan above; Gen is mountain, Kan is water; water on a mountain is the image of difficulty.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: The hexagram Jian in the Changes stacks Gen (mountain) beneath Kan (water); water above a mountain symbolizes hardship and obstruction.

sentences.zh: 六二爻上應於五，五為君位，二宜為臣也。

translations.literal: The second yin line above corresponds to the fifth; the fifth is the lord's position; the second fittingly is the minister.
translations.idiomatic: The second line answers the fifth, which stands for the ruler, while the second fittingly represents the minister.

sentences.zh: 居儉難之時，履當其位，不以五在難私身遠害，故曰「王臣蹇蹇，匪躬之故」。

translations.literal: Dwelling in times of straitened difficulty, treading in its proper place, not because the fifth is in difficulty privately keeping the body far from harm—therefore it says 'The king's minister jian jian, not for the body's sake.'
translations.idiomatic: In perilous times he holds the minister's place yet does not save himself by abandoning his lord in distress; hence the text reads, 'The king's minister goes faithfully through hardship—not for his own sake.'

sentences.zh: 孝經曰：「退思補過。

translations.literal: The Classic of Filial Piety says: 'In withdrawal think of mending faults.
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Filial Piety says, 'In retirement reflect on correcting your errors.

sentences.zh: 」「謇」與「蹇」通。

translations.literal: ' 'Jian' and 'jian' are interchangeable.
translations.idiomatic: The characters jian (outspoken) and jian (hardship) are used interchangeably in this gloss.

sentences.zh: 是時谷貴，縣官經用不足，[一]朝廷憂之。

translations.literal: At this time grain was dear, the state's regular expenditures were insufficient, [one] the court worried about it.
translations.idiomatic: Grain had grown costly and the treasury could not meet its routine outlays [1], which alarmed the court.

sentences.zh: 尚書張林上言：「谷所以貴，由錢賤故也。

translations.literal: Director of the secretariat Zhang Lin submitted words: 'The reason grain is dear is because cash is cheap.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Lin of the secretariat argued that grain was expensive because copper coin was undervalued.

sentences.zh: 可盡封錢，一取布帛為租，以通天下之用。

translations.literal: One can completely seal cash, uniformly take cloth and silk as rent, in order to circulate use throughout the realm.
translations.idiomatic: He proposed halting the circulation of coin, collecting taxes in cloth and silk alone, and thus restoring liquidity to the economy.

sentences.zh: 又鹽，食之急者，雖貴，人不得不須，官可自□。

translations.literal: Moreover salt, of food the urgent thing—though dear, people cannot but need it; the government can itself [monopolize].
translations.idiomatic: Salt is a necessity of the table; however high the price, people must buy it—so the state might as well retail it itself and capture the revenue.'

sentences.zh: [二]又宜因交址﹑益州上計吏往來，市珍寶，收采其利，武帝時所謂均輸者也。

translations.literal: [Two] Further it is fitting to rely on the coming and going of presentation clerks from Jiaozhi and Yizhou to buy precious goods and gather their profit—what in Emperor Wu's time was called equal transport.
translations.idiomatic: [2] He also urged using the annual journeys of account clerks from Jiaozhi and Yizhou to buy up valuables and capture the markup—the policy Han Wudi had called 'balanced delivery.'

sentences.zh: 」[三]於是詔諸尚書通議。

translations.literal: ' [Three] Thereupon an edict ordered all directors of the secretariat to deliberate jointly.
translations.idiomatic: [3] The throne then ordered the secretariat directors to debate the proposal in full.'

sentences.zh: 暉奏據林言不可施行，事遂寢。

translations.literal: Hui submitted a memorial citing Lin's words as not practicable to implement; the matter then was shelved.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui memorialized that Zhang Lin's plan was unworkable, and the proposal was dropped.

sentences.zh: 後陳事者復重述林前議，以為於國誠便，帝然之，有詔施行。

translations.literal: Later those who presented affairs again repeated Lin's earlier proposal, deeming it truly convenient for the state; the emperor approved it, and there was an edict to put it into practice.
translations.idiomatic: Later memorialists revived Zhang Lin's scheme, insisting it would serve the empire; the emperor agreed and issued orders to carry it out.

sentences.zh: 暉復獨奏曰：

translations.literal: Hui again alone submitted a memorial, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui once more filed a lone memorial:

sentences.zh: 「王制，天子不言有無，諸侯不言多少，祿食之家不與百姓爭利。

translations.literal: 'By the royal regulations, the Son of Heaven does not speak of having or not having; feudal lords do not speak of more or less; families on salary do not contend for profit with the common people.
translations.idiomatic: 'The royal code says the Son of Heaven does not haggle over scarcity and plenty, nobles do not quibble over quantities, and those who live on state stipends must not compete with the people for gain.'

sentences.zh: 今均輸之法與賈販無異，鹽利歸官，則下人窮怨，布帛為租，則吏多奸盜，誠非明主所當宜行。

translations.literal: Now the equal-transport method differs in nothing from peddling merchants; if salt profit returns to the government, then those below will be impoverished and resentful; if cloth and silk become rent, then clerks will mostly be treacherous and thieving—truly not what an enlightened ruler ought fittingly to practice.
translations.idiomatic: Balanced delivery would reduce the court to huckstering. State monopoly on salt would beggar and embitter the populace; collecting taxes in cloth would invite clerkly fraud and theft. No enlightened sovereign should follow such a course.'

sentences.zh: 」帝卒以林等言為然，得暉重議，因發怒，切責諸尚書。

translations.literal: ' The emperor in the end took Lin and others' words as right; obtaining Hui's repeated deliberation, therefore he grew angry and severely rebuked the directors of the secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had already sided with Zhang Lin; Zhu Hui's second memorial provoked his wrath, and he roundly berated the entire secretariat.'

sentences.zh: 暉等皆自系獄。

translations.literal: Hui and the others all bound themselves and went to prison.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui and his colleagues placed themselves under arrest.

sentences.zh: 三日，詔敕出之。

translations.literal: After three days, an edict ordered them released.
translations.idiomatic: Three days later an edict ordered their release.

sentences.zh: 曰：「國家樂聞駁議，黃發無愆，詔書過耳，[四]何故自系？

translations.literal: It said: 'The state delights to hear refuting proposals; hoary heads have no fault—the edict was excessive; [four] why did you bind yourselves?'
translations.idiomatic: It read: 'The government welcomes dissent. Gray-haired ministers owe no apology—the fault lay with my edict. [4] Why imprison yourselves?'

sentences.zh: 」暉因稱病篤，不肯復署議。

translations.literal: ' Hui thereupon claimed grave illness and refused again to sign deliberations.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui pleaded serious illness and declined to countersign any further deliberations.'

sentences.zh: 尚書令以下惶怖，謂暉曰：「今臨得譴讓，柰何稱病，其禍不細！

translations.literal: From the secretariat director downward they were frightened and said to Hui: 'Now on the verge of receiving blame and reproach—how can you claim illness? The disaster will not be slight!'
translations.idiomatic: The secretariat staff panicked and urged him, 'Rebuke is about to fall on us all—how can you fall ill now? The consequences will be grave!'

sentences.zh: 」暉曰：「行年八十，蒙恩得在機密，當以死報。

translations.literal: ' Hui said: 'My years have reached eighty; receiving favor I have been able to be in confidential affairs—I ought to repay with death.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui answered, 'I am eighty and have been honored with a place in the inner councils; I owe the throne a life for that.'

sentences.zh: 若心知不可而順旨雷同，負臣子之義。

translations.literal: If in heart knowing it is not right yet following the decree in unison echoing, that betrays the duty of minister and son.
translations.idiomatic: To know a policy is wrong yet parrot the edict like everyone else would betray every duty I owe as a subject.'

sentences.zh: 今耳目無所聞見，伏待死命。

translations.literal: Now ears and eyes have nothing to hear or see; I prostrate awaiting the command of death.
translations.idiomatic: I have nothing more to say; I await whatever sentence you impose.'

sentences.zh: 」遂閉口不復言。

translations.literal: ' Then he closed his mouth and spoke no more.
translations.idiomatic: With that he fell silent and would not speak again.'

sentences.zh: 諸尚書不知所為，乃共劾奏暉。

translations.literal: The directors of the secretariat did not know what to do, then jointly impeached and memorialized Hui.
translations.idiomatic: The secretariat directors, at a loss, joined in a memorial impeaching Zhu Hui.

sentences.zh: 帝意解，寑其事。

translations.literal: The emperor's intent relaxed, and he shelved the affair.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor's anger cooled and he let the matter drop.

sentences.zh: 後數日，詔使直事郎問暉起居，[五]太醫視疾，太官賜食。

translations.literal: After several days, an edict sent the duty gentleman to ask after Hui's well-being, [five] the imperial physician to examine his illness, the grand provisioner to grant food.
translations.idiomatic: A few days later the court dispatched a duty gentleman to inquire after Zhu Hui's health, [5] sent the imperial physician to treat him, and ordered the palace kitchen to send meals.

sentences.zh: 暉乃起謝，復賜錢十萬，布百匹，衣十領。

translations.literal: Hui then rose and thanked; again he was granted one hundred thousand cash, one hundred bolts of cloth, ten suits of clothing.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Hui rose to give thanks; he also received a hundred thousand cash, a hundred bolts of cloth, and ten suits of clothing.

sentences.zh: 注[一]經，常也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Jing means constant.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: Jing here means 'regular' or 'routine.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]前書曰：「因官器作□鹽。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The former Han History says, 'By official implements they made [boiled] salt.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: The History of the Former Han states that the government boiled salt with official equipment.'

sentences.zh: 」音義曰：「□，古『煮』字。」

translations.literal: ' The Sounds and Meanings says: '[The graph] is the ancient character for boil.'
translations.idiomatic: The Sounds and Meanings gloss identifies the lacuna as the old graph for 'to boil.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]武帝作均輸法，謂州郡所出租賦，並雇運之直，官總取之，市其土地所出之物。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Emperor Wu made the equal-transport law, meaning the rent and levies yielded by commanderies and prefectures, together with the cost of hired transport, the government gathered in sum and purchased the goods produced by their lands.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Emperor Wu's equal-transport system pooled each region's tax grain and shipping fees so the state could buy local specialties in bulk.

sentences.zh: 官自轉輸於京，謂之均輸。

translations.literal: The government itself transported to the capital—this is called equal transport.
translations.idiomatic: Officials then moved those goods to the capital themselves—that was 'balanced delivery.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]黃發，老稱。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Yellow hair is a term for the old.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: 'Yellow hair' is a polite epithet for the aged.

sentences.zh: 謂朱暉也。

translations.literal: It refers to Zhu Hui.
translations.idiomatic: That is, it refers to Zhu Hui.

sentences.zh: 注[五]直事郎謂署郎當次直者。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Duty gentleman means a gentleman of the suite who is on rotation for duty.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: A duty gentleman was a palace gentleman whose turn it was to stand watch.

sentences.zh: 後遷為尚書令，以老病乞身，拜騎都尉，賜錢二十萬。

translations.literal: Later he was promoted to be secretariat director; on account of old age and illness he begged his person; he was appointed chief of cavalry and granted two hundred thousand cash.
translations.idiomatic: He was later promoted to secretariat director, then retired on grounds of age and ill health with appointment as chief of cavalry and a gift of two hundred thousand cash.

sentences.zh: 和帝即位，竇憲北征匈奴，暉復上疏諫。

translations.literal: When Emperor He ascended the throne, Dou Xian campaigned north against the Xiongnu; Hui again submitted a memorial remonstrating.
translations.idiomatic: After Emperor He came to the throne, Dou Xian led a northern campaign against the Xiongnu; Zhu Hui again memorialized in protest.

sentences.zh: 頃之，病卒。

translations.literal: Shortly thereafter, he died of illness.
translations.idiomatic: He died of illness soon afterward.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]華嶠書曰「暉年五十失妻，昆弟欲為繼室，暉歎曰：『時俗希不以後妻敗家者！

translations.literal: [One] Commentary [one]: Hua Qiao's book says, 'When Hui was fifty he lost his wife; his brothers wished to arrange a successor wife; Hui sighed and said: 'In the customs of the time rarely is there one who does not by a later wife ruin the family!'
translations.idiomatic: [1] Hua Qiao's history records that when Zhu Hui was fifty his wife died and his brothers urged him to remarry. He sighed, 'Hardly anyone in our day escapes ruin when he takes a second wife!'

sentences.zh: 』遂不復娶」也。

translations.literal: ' Then he did not again marry.'
translations.idiomatic: So he never married again.'

sentences.zh: 子頡，修儒術，安帝時至陳相。

translations.literal: His son Jie cultivated Confucian learning; in Emperor An's time he reached being chancellor of Chen.
translations.idiomatic: His son Zhu Jie studied the Confucian classics and rose under Emperor An to chancellor of Chen.

sentences.zh: 頡子穆。

translations.literal: Jie's son was Mu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jie's son was Zhu Mu.

sentences.zh: 穆字公叔。

translations.literal: Mu, courtesy name Gongshu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu, whose courtesy name was Gongshu,

sentences.zh: 年五歲，便有孝稱。

translations.literal: At age five, then he had a reputation for filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: was already known at the age of five for filial devotion.

sentences.zh: 父母有病，輒不飲食，差乃復常。

translations.literal: When father and mother were ill, then he would not eat or drink; when they improved, then he returned to normal.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever his parents fell ill he refused food until they recovered.

sentences.zh: 及壯耽學，銳意講誦，或時思至，不自知亡失衣冠，顛隊坑岸。

translations.literal: When grown he was addicted to learning, keenly intent on lecturing and reciting; sometimes when thought arrived, without himself knowing he lost cap and garments, stumbled and fell into ditch banks.
translations.idiomatic: As a man he gave himself to study with fierce concentration; when inspiration struck he would lose track of his hat and shoes and stumble into ditches without noticing.

sentences.zh: 其父常以為專愚，幾不知數馬足。

translations.literal: His father constantly thought him solely foolish, nearly not knowing to count horses' legs.
translations.idiomatic: His father dismissed him as obtuse—almost as absentminded as the steward who could not count the horses in the stable.

sentences.zh: [一]穆愈更精篤。

translations.literal: [One] Mu became still more refined and earnest.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Zhu Mu only grew more single-minded in his scholarship.

sentences.zh: 注[一]幾音近衣反。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Ji sound near yi fan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: Ji is read with the same initial as yi (approximate fanqie spelling).

sentences.zh: 前書曰：「石慶為太僕，上問車中幾馬？

translations.literal: The former Han History says: 'Shi Qing was grand coachman; the sovereign asked how many horses in the carriage?'
translations.idiomatic: The History of the Former Han tells how Grand Coachman Shi Qing was asked how many horses drew the imperial carriage.'

sentences.zh: 慶以策數馬畢，舉手曰『六馬。

translations.literal: Qing with whip counted horses finished, raised hand and said 'six horses.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Qing counted them with his whip and answered, 'Six horses.'

sentences.zh: 』」言穆用心專愚更甚也。

translations.literal: ' ' It means Mu's using mind solely foolish was even more.
translations.idiomatic: The point is that Zhu Mu's bookish absorption surpassed even that story.'

sentences.zh: 初舉孝廉。

translations.literal: In the beginning he was recommended filial and incorrupt.
translations.idiomatic: He was first recommended as filial and incorrupt.

sentences.zh: [一]順帝末，江淮盜賊腢起，州郡不能禁。

translations.literal: [One] At the end of Emperor Shun, along the Yangzi and Huai bandits and thieves rose in bands; commanderies and prefectures could not restrain them.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Late in Emperor Shun's reign banditry flared along the Yangzi and Huai, beyond the power of local officials to suppress.

sentences.zh: 或說大將軍梁冀曰：「朱公叔兼資文武，海內奇士，若以為謀主，賊不足平也。

translations.literal: Someone persuaded the great general Liang Ji, saying: 'Zhu Gongshu combines endowment in civil and military; within the seas a strange knight—if you make him chief planner, the bandits will not be enough to pacify.
translations.idiomatic: Someone urged the great general Liang Ji: 'Zhu Gongshu is both scholar and strategist, a rare talent in the realm; appoint him your chief counselor and the rebels will soon be crushed.'

sentences.zh: 」冀亦素聞穆名，乃辟之，使典兵事，甚見親任。

translations.literal: ' Ji also had always heard Mu's name, then summoned him, made him oversee military affairs, and greatly was he trusted and employed.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji had long heard of Zhu Mu, summoned him to his staff, put him in charge of military planning, and came to rely on him intimately.'

sentences.zh: 及桓帝即位，順烈太后臨朝，穆以冀埶地親重，望有以扶持王室，因推災異，奏記以勸戒冀曰：「穆伏念明年丁亥之歲，刑德合於干位，[二]易經龍戰之會。

translations.literal: When Emperor Huan ascended the throne, Empress Dowager Shunlie held court; Mu because Ji's power, position, and closeness were heavy, hoped there would be something to support the royal house; therefore he extended portents and anomalies, submitted a written memorial to admonish Ji, saying: 'Mu prostrate thinks on next year's dinghai year, punishment and virtue join at the stem position, [two] the meeting of dragons battling in the Changes.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Huan came to the throne and Empress Dowager Shunlie ruled as regent, Zhu Mu hoped that Liang Ji, with his immense power and proximity to the throne, might strengthen the house of Han. Citing omens, he sent Liang Ji a long letter of warning: 'I have calculated that in the coming dinghai year the cosmic forces of virtue and punishment will converge on the stem position—[2] the configuration the Book of Changes calls "dragons fighting in the field."'

sentences.zh: 其文曰：『龍戰於野，其道窮也。

translations.literal: Its text says: 'Dragons battle in the field; their way is exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: The line reads, "Dragons contend in the open country; their path has reached an end."'

sentences.zh: 』[三]謂陽道將勝而陰道負也。

translations.literal: ' [Three] It means the yang way will prevail and the yin way be defeated.
translations.idiomatic: [3] That is, the yang principle is about to triumph while yin must give way.'

sentences.zh: 今年九月天氣鬱冒，五位四候連失正氣，此互相明也。

translations.literal: This year in the ninth month the weather was depressed and obscuring; the five positions and four seasons in succession lost correct qi—this mutually clarifies one another.
translations.idiomatic: This past ninth month the heavens hung heavy and murky; the five planetary stations and four seasonal winds have fallen out of alignment—omens that reinforce one another.

sentences.zh: 夫善道屬陽，惡道屬陰，若修正守陽，摧折惡類，則福從之矣。

translations.literal: The good way belongs to yang, the evil way belongs to yin; if you correct and cultivate and guard yang, crush and break evil kinds, then blessing will follow.
translations.idiomatic: Good aligns with yang and evil with yin; if you hold fast to what is right and crush wicked factions, good fortune will follow.

sentences.zh: 穆每事不逮，所好唯學，傳受於師，時有可試。

translations.literal: Mu in every affair does not reach; what he loves is only learning, receiving transmission from teachers; at times there may be something to try.
translations.idiomatic: I am no statesman; my only strength is scholarship learned from my teachers, yet even that may sometimes prove useful.

sentences.zh: 願將軍少察愚言，申納諸儒，[四]而親其忠正，絕其姑息，[五]專心公朝，割除私慾，廣求賢能，斥遠佞惡。

translations.literal: I hope the general will slightly examine my foolish words, extend and accept the various Confucians, [four] and be intimate with their loyal correctness, cut off their indulgent forbearance, [five] concentrate mind on the public court, cut away private desires, broadly seek worthy and able, reject and distance flattery and evil.
translations.idiomatic: I beg you to weigh these clumsy words, give ear to the scholars, [4] draw close to the loyal and upright, reject easy appeasement, [5] fix your mind on the public good, curb private appetite, seek out talent widely, and drive sycophants and villains from your side.'

sentences.zh: 夫人君不可不學，當以天地順道漸漬其心。

translations.literal: As for the lord of men, he cannot but study; ought by heaven and earth's compliant way gradually soak his heart.
translations.idiomatic: A ruler must never stop learning; let the natural order of heaven and earth slowly reshape his heart.'

sentences.zh: 宜為皇帝選置師傅及侍講者，得小心忠篤敦禮之士，將軍與之俱入，參勸講授，師賢法古，此猶倚南山坐平原也，誰能傾之！

translations.literal: It is fitting for the emperor to select and install tutors and attendants for lecturing, obtaining careful loyal earnest reverent ritual gentlemen; the general with them together enter, join in urging lecture and instruction, take teachers worthy and model on antiquity—this is like leaning on South Mountain sitting on a plain—who can overturn it!'
translations.idiomatic: Choose tutors and readers for the emperor—men of loyalty, gravity, and ritual—and attend the lectures with them. Take the worthy as teachers and antiquity as your model. That would be like resting against Mount Tai while seated on the plain; who could shake you?'

sentences.zh: 今年夏，月暈房星，明年當有小□。

translations.literal: This year in summer, the moon haloed the House constellation; next year there ought to be a small [calamity].
translations.idiomatic: This summer a lunar halo ringed the House asterism; expect a minor disaster next year.'

sentences.zh: 宜急誅奸臣為天下所怨毒者，以塞災咎。

translations.literal: It is fitting urgently to execute treacherous ministers whom the realm hates and poisons, in order to stop disasters and faults.
translations.idiomatic: Execute at once the wicked ministers whom the empire detests, and you will avert Heaven's rebuke.'

sentences.zh: 議郎﹑大夫之位，本以式序儒術高行之士，今多非其人；

translations.literal: The positions of gentleman consultant and grandee, fundamentally were used to order Confucian learning and high-conduct gentlemen; now many are not their persons;
translations.idiomatic: The posts of consultant and grandee were meant to rank men of learning and integrity; too many incumbents are unworthy.'

sentences.zh: 九卿之中，亦有乖其任者。

translations.literal: Among the nine ministers also there are those who violate their charge.
translations.idiomatic: Even among the nine ministers some betray their trust.'

sentences.zh: 惟將軍察焉。

translations.literal: Only the general examine this.
translations.idiomatic: I leave these matters to your judgment, General.'

sentences.zh: 」又薦種暠﹑欒巴等。

translations.literal: ' He also recommended Zhong Hao, Luan Ba, and others.
translations.idiomatic: He also recommended Zhong Hao, Luan Ba, and others.'

sentences.zh: 而明年嚴鮪謀立清河王蒜，又黃龍二見沛國。

translations.literal: And the next year Yan Wei plotted to establish Prince Suan of Qinghe; moreover two yellow dragons appeared in Pei state.
translations.idiomatic: The following year Yan Wei conspired to set Prince Suan of Qinghe on the throne, and pair of yellow dragons was sighted in the kingdom of Pei.

sentences.zh: 冀無術學，遂以穆「龍戰」之言為應，於是請暠為從事中郎，薦巴為議郎，舉穆高第，為侍御史。

translations.literal: Ji had no technical learning; therefore he took Mu's 'dragons battle' words as corresponding; thereupon he requested Hao as retainer attendant gentleman, recommended Ba as gentleman consultant, raised Mu in high grade, as attendant censor.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji, no scholar himself, decided Zhu Mu's talk of battling dragons had come true. He appointed Zhong Hao retainer attendant gentleman, nominated Luan Ba as consultant, and advanced Zhu Mu with highest marks to attendant censor.

sentences.zh: [六] 注[一]謝承書曰「穆少有英才，學明五經。

translations.literal: [Six] Commentary [one]: Xie Cheng's book says, 'Mu in youth had outstanding talent, learning clarified the Five Classics.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Xie Cheng's history says that Zhu Mu showed exceptional gifts in youth and mastered the Five Classics.'

sentences.zh: 性矜嚴疾惡，不交非類。

translations.literal: By nature stern and strict, hating evil, he did not associate with non-kind.
translations.idiomatic: He was austere, hated wickedness, and kept company only with the worthy.'

sentences.zh: 年二十為郡督郵，迎新太守，見穆曰：『君年少為督郵，因族埶？

translations.literal: At twenty he was commandery supervising secretary; welcoming the new grand administrator, seeing Mu he said: 'You are young to be supervising secretary—because of clan power?'
translations.idiomatic: At twenty he became a commandery supervising secretary. When the new grand administrator arrived he asked Zhu Mu, 'How does a man your age hold this post—family influence?'

sentences.zh: 為有令德？

translations.literal: Or because there is fine virtue?'
translations.idiomatic: Or because you possess true virtue?'

sentences.zh: 』穆荅曰：『郡中瞻望明府謂如仲尼，非顏回不敢以迎孔子。

translations.literal: ' Mu answered saying: 'Within the commandery gazing up they deem the enlightened prefect like Zhongni; were it not Yan Hui I would not dare thereby to welcome Confucius.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu replied, 'The whole commandery looks up to Your Honor as to Confucius himself; only a Yan Hui would be fit to welcome Confucius.'

sentences.zh: 』更問風俗人物。

translations.literal: ' Again he asked customs and persons.
translations.idiomatic: The administrator went on to ask about local customs and leading figures.'

sentences.zh: 太守甚奇之，曰：『僕非仲尼，督郵可謂顏回也。

translations.literal: The grand administrator greatly marveled at him, saying: 'I am not Zhongni; the supervising secretary may be called Yan Hui.
translations.idiomatic: The grand administrator was deeply impressed. 'I am no Confucius,' he said, 'but you may well be my Yan Hui.'

sentences.zh: 』遂歷職股肱，舉孝廉」也。

translations.literal: ' Then he passed through offices as thigh and arm, was recommended filial and incorrupt.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu went on to serve him in key posts and was eventually recommended as filial and incorrupt.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]曆法，太歲在丁﹑壬，歲德在北宮，太歲在亥﹑卯﹑未，歲刑亦在北宮，故合於干位也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: In calendrical method, when Grand Year is at ding or ren, year's virtue is in north palace; when Grand Year is at hai, mao, or wei, year's punishment also is in north palace; therefore it joins at the stem position.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: When the Grand Year stands at ding or ren, the year's virtue lodges in the northern palace; when it stands at hai, mao, or wei, the year's punishment also lies in the northern palace—hence the convergence on the stem position for dinghai.

sentences.zh: 注[三]易坤卦上六象詞也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: It is the image word of the Changes' Kun hexagram top six.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: The line is the image commentary on the top yin line of the Kun hexagram.

sentences.zh: 以爻居上六，故雲其道窮也。

translations.literal: Because the line occupies top six, therefore it says its way is exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: Because the line sits at the top of Kun, the text says its path has reached its limit.

sentences.zh: 王弼注云：「陰之為道，卑順不逆，乃全其美，盛而不已。

translations.literal: Wang Bi's commentary says: 'Yin's being the way, lowly compliant not opposing, then completes its beauty; flourishing yet not stopping.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Bi comments: 'Yin should stay lowly and yielding; only thus does it preserve its excellence. When it swells without end,'

sentences.zh: 固陽之地，陽所不堪，故戰於野。」

translations.literal: firmly in yang's territory, what yang cannot bear, therefore battles in the field.'
translations.idiomatic: it occupies ground yang cannot tolerate, and so the dragons fight in the open field.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]申，重也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Shen means heavy / repeat.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: Shen means 'to extend' or 'double.'

sentences.zh: 注[五]姑，且也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Gu means moreover / for the moment.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: Gu here means 'for the nonce' or 'temporarily.'

sentences.zh: 息，安也。

translations.literal: Xi means peace.
translations.idiomatic: Xi means 'peace' or 'ease.'

sentences.zh: 小人之道，苟且取安也。

translations.literal: The petty man's way is to be careless for the moment and take peace.
translations.idiomatic: The small man's way is to settle for a quiet life at any cost.

sentences.zh: 禮記曰「君子之愛人也以德，細人之愛人也以姑息」也。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites says: 'The gentleman's loving people is by virtue; the petty man's loving people is by indulgent forbearance.'
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Rites says, 'The gentleman cherishes others by building their character; the small man cherishes them by letting things slide.'

sentences.zh: 注[六]續漢書曰：「穆舉高第，拜侍御史。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: The Continued Han History says: 'Mu was raised in high grade, appointed attendant censor.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 6: The Continued Han History records that Zhu Mu passed with highest honors and was appointed attendant censor.'

sentences.zh: 桓帝臨辟雍，行禮畢，公卿出，虎賁置弓階上，公卿下階皆避弓。

translations.literal: Emperor Huan attended the circular moat; when rites were finished, dukes and ministers came out; tiger braves placed bows on the steps; dukes and ministers descending the steps all avoided the bows.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Huan visited the imperial academy and the ceremony ended, the high ministers filed out. Palace guards laid ceremonial bows on the stairs, and every minister stepped around them on the way down.

sentences.zh: 穆過，呵虎賁曰：『執天子器，何故投於地！

translations.literal: Mu passed, shouted at the tiger braves saying: 'You hold the Son of Heaven's implements—why cast them on the ground!'
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu stopped and rebuked the guards: 'These are the Son of Heaven's regalia—why throw them on the steps?'

sentences.zh: 』虎賁怖，即攝弓。

translations.literal: ' The tiger braves were frightened and immediately gathered the bows.
translations.idiomatic: The guards, alarmed, picked the bows up at once.'

sentences.zh: 穆劾奏虎賁抵罪，公卿皆籩，曰『朱御史可謂臨事不惑者也』。」

translations.literal: Mu impeached and memorialized the tiger braves to answer for crime; dukes and ministers all were abashed and said 'Attendant Censor Zhu may be called one who facing affairs is not confused.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu memorialized to punish the guards. The ministers were abashed and said, 'Censor Zhu keeps his head when others lose theirs.'

sentences.zh: 時同郡趙康叔盛者，隱於武當山，清靜不仕，以經傳教授。

translations.literal: At that time Zhao Kang, courtesy name Shusheng, of the same commandery, hid on Mount Wudang, pure and quiet not serving in office, by classics and commentaries taught.
translations.idiomatic: A fellow townsman, Zhao Kang (courtesy name Shusheng), had withdrawn to Mount Wudang, where he lived in seclusion, declined office, and taught the classics.

sentences.zh: 穆時年五十，乃奉書稱弟子。

translations.literal: Mu at that time was fifty; then he presented a letter calling himself disciple.
translations.idiomatic: At fifty Zhu Mu sent him a letter enrolling himself as a student.

sentences.zh: 及康歿，喪之如師。

translations.literal: When Kang died, he mourned him as a teacher.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhao Kang died, Zhu Mu mourned him as he would a master.

sentences.zh: 其尊德重道，為當時所服。

translations.literal: His honoring virtue and esteeming the Way was by the age submitted to.
translations.idiomatic: His reverence for learning and moral character won wide admiration.

sentences.zh: 常感時澆薄，慕尚敦篤，乃作祟厚論。

translations.literal: He constantly was moved that the age was thin and diluted, admired and esteemed honest and earnest, then made a Treatise on Honoring Thickness.
translations.idiomatic: Distressed by the thinness of public morals, he longed for plain sincerity and composed his Treatise on Honoring Thickness.

sentences.zh: 其辭曰：

translations.literal: Its words say:
translations.idiomatic: It begins:

sentences.zh: 夫俗之薄也，有自來矣。

translations.literal: As for custom's being thin, it has come from antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: The coarsening of manners is nothing new.

sentences.zh: 故仲尼歎曰：「大道之行也，而丘不與焉。

translations.literal: Therefore Zhongni sighed and said: 'When the great way was practiced, yet Qiu did not participate in it.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius once lamented, 'The great way once prevailed in the world, yet I was not born in time to see it.'

sentences.zh: 」[一]蓋傷之也。

translations.literal: ' [One]—generally he grieved for it.
translations.idiomatic: [1]—because the loss filled him with sorrow.'

sentences.zh: 夫道者，以天下為一，在彼猶在己也。

translations.literal: As for the way, it takes the realm as one; in that other it is as in oneself.
translations.idiomatic: The Way treats the empire as a single whole; another's good is one's own.

sentences.zh: 故行違於道則愧生於心，非畏義也；

translations.literal: Therefore when conduct violates the Way, then shame is born in the heart—not from fearing righteousness;
translations.idiomatic: When your deeds stray from the Way, shame rises within you; that is not mere fear of moral censure.

sentences.zh: 事違於理則負結於意，非憚禮也。

translations.literal: When affairs violate principle, then burden knots in the intent—not from fearing ritual.
translations.idiomatic: When affairs run counter to right principle, your conscience is burdened; that is not mere dread of ritual forms.

sentences.zh: 故率性而行謂之道，[二]得其天性謂之德。

translations.literal: Therefore following nature and acting is called the Way, [two] obtaining one's heaven-given nature is called virtue.
translations.idiomatic: To follow innate nature in action is the Way; [2] to preserve that heaven-endowed nature intact is virtue.

sentences.zh: [三] 德性失然後貴仁義，[四]是以仁義起而道德遷，[五]禮法興而淳樸散。

translations.literal: [Three] When virtue-nature is lost, then benevolence and righteousness are prized, [four] therefore benevolence and righteousness arise and the Way and virtue shift, [five] ritual and law rise and simplicity and plainness scatter.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Only after innate virtue is lost do people prize benevolence and duty; [4] hence those virtues appear and the true Way recedes; [5] as ritual and penal law multiply, simplicity vanishes.

sentences.zh: 故道德以仁義為薄，淳樸以禮法為賊也。

translations.literal: Therefore the Way and virtue take benevolence and righteousness as thin; simplicity and plainness take ritual and law as injury.
translations.idiomatic: Thus the higher Way looks on forced benevolence and duty as a thin substitute, and natural simplicity sees elaborate ritual and statutes as its destroyer.

sentences.zh: [六]夫中世之所敦，已為上世之所薄，[七] 況又薄於此乎！

translations.literal: [Six] As for what the middle age esteems, already it is what the high age deemed thin, [seven] how much more what is thinner than this!
translations.idiomatic: [6] The virtues later ages hold dear were already thought shallow in high antiquity; [7] how much more contemptible are the habits of our own day!

sentences.zh: 注[一]禮記仲尼歎曰：「大道之行，三代之英，丘未之逮也，而有志焉。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Record of Rites: Zhongni sighed and said: 'When the great way was practiced, the excellence of the three dynasties—Qiu did not reach it, yet had the will toward it.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: The Book of Rites records Confucius sighing, 'The age when the great way prevailed, the finest rulers of the three dynasties—I never lived to see them, though I aspired to their standard.'

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注曰：「大道，謂三皇﹑五帝時也。」

translations.literal: ' Zheng Xuan's commentary says: 'The great way means the time of the Three August Ones and Five Emperors.'
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses the great way as the era of the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]率，循也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Shuai means follow.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Shuai means to follow or accord with.

sentences.zh: 子思曰「天命之謂性，率性之謂道，修道之謂教」也。

translations.literal: Zisi said 'What heaven commands is called nature; following nature is called the Way; cultivating the Way is called teaching.'
translations.idiomatic: Zisi wrote, 'What heaven imparts is nature; to follow nature is the Way; to cultivate the Way is education.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]天之所命之謂性，不失天性是為德。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: What heaven commands is called nature; not losing heaven-given nature is called virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Heaven's endowment is nature; virtue means never forfeiting that endowment.

sentences.zh: 注[四]道德之性失，仁義之多彰。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: When the nature of the Way and virtue is lost, benevolence and righteousness multiply and show.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: Once the innate Way is lost, people parade an excess of preached benevolence and duty.

sentences.zh: 注[五]遷，徙也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Qian means shift / move.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: Qian means to shift aside or depart.

sentences.zh: 注[六]老子曰：「失道而後德，失德而後仁，失仁而後義，失義而後禮。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Laozi says: 'When the Way was lost, then there was virtue; when virtue was lost, then there was benevolence; when benevolence was lost, then there was righteousness; when righteousness was lost, then there was ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 6: Laozi says, 'When the Way is lost, men speak of virtue; when virtue is lost, of benevolence; when benevolence is lost, of righteousness; when righteousness is lost, of ritual.'

sentences.zh: 夫禮者，忠信之薄而亂之首也。」

translations.literal: As for ritual, it is the thinning of loyalty and trustworthiness and the head of chaos.'
translations.idiomatic: Ritual is the scant remnant of good faith and the first step toward disorder.'

sentences.zh: 注[七]中世謂五帝時。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: Middle age means the time of the Five Emperors.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 7: 'Middle age' here denotes the era of the Five Emperors.

sentences.zh: 故夫天不崇大則覆幬不廣，地不深厚則載物不博，[一]人不敦厖則道數不遠。

translations.literal: Therefore if heaven is not lofty and great then its covering canopy is not broad; if earth is not deep and thick then what it bears is not extensive, [one] if man is not honest and great then the Way's reckoning does not reach far.
translations.idiomatic: If heaven were not vast, it could not shelter all beneath it; if earth were not deep, it could not sustain every creature; [1] if a man lacks breadth of character, he cannot grasp the subtle principles of the Way.

sentences.zh: [二] 昔在仲尼不失舊於原壤，[三]楚嚴不忍章於絕纓。

translations.literal: [Two] Formerly Zhongni did not lose old friendship toward Yuan Rang, [three] Chu Yan (Zhuang) could not bear to expose at severing tassel.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Confucius never cast off his old friend Yuan Rang; [3] King Zhuang of Chu refused to expose the courtier who had tugged his consort's sash when the lamps went out.

sentences.zh: [四]由此觀之，聖賢之德敦矣。

translations.literal: [Four] From this observing, the virtue of sages and worthies is honest.
translations.idiomatic: [4] From this we see how large-minded the sages truly were.

sentences.zh: 老氏之經曰：「大丈夫處其厚不處其薄，居其實不居其華，故去彼取此。」

translations.literal: Laozi's classic says: 'The great man dwells in the thick, not in the thin; abides in the fruit, not in the flower; therefore discards that and takes this.'
translations.idiomatic: Laozi teaches that the true man stands on what is solid, not on what is shallow; he rests in substance, not in show—so he leaves the one and holds to the other.'

sentences.zh: [五]夫時有薄而厚施，行有失而惠用。

translations.literal: [Five] As for the age being thin yet thick bestowal, conduct having fault yet kindness employed.
translations.idiomatic: [5] When the times turn mean, respond with generosity; when others stumble, treat them with kindness.

sentences.zh: [六]故覆人之過者，敦之道也；

translations.literal: [Six] Therefore covering people's faults is the way of honesty;
translations.idiomatic: [6] To shield another's error is the path of true magnanimity;

sentences.zh: 救人之失者，厚之行也。

translations.literal: saving people's slips is the conduct of thickness.
translations.idiomatic: to rescue a man from his mistakes is the practice of deep humanity.

sentences.zh: 往者，馬援深昭此道，可以為德，誡其兄子曰：「吾欲汝曹聞人之過如聞父母之名。

translations.literal: In the past, Ma Yuan deeply understood this way, could take it as virtue, admonished his elder brother's sons, saying: 'I desire you fellows to hear people's faults as hearing father and mother's names.
translations.idiomatic: Ma Yuan grasped this truth and made it a family rule, telling his nephews, 'I want you to react to gossip about others' faults as you would to hearing your parents' personal names.'

sentences.zh: 耳可得聞，口不得言。

translations.literal: The ear may obtain to hear; the mouth may not obtain to speak.
translations.idiomatic: You may hear such things with your ears, but you must not repeat them with your mouth.'

sentences.zh: 」斯言要矣。

translations.literal: ' This saying is essential.
translations.idiomatic: Those words hit the mark.'

sentences.zh: 遠則聖賢履之上世，[七]近則丙吉﹑張子孺行之漢廷。

translations.literal: In the far case, sages and worthies trod it in high antiquity, [seven] in the near case Bing Ji and Zhang Ziru practiced it in the Han court.
translations.idiomatic: [7] The ancients lived by this principle; in our own dynasty Bing Ji and Zhang Ziru put it into practice at court.

sentences.zh: [八]故能振英聲於百世，播不滅之遺風，不亦美哉！

translations.literal: [Eight] Therefore able to rouse heroic fame through a hundred generations, sow undying leftover wind—is it not also beautiful!
translations.idiomatic: [8] Hence their noble reputations ring down the centuries and an imperishable breeze of virtue still stirs—is that not splendid!

sentences.zh: 注[一]幬亦覆。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Chou also means cover.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: Chou here means 'to cover' or 'shelter.'

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「如天之無不燾，如地之無不載。

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says: 'As heaven's nothing not covering, as earth's nothing not bearing.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Commentary says heaven hides nothing in its canopy and earth supports nothing beneath its load.'

sentences.zh: 」「幬」與「燾」同。

translations.literal: ' 'Chou' and 'dao' are the same.
translations.idiomatic: The graphs chou and dao are equivalent here.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]敦厖，厚大也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Dun pang means thick and great.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Dun pang means broad and substantial.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「人生敦厖。

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says: 'Human life dun pang.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Commentary speaks of a man 'born to breadth of character.'

sentences.zh: 」數猶理也。

translations.literal: ' Shu is like li (pattern).
translations.idiomatic: Shu here means principle or inner pattern.'

sentences.zh: 言人不敦厚，不能入道之精理也。

translations.literal: It means if man is not honest and thick, he cannot enter into the Way's subtle li.
translations.idiomatic: Without generous depth of character one cannot fathom the subtle truths of the Way.

sentences.zh: 注[三]原壤，孔子之舊也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Yuan Rang was Confucius's old (friend).
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Yuan Rang was an old acquaintance of Confucius.

sentences.zh: 禮記曰：「原壤之母死，孔子助之沐幟。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites says: 'When Yuan Rang's mother died, Confucius assisted him in washing the funeral canopy.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Rites records that when Yuan Rang's mother died, Confucius helped him prepare the mortuary drapery.'

sentences.zh: 原壤登木而歌曰：『狸首之班然，執女手之卷然。

translations.literal: Yuan Rang climbed a tree and sang: 'The badger-head's speckled pattern, holding your hand's curled softness.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Rang climbed a beam and sang, 'How dappled the fox's head, how soft the hand I hold.'

sentences.zh: 』從者曰：『子未可以已乎？

translations.literal: ' Followers said: 'Master, may you not yet stop?'
translations.idiomatic: His followers asked, 'Master, can you not put a stop to this?'

sentences.zh: 』夫子曰：『親者無失其為親，故者無失其為故。

translations.literal: ' The Master said: 'Kin—do not lose their being kin; old—do not lose their being old.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius replied, 'Kin remain kin, and old friends remain friends—that is all.'

sentences.zh: 』」注[四]說苑曰：「楚莊王賜腢臣酒，日暮燭滅，乃有人引美人之衣者。

translations.literal: ' ' Commentary [four]: The Garden of Persuasions says: 'King Zhuang of Chu granted assembled ministers wine; at sunset the candles went out, then there was someone who pulled the beautiful woman's garment.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: Liu Xiang's Garden of Persuasions tells how King Zhuang of Chu feasted his ministers; when dusk came and the torches died, one guest tugged at a lady of the court.'

sentences.zh: 美人援絕其冠纓，告王趣火來上，視絕纓者。

translations.literal: The beautiful woman pulled and broke his cap tassel, told the king hasten fire come up, look at the tassel-severer.
translations.idiomatic: She tore off his hat cord and asked the king to relight the lamps so the culprit could be identified.

sentences.zh: 王曰：『賜人酒，使醉失禮，柰何欲顯婦人之節而辱士乎？

translations.literal: The king said: 'Having given people wine, causing them drunk to lose ritual—how can I desire to display the woman's chastity and humiliate gentlemen?'
translations.idiomatic: The king said, 'I gave them wine; if drunken discourtesy follows, why should I vindicate a woman's modesty at a gentleman's expense?'

sentences.zh: 』乃命左右曰：『與寡人飲，不絕冠纓者不歡。

translations.literal: ' Then he ordered left and right saying: 'Who drinks with me—who does not break cap tassels is not merry.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered his attendants, 'Let no man call himself merry at my feast until he has snapped his hat cord.'

sentences.zh: 』腢臣百餘人皆絕去其冠纓，乃上火」也。

translations.literal: ' Over a hundred assembled ministers all broke off their cap tassels, then they raised fire.'
translations.idiomatic: More than a hundred ministers ripped their cords free, and only then were the lights brought back.'

sentences.zh: 注[五]此老子*[道]*德經之詞也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: This is wording from Laozi's *[Way]* Virtue classic.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: The quotation comes from Laozi's Daodejing.

sentences.zh: 顧歡注曰：「道德為厚，禮法為薄，清虛為實，聲色為華。

translations.literal: Gu Huan's commentary says: 'The Way and virtue are thick, ritual and law are thin; pure emptiness is fruit, sound and color are flower.
translations.idiomatic: Gu Huan comments that the Way and inner power are the solid foundation, ritual and law the shallow veneer; stillness is substance, sensual display mere blossom.'

sentences.zh: 去彼華薄，取此厚實。」

translations.literal: Discard that flower and thin, take this thick and fruit.'
translations.idiomatic: Reject the showy and thin; embrace the deep and real.'

sentences.zh: 注[六]俗之凋薄，以厚御之；

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Custom's withering thinness, with thickness govern it;
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 6: Meet social decay with steadfast generosity;

sentences.zh: 行*[之]*有失，以惠待之。

translations.literal: conduct *[having]* fault, with kindness treat it.
translations.idiomatic: meet others' missteps with forgiving kindness.

sentences.zh: 即上孔子﹑楚莊是也。

translations.literal: That is above Confucius, Chu Zhuang are thus.
translations.idiomatic: That is precisely what Confucius and King Zhuang of Chu exemplified.

sentences.zh: 注[七]履，踐也。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: Lu means tread.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 7: Lu means to tread or put into practice.

sentences.zh: 言敦厚之道，孔子﹑楚莊已踐履之。

translations.literal: It means the way of honest thickness, Confucius and Chu Zhuang already trod it.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius and King Zhuang had already walked this path of magnanimity.

sentences.zh: 注[八]宣帝時丙吉為丞相，不案吏，曰：「夫以三公府案吏，吾竊陋之。

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: In Emperor Xuan's time Bing Ji was chancellor, did not investigate clerks, said: 'As for using the three dukes' office to investigate clerks, I privately find it mean.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 8: Under Emperor Xuan, Chancellor Bing Ji refused to grill his subordinates, saying, 'I would be ashamed to see the highest office used as an inquisitorial court.'

sentences.zh: 」子孺為車騎將軍，匿名遠權，隱人過失。

translations.literal: ' Ziru was chariot-and-cavalry general, concealed name, distanced power, hid people's faults.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ziru, as general of chariots and cavalry, shunned publicity and power and glossed over others' errors.'

sentences.zh: 然而時俗或異，風化不敦，而尚相誹謗，謂之臧否。

translations.literal: Yet the age's customs perhaps differ; transformation of manners is not honest, yet they esteem mutual slander, calling it praise-and-blame.
translations.idiomatic: Yet customs change; when public morals grow lax, people take delight in mutual slander and call it honest appraisal.

sentences.zh: 記短則兼折其長，貶惡則並伐其善。

translations.literal: Recording shortness then together breaks their length; demoting evil then together cuts their good.
translations.idiomatic: To harp on a man's faults is to deny his strengths; to dwell on his vices is to erase his virtues.

sentences.zh: 悠悠者皆是，其可稱乎！

translations.literal: The vast many are all thus—can it be praised!
translations.idiomatic: The common herd all behave so—can that be called admirable!

sentences.zh: [一]凡此之類，豈徒乖為君子之道哉，將有危身累家之禍焉。

translations.literal: [One] All this kind—how merely do they violate the way of being a gentleman—there will be danger to body and disaster heaping the family.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Such conduct does not merely break the gentleman's code; it invites ruin upon oneself and one's kin.

sentences.zh: 悲夫！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: How lamentable!

sentences.zh: 行之者不知憂其然，故害興而莫之及也。

translations.literal: Those who practice it do not know to worry that it is so; therefore harm arises and none reach it (prevent it).
translations.idiomatic: Those who indulge in it never foresee the end; disaster strikes before they can mend their ways.

sentences.zh: 斯既然矣，又有異焉。

translations.literal: This being already so, again there is strangeness.
translations.idiomatic: Nor is that the whole evil.

sentences.zh: 人皆見之而不能自遷。

translations.literal: People all see it yet cannot themselves change.
translations.idiomatic: Everyone sees the danger yet few can turn themselves around.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: What then?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 務進者趨前而不顧後，榮貴者矜己而不待人，智不接愚，富不賑貧，貞士孤而不恤，賢者□而不存。

translations.literal: Those who strive for advance hurry forward and do not look behind; those who are honored and noble are proud of self and do not treat others; the wise do not reach to the foolish, the rich do not relieve the poor, upright gentlemen are alone and not pitied, worthies [perish] and are not preserved.
translations.idiomatic: The ambitious press ahead and never look back; the privileged preen themselves and spare no thought for others. The clever ignore the simple, the rich ignore the poor, men of integrity are left to stand alone, and true talent goes unsupported.

sentences.zh: 故田蚡以尊顯致安國之金，[二]淳於以貴埶引方進之言。

translations.literal: Therefore Tian Fen by honored prominence obtained An's gold, [two] Chunyu by noble power drew Fangjin's words.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Tian Fen traded rank for Han Anguo's bribes; Chunyu Chang used influence to win praise from Zhai Fangjin.

sentences.zh: [三]夫以韓、翟之操，為漢之名宰，[四]然猶不能振一貧賢，薦一孤士，又況其下者乎！

translations.literal: [Three] As for with Han's, Zhai's conduct, being Han's famed ministers, [four] yet still could not rouse one poor worthy, recommend one orphaned gentleman—how much more those below them!
translations.idiomatic: [3] Men of Han Anguo's and Zhai Fangjin's caliber served as the Han's most celebrated ministers, [4] yet even they could not lift a single needy scholar or recommend one friendless gentleman—what hope for lesser men!

sentences.zh: 此禽息、史魚所以專名於前，而莫繼於後者也。

translations.literal: This is why Qin Xi and Shi Yu thereby alone had fame in front, yet none continued after.
translations.idiomatic: That is why Qin Xi and Shi Yu stand alone in history, with scarcely anyone to follow their example.

sentences.zh: [五]故時敦俗美，則小人守正，利不能誘也；

translations.literal: [Five] Therefore when the age is honest and custom beautiful, then petty men keep correctness, profit cannot entice them;
translations.idiomatic: [5] When the age is generous and manners sound, even ordinary folk keep to the straight path and profit cannot sway them;

sentences.zh: 時否俗薄，雖君子為邪，義不能止也。
