translations.literal: when the age is bad and custom thin, although gentlemen do evil, righteousness cannot stop them.
translations.idiomatic: when the times turn sour and morals thin, gentlemen themselves turn crooked, and duty cannot hold them back.

sentences.zh: [六]何則？

translations.literal: [Six] What then?
translations.idiomatic: [6] Why?

sentences.zh: 先進者既往而不反，後來者複習俗而追之，是以虛華盛而忠信微，刻薄稠而純篤稀。

translations.literal: Those who advance first have already gone and do not return; those who come later again practice custom and pursue them; therefore empty flower flourishes and loyalty and trustworthiness wane, harsh thinness is dense and pure earnestness is rare.
translations.idiomatic: The first generation passes and does not return; the next absorbs the prevailing habit and follows suit—so hollow display thrives while good faith withers, harshness spreads and simple sincerity grows rare.

sentences.zh: 斯蓋谷風有「棄予」之歎，[七]伐木有「鳥鳴」之悲矣！

translations.literal: This is why the Valley Wind has the sigh of 'discarding me,' [seven] Felling Trees has the grief of 'birds calling'!
translations.idiomatic: [7] Hence the Valley Wind laments the lover who casts his mate away, and Felling Trees mourns the friend who will not heed the calling bird!

sentences.zh: [八] 注[一]悠悠，多也。

translations.literal: [Eight] Commentary [one]: You you means many.
translations.idiomatic: [8] Commentary 1: You you means 'numerous' or 'the multitude.'

sentences.zh: 稱，舉也。

translations.literal: Cheng means raise / praise.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng means to praise or commend.

sentences.zh: 注[二]田蚡，*

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Tian Fen, *
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Tian Fen

translations.literal: (Martial)
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu's

sentences.zh: **[景]*帝王皇后同產弟，為太尉，親貴用事。

translations.literal: **[Jing]* emperor empress same-birth younger brother, was grand commandant, intimate noble wielded affairs.
translations.idiomatic: younger brother of Empress Wang of Emperor Jing by the same mother—served as grand commandant and wielded power as a favorite of the throne.

sentences.zh: 韓安國為梁王太傅，坐法失官，安國以五百金遺蚡，蚡為言太后，即召以為北地都尉也。

translations.literal: Han Anguo was Prince of Liang's grand tutor, sat for law lost office; Anguo with five hundred jin gifted Fen; Fen for him spoke to the empress dowager, then summoned to be northland commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Han Anguo had been tutor to the king of Liang but lost his post for a legal offense; he sent Tian Fen five hundred pounds of gold, Tian Fen spoke to the empress dowager on his behalf, and Han Anguo was recalled as commandant of the northern frontier.

sentences.zh: 注[三]翟方進，成帝時為丞相。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Zhai Fangjin, in Emperor Cheng's time was chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Zhai Fangjin served as chancellor under Emperor Cheng.

sentences.zh: 淳于長，元後姊子，封定陵侯，以能謀議為九卿，用事。

translations.literal: Chunyu Chang, Empress Yuan's elder sister's son, enfeoffed Marquis of Dingling, by ability to plan and discuss became one of the nine ministers, wielded affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Chang, nephew of Empress Yuan, was made marquis of Dingling; his talent for counsel won him one of the nine ministerial posts and real influence at court.

sentences.zh: 方進獨與長交，稱薦之也。

translations.literal: Fangjin alone with Chang associated, praised and recommended him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Fangjin alone cultivated his friendship and sang his praises.

sentences.zh: 注[四]前書曰：「天子以韓安國為國器，拜御史大夫。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: The former Han History says: 'The Son of Heaven took Han Anguo as state vessel, appointed grandee secretary.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: The History of the Former Han records that the emperor deemed Han Anguo a pillar of the state and appointed him grandee secretary.'

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「翟方進智能有餘，天子甚重之。

translations.literal: ' Again it says: 'Zhai Fangjin's intelligence and ability had surplus, the Son of Heaven greatly esteemed him.
translations.idiomatic: It also says of Zhai Fangjin that his abilities exceeded ordinary measure and the emperor held him in the highest regard.'

sentences.zh: 」故言名宰也。

translations.literal: ' Therefore it says famed ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the text speaks of them as eminent chief ministers.'

sentences.zh: 注[五]韓詩外傳曰：「禽息，秦大夫，薦百里奚不見納。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: The Han Poetry Outer Commentary says: 'Qin Xi, Qin grandee, recommended Baili Xi but was not accepted.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: The Outer Commentary to the Han version of the Songs tells how Qin Xi, a Qin minister, urged the employment of Baili Xi without success.'

sentences.zh: 繆公出，當車以頭擊闑，腦乃精出，曰：『臣生無補於國，不如死也。

translations.literal: Duke Mu went out; blocking the chariot with head struck the doorbolt, brains then essence came out, said: 'Your servant in life had no benefit for the state—better to die.
translations.idiomatic: When Duke Mu came forth, Qin Xi dashed his head against the chariot gate until his brains burst, crying, 'Alive I did the state no good—I might as well be dead!'

sentences.zh: 』繆公感寤而用百里奚，秦以大化。

translations.literal: ' Duke Mu was moved to awakening and employed Baili Xi; Qin by this greatly transformed.
translations.idiomatic: The duke was shaken into sense, employed Baili Xi, and Qin flourished.'

sentences.zh: 」禮，大夫殯於正室，士於適室。

translations.literal: ' Rites: grandees lay out in main chamber, gentlemen in appropriate chamber.
translations.idiomatic: By ritual, a grandee's corpse lies in the main chamber and a gentleman's in the side chamber.'

sentences.zh: 韓子曰，史魚，□大夫。

translations.literal: Hanzi says, Shi Yu, [Wei] grandee.
translations.idiomatic: Han Feizi records that Shi Yu was a grandee of Wei.

sentences.zh: 卒，委柩後寢。

translations.literal: Died, entrusted coffin in rear chamber.
translations.idiomatic: When he died he had his coffin placed in the rear chamber.

sentences.zh: □君吊而問之。

translations.literal: The lord of Wei condoled and asked him.
translations.idiomatic: The ruler of Wei came to condole and inquired into the meaning of the arrangement.

sentences.zh: 曰：「不能進蘧伯玉，退彌子瑕。

translations.literal: Said: 'Unable to advance Qu Boyu, retire Mi Zixia.
translations.idiomatic: The inscription recorded his final remonstrance: 'I failed to advance Qu Boyu and to dismiss Mi Zixia.'

sentences.zh: 」以尸諫也。

translations.literal: ' By corpse remonstrated.
translations.idiomatic: Thus he remonstrated from beyond the grave.'

sentences.zh: 注[六]皆牽於時也。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: All are dragged by the age.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 6: In each case they were constrained by the temper of their times.

sentences.zh: 注[七]詩小雅曰：「習習谷風，維風及雨。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: The Classic of Poetry Minor Odes says: 'Rustling valley wind, with wind comes rain.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 7: The Minor Odes sing, 'The valley wind blows softly; wind brings the rain along.'

sentences.zh: 將恐將懼，維予與汝。

translations.literal: About to fear about to dread, only I with you.
translations.idiomatic: In fear and trembling I clung to you alone.'

sentences.zh: 將安將樂，汝轉棄予。」

translations.literal: About to peace about to joy, you turn and discard me.'
translations.idiomatic: When peace and joy returned, you cast me away instead.'

sentences.zh: 注[八]詩小雅曰「伐木丁丁，鳥鳴嚶嚶。

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: The Classic of Poetry Minor Odes says 'Felling trees ding ding, birds cry ying ying.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 8: The Minor Odes read, 'Woodmen's axes ring; birds cry in chorus.'

sentences.zh: 出自幽谷，遷於喬木。

translations.literal: Emerging from dark valley, moving to tall tree.
translations.idiomatic: They leave the deep valley and settle in the tall trees.'

sentences.zh: 嚶其鳴矣，求其友聲」也。

translations.literal: Ying they cry, seeking their friends' voices'—thus.
translations.idiomatic: They call in chorus, seeking the answering cry of friends.'

sentences.zh: 嗟乎！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 世士誠躬師孔聖之崇則，嘉楚嚴之美行，希李老之雅誨，思馬援之所尚，鄙二宰之失度，美韓稜之抗正，[一]貴丙、張之弘裕，賤時俗之誹謗，則道豐績盛，名顯身榮，載不刊之德，[二]播不滅之聲。

translations.literal: If gentlemen of the age truly personally took as teacher Confucius's lofty norms, praised Chu Yan's beautiful conduct, hoped for Laozi's elegant instruction, pondered what Ma Yuan esteemed, despised the two ministers' loss of measure, praised Han Ling's upright resistance, [one] honored Bing and Zhang's magnanimity, despised the age's customs of slander—then the Way would be rich and deeds abundant, name displayed and person honored, bearing uncarvable virtue, [two] sowing undying fame.
translations.idiomatic: If scholars would truly model themselves on Confucius's supreme standard, applaud King Zhuang of Chu's magnanimity, heed Laozi's gentle teaching, remember Ma Yuan's precepts, scorn the excesses of Tian Fen and Zhai Fangjin, honor Han Ling's fearless integrity, [1] esteem the breadth of Bing Ji and Zhang Ziru, and spurn the fashion for backbiting—then the Way would flourish, their achievement would be great, their names glorious and persons honored, they would bear imperishable virtue, [2] and leave a fame that never dies.

sentences.zh: 然*[後]*知薄者之不足，厚者之有餘也。

translations.literal: Only *[then]* knowing the thin's insufficiency, the thick's surplus.
translations.idiomatic: Only then do we see how shallow conduct falls short and generous conduct abounds.

sentences.zh: 彼與草木俱朽，[三]此與金石相傾，[四]豈得同年而語，並日而談哉？

translations.literal: Those with grass and wood together rot, [three] these with metal and stone mutually lean, [four] how can they be spoken of in the same year, discussed on the same day!
translations.idiomatic: The one sort decays like grass and wood; [3] the other endures like metal and stone set against each other in lasting balance; [4] how can the two be named in the same breath!

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]事具韓稜傳也。

translations.literal: ' Commentary [one]: The affair is fully in Han Ling's biography.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: The episode is given in full in the biography of Han Ling.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]刊，削也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Kan means carve / cut away.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Kan means to inscribe by cutting—hence 'indelible.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]彼謂薄也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: That means the thin.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: 'Those' refers to men of shallow conduct.

sentences.zh: 注[四]此謂厚也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: This means the thick.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: 'These' refers to the magnanimous.

sentences.zh: 老子曰：「高下之相傾。」

translations.literal: Laozi says: 'High and low mutually lean.'
translations.idiomatic: Laozi says, 'High and low define each other.'

sentences.zh: 穆又著絕交論，亦矯時之作。

translations.literal: Mu also composed Treatise on Severing Relations—again a work correcting the age.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu also wrote his Treatise on Severing Friendships, another essay aimed at the vices of the day.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]穆集載論，其略曰：「或曰：『子絕存問，不見客，亦不荅也，何故？

translations.literal: [One] Commentary [one]: Mu's collected works carry the treatise; its outline says: Someone said: You cut off inquiries, do not see guests, and answer no one—what reason?'
translations.idiomatic: [1] Zhu Mu's collected works preserve the treatise, which opens with a question: 'You refuse visits, turn away callers, and answer no one—why?'

sentences.zh: 』曰：『古者，進退趨業，無私游之交，相見以公朝，享會以禮紀，否則朋徒受習而已。

translations.literal: The reply: In antiquity men moved between duty and rest without cultivating private friendships; they met in the public hall and feasted only by ritual; otherwise they studied only with fellow students.'
translations.idiomatic: The answer: In antiquity men moved between duty and rest without cultivating private friendships; they met in the public hall and feasted only by ritual; otherwise they simply studied with fellow students.'

sentences.zh: 』曰：『人將疾子，如何？

translations.literal: They asked, 'People will despise you for it—what then?'
translations.idiomatic: They asked, 'People will despise you for it—what then?'

sentences.zh: 』曰：『寧受疾。

translations.literal: He answered, 'I would rather be despised.'
translations.idiomatic: He answered, 'I would rather be despised.'

sentences.zh: 』曰：『受疾可乎？

translations.literal: They pressed, 'Can you accept being hated?'
translations.idiomatic: They pressed, 'Can you accept being hated?'

sentences.zh: 』曰：『世之務交遊也久矣，敦千乘不忌於君，犯禮以追之，背公以從之。

translations.literal: He replied, 'Fashionable networking has gone on too long: men with the weight of princes show no scruple toward their ruler, break ritual to chase connections, and abandon the public good for private ties.'
translations.idiomatic: He replied, 'Fashionable networking has gone on too long: men with the weight of princes show no scruple toward their ruler, break ritual to chase connections, and abandon the public good for private ties.'

sentences.zh: 其愈者，則孺子之愛也；

translations.literal: In the better case, then it is childish affection;
translations.idiomatic: At best it is the fondness of children;

sentences.zh: 其甚者，則求蔽過竊譽，以贍其私。

translations.literal: in the severe case, then seeking to cover faults and steal praise, thereby to enrich their private ends.
translations.idiomatic: at worst it means hiding faults, stealing reputation, and lining private pockets.

sentences.zh: 事替義退，公輕私重，居勞於聽也。

translations.literal: Affairs decline and righteousness retreats, the public light and private heavy, dwelling in toil of listening.
translations.idiomatic: Duty withers and right retreats, the state counts for little and selfish interest for much, and one's ears tire of the clamor.'

sentences.zh: 或於道而求其私，贍矣。

translations.literal: Some on the Way seek their private ends—enough!
translations.idiomatic: Some twist the Way itself to serve private greed—there is no end to it.'

sentences.zh: 是故遂往不反，而莫敢止焉。

translations.literal: Therefore they go forward and do not return, yet none dares stop them.
translations.idiomatic: So the rush goes ever forward and none dare call a halt.'

sentences.zh: 是川瀆並決，而莫之敢塞；

translations.literal: It is rivers and canals together bursting, yet none dares dam them;
translations.idiomatic: It is like rivers in flood that no one dares to dam;

sentences.zh: 游豶蹂稼，而莫之禁也。

translations.literal: wandering boars trample crops, yet none forbids them.
translations.idiomatic: like wild boars rooting through the grain that no one can drive off.'

sentences.zh: 詩云：「威儀棣棣，不可筭也。

translations.literal: The Poetry says: 'Dignified deportment in harmony, cannot be calculated.
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry says, 'Solemn deportment, orderly and serene, cannot be reckoned.'

sentences.zh: 」後生將復何述？

translations.literal: ' Later born—what again will they narrate?
translations.idiomatic: What will the next generation have left to tell?'

sentences.zh: 而吾不才，焉能規此？

translations.literal: Yet I lack talent—how can I regulate this?
translations.idiomatic: I am no genius—how can I reform the world?'

sentences.zh: 實悼無行，子道多闕，臣事多尤，思復白圭，重考古言，以補往過。

translations.literal: Truly I grieve lack of conduct, son's way much lacking, minister's affairs many faults, thinking to recover the white jade tablet, again examine ancient words, thereby to mend past errors.
translations.idiomatic: I mourn my own want of virtue, my failures as a son and subject; I wish to polish the 'white jade' of my conduct, study the words of the ancients again, and make good past mistakes.'

sentences.zh: 時無孔堂，思兼則滯，匪有廢也，則亦焉興？

translations.literal: The age has no Confucius hall; thinking of both then is blocked—if not having abandonment, then how arise?
translations.idiomatic: We have no Confucian academy today; to pursue every tie is to be overwhelmed. Unless something is given up, how can anything worthwhile rise?'

sentences.zh: 是以敢受疾也，不亦可乎！

translations.literal: Therefore I dare receive hatred—is it not also acceptable!
translations.idiomatic: So I choose to bear men's hatred—why should that not serve?'

sentences.zh: 』」文士傳曰：「世無絕交。

translations.literal: ' ' The Literary Men's Tradition says: 'The age has no severing of relations.
translations.idiomatic: The Biographies of Literary Men remarks, 'No one in our time really breaks off friendships.'

sentences.zh: 」又與劉伯宗絕交書及詩曰：「昔我為豐令，足下不遭母憂乎？

translations.literal: ' Again to Liu Bozong a letter severing relations and poem says: 'When I was magistrate of Feng, were you not in mourning for your mother?'
translations.idiomatic: He also addressed Liu Bozong in a letter of rupture and a poem: 'When I was magistrate of Feng, had you not just lost your mother?'

sentences.zh: 親解縗絰，來入豐寺。

translations.literal: Personally removing mourning bands, came into Feng monastery.
translations.idiomatic: You took off your mourning garments and came to the county office in person.'

sentences.zh: 及我為持書御史，足下親來入台。

translations.literal: When I became secretary censor, you came in person to the censorate.'
translations.idiomatic: When I became secretary censor, you came in person to the censorate.'

sentences.zh: 足下今為二千石，我下為郎，乃反因計吏以謁相與。

translations.literal: You now rank at two thousand piculs while I am only a court gentleman, yet you would pay a call through a county clerk as if we were strangers trading favors.'
translations.idiomatic: Now you rank at two thousand piculs while I am only a gentleman at court, yet you would visit me through a county clerk as if we were strangers trading favors.'

sentences.zh: 足下豈丞尉之徒，我豈足下部*[民]*，欲以此謁為榮寵乎？

translations.literal: Do you rank no higher than an assistant magistrate, and I no higher than your subordinate—do you imagine this visit would honor me?'
translations.idiomatic: Do you take me for some petty clerk, yourself for my superior, and think this call would honor me?'

sentences.zh: 咄！

translations.literal: Tut!
translations.idiomatic: Fie!

sentences.zh: 劉伯宗於仁義道何其薄哉！

translations.literal: Liu Bozong toward the way of benevolence and righteousness how thin!
translations.idiomatic: How thin Liu Bozong's notion of duty and decency has grown!

sentences.zh: 」其詩曰：「北山有鴟，不絜其翼。

translations.literal: ' His poem says: 'On North Mountain is an owl, not clean its wings.
translations.idiomatic: His poem runs: 'On the northern hill an owl sits, wings never clean.'

sentences.zh: 飛不正向，寢不定息。

translations.literal: Flying not straight direction, sleeping not fixed rest.
translations.idiomatic: It flies askew and roosts without peace.'

sentences.zh: 饑則木攬，飽則泥伏。

translations.literal: Hungry then grasps tree, full then mud crouches.
translations.idiomatic: Hungry, it claws the trees; gorged, it wallows in the mire.'

sentences.zh: 饕餮貪污，臭腐是食。

translations.literal: Taotie greedy filth, stench and rot is what it eats.
translations.idiomatic: Gluttonous and foul, it feeds on carrion and rot.'

sentences.zh: 填腸滿嗉，嗜欲無極。

translations.literal: Fills intestines full crop, craving without limit.
translations.idiomatic: It stuffs gut and craw, yet its appetites know no bound.'

sentences.zh: 長鳴呼鳳，謂鳳無德。

translations.literal: Long cry calls phoenix, saying phoenix lacks virtue.
translations.idiomatic: It screeches at the phoenix and calls the phoenix virtueless.'

sentences.zh: 鳳之所趣，與子異域。

translations.literal: What the phoenix hastens toward, with you is different region.
translations.idiomatic: The phoenix aims for a realm you will never know.'

sentences.zh: 永從此訣，各自努力！

translations.literal: Forever from this parting, each self exert!
translations.idiomatic: This is farewell forever—each of us must go his own way!'

sentences.zh: 」蓋因此而著論也。

translations.literal: ' Generally because of this he composed the treatise.
translations.idiomatic: It was this quarrel that prompted his treatise.'

sentences.zh: 梁冀驕暴不悛，朝野嗟毒，穆以故吏，懼其釁積招禍，復奏記諫曰：「古之明君，必有輔德之臣，規諫之官，下至器物，銘書成敗，以防遺失。

translations.literal: Liang Ji arrogant and violent did not reform; court and wilds sighed poison; Mu as former clerk, fearing his rifts accumulating would summon disaster, again submitted a written memorial remonstrating, saying: 'Ancient enlightened lords necessarily had ministers who aided virtue, officials who regulated remonstrance; down to vessels and tools, inscribed writing success and failure, to guard against loss.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji grew ever more arrogant and cruel, and the whole realm groaned under him. Zhu Mu, as his former subordinate, feared that mounting grievances would bring catastrophe, and sent another letter of remonstrance: 'Every enlightened ruler of old had ministers to strengthen his virtue and officers to speak plain truth; even on utensils he recorded success and failure to guard against error.'

sentences.zh: [一]故君有正道，臣有正路，[二]從之如升堂，違之如赴壑。

translations.literal: [One] Therefore the lord has correct way, ministers have correct road, [two] following it like ascending hall, violating it like rushing to ravine.
translations.idiomatic: [1] When the ruler holds to the straight path and ministers to the straight course, [2] to follow it is like climbing the high hall; to abandon it is like hurtling into a chasm.'

sentences.zh: 今明將軍地有申伯之尊，[三]位為腢公之首，[四]一日行善，天下歸仁，[五]終朝為惡，四海傾覆。

translations.literal: Now the enlightened general's territory has the Duke of Shen's eminence, [three] position is head of assembled dukes, [four] one day doing good, the realm returns to benevolence, [five] one morning doing evil, the four seas overturn.
translations.idiomatic: You, General, hold lands as honored as those of the Earl of Shen, [3] and stand first among the great ministers of state. [4] A single day of good deeds would win the empire to humaneness; [5] one morning of evil could overturn the four seas.'

sentences.zh: 頃者，官人俱匱，加以水蟲為害。

translations.literal: Recently, officials and people together were exhausted, added to which water and insects were harm.
translations.idiomatic: Lately officials and commoners alike have been drained, and flood and locusts have added to their misery.'

sentences.zh: [六]京師諸官費用增多，詔書發調或至十倍。

translations.literal: [Six] Capital various offices' expenses increased many; edict-issued levies sometimes reached tenfold.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Expenses at the capital have multiplied, and edicts for conscript labor and supplies sometimes demand ten times the old amounts.'

sentences.zh: 各言官無見財，皆當出民，搒掠割剝，強令充足。

translations.literal: Each saying offices had no visible wealth, all ought to come from the people, flaying stripping cutting peeling, forcibly ordered to be sufficient.
translations.idiomatic: Each office claims empty coffers and says everything must come from the people; they beat and extort until quotas are met.'

sentences.zh: 公賦既重，私斂又深。

translations.literal: Public levies already heavy, private exactions again deep.
translations.idiomatic: State taxes are crushing and private squeeze goes deeper still.'

sentences.zh: 牧守長吏，多非德選，貪聚無猒，遇人如虜，或絕命於棰楚之下，或自賊於迫切之求。

translations.literal: Shepherds, guards, senior clerics, many not virtuously selected, greedy gathering without satiety, treating people like captives; some cut off life under flogging, some self-injured by pressing demands.
translations.idiomatic: Many prefects and magistrates are not men of moral worth; they pile up wealth insatiably and handle the people like prisoners—some die under the rod, others destroy themselves trying to meet impossible demands.'

sentences.zh: [七] 又掠奪百姓，皆托之尊府。

translations.literal: [Seven] Again plundering the hundred surnames, all entrust it to the honored mansion.
translations.idiomatic: [7] Robbery of the common people is everywhere laid at your mansion's door.'

sentences.zh: 遂令將軍結怨天下，吏人酸毒，道路歎嗟。

translations.literal: Thereby causing the general to knot resentment with the realm, clerks and people bitter poison, roads sighing.
translations.idiomatic: You have made the General hated across the land; officials and folk are embittered, and travelers groan on every road.'

sentences.zh: 昔秦政煩苛，百姓土崩，陳勝奮臂一呼，天下鼎沸，[八]而面諛之臣，猶言安耳。

translations.literal: Formerly Qin government was vexing and harsh, the hundred surnames earth-collapsed; Chen Sheng raised arm one shout, the realm cauldron-boiled, [eight] yet face-flattering ministers still said peace.
translations.idiomatic: When Qin's laws grew intolerable, the people crumbled like clods of earth; Chen Sheng raised his arm and the empire boiled over, [8] yet courtiers still looked the emperor in the face and called all well.'

sentences.zh: [九] 諱惡不悛，卒至亡滅。

translations.literal: [Nine] Hiding evil not reforming, finally reached perishing destruction.
translations.idiomatic: [9] Because the court hid its faults and would not change, it was destroyed in the end.'

sentences.zh: 昔永和之末，綱紀少□，頗失人望。

translations.literal: Formerly at Yonghe's end, net cords little [slack], quite lost people's hope.
translations.idiomatic: Late in the Yonghe era discipline grew lax and the court forfeited much of the people's confidence.'

sentences.zh: 四五歲耳，而財空戶散，下有離心。

translations.literal: Only four or five years, yet wealth empty households scattered, below had parting hearts.
translations.idiomatic: Within four or five years the treasury was bare, households scattered, and the provinces nursed rebellion in their hearts.'

sentences.zh: 馬免之徒乘敝而起，荊揚之閒幾成大患。

translations.literal: Ma Mian's sort riding exhaustion rose; between Jing and Yang nearly became great calamity.
translations.idiomatic: Men like Ma Mian seized the moment of weakness; between Jing and Yang the realm nearly faced disaster.'

sentences.zh: [一０]幸賴順烈皇后初政清靜，內外同力，僅乃討定。

translations.literal: [Ten] Fortunately relying on Empress Shunlie at first government pure quiet, inside and outside same strength, barely then was suppressed and settled.
translations.idiomatic: [10] Only because Empress Dowager Shunlie began her regency with calm government and court and countryside worked together was the trouble barely put down.'

sentences.zh: 今百姓戚戚，困於永和，內非仁愛之心可得容忍，外非守國之計所宜久安也。

translations.literal: Now the hundred surnames anxious, trapped worse than Yonghe; inward not benevolent loving heart can obtain tolerance; outward not guarding-the-state's plan is what ought long to be secure.
translations.idiomatic: Today the people are as distressed as in Yonghe times; inwardly no government founded on benevolence can long endure such strain, outwardly no policy for defending the state can rest on endless oppression.'

sentences.zh: 夫將相大臣，均體元首，共輿而馳，同舟而濟，輿傾舟覆，患實共之。

translations.literal: As for generals and ministers great officers, equally body with the head; same chariot and gallop, same boat and cross; chariot tilts boat capsizes—the disaster truly is shared.
translations.idiomatic: Generals and chief ministers share one body with the sovereign: they ride the same carriage and sail the same boat—if it overturns, they drown together.'

sentences.zh: 豈可以去明即昧，履危自安，[一一]主孤時困，而莫之恤乎！

translations.literal: How can one discard brightness and approach darkness, tread danger and self be secure, [eleven] lord alone age hard, yet none pity him!
translations.idiomatic: How can you turn from light to shadow, court danger for private ease, [11] while the ruler stands alone in hard times and no one shows him mercy!

sentences.zh: 宜時易宰守非其人者，減省第宅園池之費，拒絕郡國諸所奉送。

translations.literal: It is fitting timely to change shepherds and guards who are not their persons, reduce and cut mansion garden pool expenses, refuse commanderies' and kingdoms' various offerings.
translations.idiomatic: Replace unworthy prefects and magistrates at once, cut back spending on mansions, gardens, and pools, and refuse the gifts sent from every commandery and kingdom.'

sentences.zh: 內以自明，外解人惑，使挾奸之吏無所依托，司察之臣得盡耳目。

translations.literal: Inward thereby self clarify, outward dissolve people's doubt, causing treacherous clerks to have nowhere to rely, investigating ministers to exhaust ears and eyes.
translations.idiomatic: Thus you clear your own name within and lift suspicion abroad, give corrupt clerks no cover, and let censors and inspectors use their eyes and ears to the full.'

sentences.zh: 憲度既張，遠邇清壹，則將軍身尊事顯，德耀無窮。

translations.literal: When statutes are already stretched, far and near pure one, then the general's person honored affairs manifest, virtue shining without limit.
translations.idiomatic: Once the law stands firm and near and far are brought into good order, you will stand honored, your deeds illustrious, and your virtue will shine without end.'

sentences.zh: 天道明察，無言不信，惟垂省覽。

translations.literal: Heaven's way is bright inspection; without words not trusted—only deign to examine.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven sees all and never fails to answer; I beg you to weigh these words carefully.'

sentences.zh: 」冀不納，而縱放日滋，遂復賂遺左右，交通宦者，任其子弟﹑賓客以為州郡要職。

translations.literal: ' Ji did not accept, yet license and release daily increased; then again bribing and gifting left and right, communicating with eunuchs, appointing his sons younger brothers, retainers, guests as commandery and prefecture key posts.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji ignored the advice and grew more wanton by the day; he bribed the emperor's attendants, colluded with the eunuchs, and placed his kinsmen, clients, and guests in key posts across the provinces.'

sentences.zh: 穆又奏記極諫，冀終不悟。

translations.literal: Mu again submitted a memorial remonstrating to the utmost; Ji in the end did not awaken.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu sent further letters of fierce remonstrance, but Liang Ji never took the warning to heart.

sentences.zh: 報書云：「如此，僕亦無一可邪？

translations.literal: Reply letter said: 'If so, have I also not one thing right?'
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji's reply read, 'By your reckoning, am I good for nothing at all?'

sentences.zh: 」穆言雖切，然亦不甚罪也。

translations.literal: ' Though Mu's words were cutting, yet also he was not much punished.
translations.idiomatic: Though Zhu Mu's language was blunt, he was not seriously punished.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]黃帝作巾機之法，孔甲有盤盂之誡。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Yellow Lord made napkin-loom's method; Kong Jia had basin-bowl's warning.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: The Yellow Emperor devised warnings on the napkin-loom; Kong Jia inscribed admonitions on basins and bowls.

sentences.zh: 太公陰謀曰，武王衣之銘曰：「桑蠶苦，女工難，得新捐故後必寒。

translations.literal: Taigong's Secret Stratagem says, King Wu's garment inscription said: 'Mulberry silkworm bitter, woman's work hard; obtaining new discarding old afterward must be cold.
translations.idiomatic: The Taigong yinmou records an inscription on King Wu's robe: 'Sericulture is bitter and women's work is hard; he who throws away the old for the new will soon feel cold.'

sentences.zh: 」鏡銘曰：「以鏡自照者見形容，以人自照者見吉凶。

translations.literal: ' Mirror inscription said: 'By mirror self-illumining sees form and appearance; by others self-illumining sees good and ill fortune.
translations.idiomatic: A mirror inscription says, 'The mirror shows your face; other men show your fortune.'

sentences.zh: 」觴銘曰「樂極則悲，沉湎致非，社稷為危」也。

translations.literal: ' Cup inscription said 'Joy extreme then grief, drowning in drink brings error, altars of soil and grain become peril.'
translations.idiomatic: A goblet inscription warns, 'Extreme joy turns to grief; deep drinking leads to wrong; the altars of state are put at risk.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]說苑君道篇曰：「人君之道，清淨無為，務在博愛，趨在任賢，廣開耳目，以察萬方，不固溺於流俗，不拘繫於左右。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Shuoyuan lord's way chapter says: 'The lord of men's way: pure quiet non-action, task in broad love, hurry in appointing worthies, broadly open ears and eyes, thereby to inspect the myriad directions, not stubbornly drowning in vulgar custom, not bound and tied by left and right.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: The Garden of Persuasions, 'Way of the Ruler' chapter, says the sovereign should keep still and selfless, devote himself to universal love, hasten to employ the worthy, open his eyes and ears to the empire, not be drowned in vulgar fashion, nor tethered to his favorites.'

sentences.zh: 」臣術篇曰「人臣之術，順從覆命，無所敢專，義不苟合，位不苟尊，必有益於國，必有補於君」也。

translations.literal: ' Minister's arts chapter says 'The minister of men's arts: compliant following covering orders, nothing dare monopolize; in righteousness not careless joining, in position not careless honoring—must have benefit for the state, must have supplement for the lord.'
translations.idiomatic: The 'Arts of the Minister' chapter says ministers must obey orders without arrogating power, must not cling to office unworthily, and must bring real benefit to the state and their ruler.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]申國之伯，周宣王之元舅。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The Earl of Shen state, King Xuan of Zhou's eldest maternal uncle.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: The Earl of Shen was the senior maternal uncle of King Xuan of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 注[四]冀絕席於三公。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Ji separated mat from the three dukes.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: Liang Ji was ranked apart from the three dukes—above them in precedence.

sentences.zh: 注[五]論語曰：「一日克己復禮，天下歸仁焉。」

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: The Analects says: 'One day restraining self and returning to ritual, the realm returns to benevolence therein.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: The Analects says, 'Master yourself for one day and return to ritual, and the world will rally to humaneness.'

sentences.zh: 注[六]水災及蝗蟲也。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Water disaster and locusts.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 6: That is, floods and locusts.

sentences.zh: 注[七]賊，殺也。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: Zei means kill.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 7: Zei here means to kill oneself.

sentences.zh: 注[八]前書淮南王謂伍被曰「陳勝，吳廣起於大澤，奮臂大呼，天下響應」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: The former Han History: Prince of Huainan said to Wu Bei 'Chen Sheng, Wu Guang rose from great marsh, raised arm great shout, the realm echoed response.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 8: The History of the Former Han records the prince of Huainan telling Wu Bei that Chen Sheng and Wu Guang rose from the swamps, waved their arms, and the empire answered like an echo.'

sentences.zh: 注[九]秦胡亥時，山東兵大起，叔孫通謂胡亥曰：「鼠竊狗盜，郡縣逐捕之，不足憂。

translations.literal: Commentary [nine]: In Qin Huhai's time, east-of-mountains troops greatly rose; Shusun Tong said to Huhai: 'Rat-stealing dog-thieving—commanderies and prefectures chase and capture them—not worth worry.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 9: When rebellion flared east of the mountains under the Second Emperor of Qin, Shusun Tong told him the trouble was mere 'rat theft and dog robbery' that local officials could handle—nothing to fear.'

sentences.zh: 」諸生曰：「何先生言之諛也！」

translations.literal: ' The scholars said: 'How Master speaks flatteringly!'
translations.idiomatic: The scholars cried, 'How servile your words are, sir!'

sentences.zh: 注[一０]質帝時，九江賊馬免稱「黃帝」，歷陽賊華孟稱「黑帝」，並九江都尉滕撫討斬之。

translations.literal: Commentary [ten]: In Emperor Zhi's time, Jiujiang bandit Ma Mian titled himself 'Yellow Emperor,' Liyang bandit Hua Meng titled himself 'Black Emperor'—together Jiujiang commandant Teng Fu attacked and beheaded them.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 10: Under Emperor Zhi, the Jiujiang rebel Ma Mian called himself Yellow Emperor and the Liyang rebel Hua Meng called himself Black Emperor; both were defeated and beheaded by the Jiujiang commandant Teng Fu.

sentences.zh: 九江、歷陽是荊揚之閒也。

translations.literal: Jiujiang, Liyang are between Jing and Yang.
translations.idiomatic: Jiujiang and Liyang lie in the region between Jing and Yang.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]即，就也。

translations.literal: Commentary [eleven]: Ji means approach / go to.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 11: Ji means to choose or embrace.

sentences.zh: 永興元年，河溢，漂害人庶數十萬戶，百姓荒饉，流移道路。

translations.literal: In Yongxing year one, the river overflowed, drifting and harming people in several hundred thousand households; the hundred surnames famine, flowed and moved on roads.
translations.idiomatic: In the first year of Yongxing the Yellow River burst its banks and drowned hundreds of thousands of families; famine drove multitudes onto the roads.

sentences.zh: 冀州盜賊尤多，故擢穆為冀州刺史。

translations.literal: Jiuzhou bandits especially many; therefore promoted Mu to be Jiuzhou inspector.
translations.idiomatic: Banditry was worst in Ji Province, so Zhu Mu was promoted to inspector of Ji.

sentences.zh: 州人有宦者三人為中常侍，並以檄謁穆。

translations.literal: Men of the province who were eunuchs, three being palace attendants, together with dispatch visited Mu.
translations.idiomatic: Three eunuchs from the province who held rank as regular palace attendants sent calling cards to announce a visit.

sentences.zh: 穆疾之，辭不相見。

translations.literal: Mu hated it, declined not to meet.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu detested the impropriety and refused to receive them.

sentences.zh: 冀部令長聞穆濟河，解印綬去者四十餘人。

translations.literal: Ji department magistrates and chiefs hearing Mu crossed the river, unsealed seals and cords and departed were over forty.
translations.idiomatic: When word spread that Zhu Mu had crossed the river into Ji, more than forty county magistrates resigned their seals and fled.

sentences.zh: 及到，奏劾諸郡，至有自殺者。

translations.literal: When he arrived, memorial-impeached various commanderies, reaching some who killed themselves.
translations.idiomatic: After his arrival he impeached whole commanderies; some officials took their own lives.

sentences.zh: 以威略權宜，盡誅賊渠帥。

translations.literal: By awe strategy expedient, completely executed bandit chieftains.
translations.idiomatic: By a mix of authority and flexible tactics he exterminated the bandit chiefs.

sentences.zh: 舉劾權貴，或乃死獄中。

translations.literal: Raised impeachment against powerful nobles; some then died in prison.
translations.idiomatic: He brought charges against powerful men at court; some died in custody.

sentences.zh: 有宦者趙忠喪父，歸葬安平，[一]僭為璵璠、玉匣、偶人。

translations.literal: There was eunuch Zhao Zhong mourning father, returning to bury in Anping, [one] usurpingly made yufan, jade box, wooden figures.
translations.idiomatic: When the eunuch Zhao Zhong went home to Anping to bury his father, [1] he used forbidden grave goods—ornamental jade, a jade burial suit, and clay substitutes for the living.'

sentences.zh: [二]穆聞之，下郡案驗。

translations.literal: [Two] When Mu heard of it, he sent orders down to the commandery to investigate.
translations.idiomatic: [2] When Zhu Mu learned of it, he ordered the commandery to open a formal inquiry.

sentences.zh: 吏畏其嚴明，遂發墓剖棺，陳屍出之，而收其家屬。

translations.literal: The clerks, fearing his severity, then opened the tomb, split the coffin, displayed the corpse, and seized his family members.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing his rigor, the officials exhumed the grave, broke open the coffin, exposed the corpse, and took his kin into custody.

sentences.zh: 帝聞大怒，征穆詣廷尉，[三]輸作左校。

translations.literal: The emperor heard and was greatly angry; he summoned Mu to the commandant of justice, [three] and sentenced him to hard labor at the Left Work Camp.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor, enraged, had Zhu Mu summoned before the commandant of justice [3] and condemned to corvée labor at the Left Work Camp.

sentences.zh: [四] 太學書生劉陶等數千人詣闕上書訟穆曰：「伏見施刑徒朱穆，處公憂國，拜州之日，志清奸惡。

translations.literal: [Four] Several thousand Imperial Academy students including Liu Tao went to the palace gate and submitted a memorial defending Mu, saying: 'We have seen the punished convict Zhu Mu, who stood for the public good and grieved for the state; the day he took up his provincial post he resolved to purge wickedness.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Several thousand academy students, Liu Tao among them, thronged the palace gate with a petition for Zhu Mu: 'We see Zhu Mu, now made a convict, as a man who served the common weal and mourned for the realm; from the day he entered office he meant to cleanse away evil.'

sentences.zh: 誠以常侍貴寵，父兄子弟布在州郡，競為虎狼，噬食小人，故穆張理天網，補綴漏目，羅取殘禍，以塞天意。

translations.literal: Truly because the regular attendants are honored and favored, fathers, elder brothers, sons, and younger brothers are spread through commanderies and prefectures, competing to be tigers and wolves devouring the little people—therefore Mu spread heaven's net, patched its leaking meshes, and snared lingering disaster to answer heaven's intent.
translations.idiomatic: The regular attendants are powerful; their kin fill every province and prey like wolves on the common folk. Zhu Mu only tightened heaven's net, mended its gaps, and caught lingering scourges to satisfy what heaven demands.'

sentences.zh: 由是內官鹹共恚疾，謗讟煩興，讒隙仍作，極其刑□，輸作左校。

translations.literal: Thereby inner officials together hated him; slander arose thick and fast, slanderous rifts repeatedly formed; they pressed punishment to the utmost [and] sent him to labor at the Left Work Camp.
translations.idiomatic: The eunuchs therefore nursed a common grudge; calumny multiplied and intrigue never ceased until he was broken with the harshest penalties and sent to the Left Work Camp.'

sentences.zh: 天下有識，皆以穆同勤禹、稷而被共、鯀之戾，若死者有知，則唐帝怒於崇山，重華忿於蒼墓矣。

translations.literal: All who have understanding in the realm deem that Mu, like the diligent Yu and Ji, yet suffered Gun and Gong's injustice; if the dead have knowledge, then the Tang emperor would rage at Mount Chong and Chonghua would resent at the green grave.
translations.idiomatic: Thoughtful men everywhere compare Zhu Mu to the toil of Yu and Hou Ji yet punished like Gun or Huandou; had the dead knowledge, Yao would rage at Mount Chong where Huandou was banished, and Shun would chafe at his own tomb on Cangwu.'

sentences.zh: [五]當今中官近習，[六]竊持國柄，[七]手握王爵，口含天憲，運賞則使餓隸富於季孫，[八]呼腔則令伊、顏化為桀、跖。

translations.literal: [Five] Today's palace attendants and intimates, [six] secretly grasp the state's handle, [seven] hold noble ranks in their hands and carry the imperial edict in their mouths; when they dispense rewards they make starving bondservants richer than Jisun, [eight] when they breathe orders they turn Yi Yin and Yan Hui into Jie and Robber Zhi.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Today's eunuchs and favorites [6] usurp the levers of power, [7] dispense noble rank with a gesture and pronounce law with a word; [8] their favors can enrich a starving slave beyond the house of Jisun, their breath can turn men like Yi Yin and Yan Hui into tyrants like Jie or thieves like Zhi.'

sentences.zh: [九]而穆獨亢然不顧身害。

translations.literal: [Nine] Yet Mu alone stood high and did not consider harm to his person.
translations.idiomatic: [9] Yet Zhu Mu alone stood upright and ignored the danger to himself.'

sentences.zh: 非惡榮而好辱，惡生而好死也，徒感王綱之不攝，[一０] 懼天網之久失，故竭心懷憂，為上深計。

translations.literal: It is not that he hated honor and loved shame, hated life and loved death—only he was moved that the kingly net was not held, [ten] feared heaven's net had long been lost; therefore he exhausted his heart, harbored worry, and planned deeply for his superiors.
translations.idiomatic: He did not court disgrace or death for their own sake; he was stirred because the royal discipline hung slack [10] and feared heaven's justice had long failed—so he gave his whole heart in loyal anxiety and counseled his ruler with care.'

sentences.zh: 臣願黥首系趾，[一一]代穆校作。」

translations.literal: Your servants wish to tattoo foreheads and fetter toes, [eleven] to replace Mu in corvée labor.'
translations.idiomatic: We beg to be branded and shackled [11] in Zhu Mu's place at the work camp.'

sentences.zh: 帝覽其奏，乃赦之。

translations.literal: The emperor read their memorial and then pardoned him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read the petition and granted a pardon.

sentences.zh: 注[一]安平，郡，冀州所部。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Anping is a commandery under Ji Province.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: Anping was a commandery in Ji Province.

sentences.zh: 注[二]玉匣長尺，廣二寸半，衣死者自□以下至足，連以金縷，天子之制也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The jade box is one chi long, two and a half cun wide; it clothes the dead from [chest] down to the foot, linked with gold thread—regulations for the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: The jade suit was a foot long and two and a half inches wide, covering the corpse from chest to foot with gold thread—the privilege of emperors alone.'

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「陽虎將以璵璠斂。

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says: 'Yang Hu was about to use yufan for burial.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Commentary records that Yang Hu meant to bury himself with fine jade.'

sentences.zh: 」杜預注云：「美玉名，君所佩也。

translations.literal: ' Du Yu's commentary says: 'Names of fine jade, what the ruler wears on his belt.
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu glosses them as precious jades proper to a ruler's girdle.'

sentences.zh: 」偶人，明器之屬也。

translations.literal: ' Wooden figures belong to mingqi (tomb goods).'
translations.idiomatic: Funerary figurines are a class of grave goods.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]謝承書曰：「穆臨當就道，冀州從事欲為畫像置聽事上，穆留板書曰：『勿畫吾形，以為重負。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Xie Cheng's book says: 'When Mu was about to take the road, a Ji province clerk wished to paint his portrait and set it in the reception hall; Mu left a note on a board saying: Do not paint my likeness, taking it as a heavy burden.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Xie Cheng relates that as Zhu Mu set out for punishment, a Ji staff officer wanted to hang his portrait in the hall; Zhu Mu left a placard reading, 'Do not paint my face—I would find it an intolerable burden.'

sentences.zh: 忠義之未顯，何形象之足紀也！

translations.literal: Loyalty and righteousness not yet displayed—what is likeness worth recording!
translations.idiomatic: When my loyalty is still unrecognized, what use is a portrait?'

sentences.zh: 』」注[四]左校，署名，屬將作，掌左工徒。

translations.literal: ' ' Commentary [four]: Left Work Camp is an office name, subordinate to the superintendent of works, in charge of left-side laborers.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: The Left Work Camp was a convict workshop under the superintendent of works.'

sentences.zh: 注[五]尚書曰：「放驩兜於崇山。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: The Documents says: 'Banish Huandou to Mount Chong.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: The Book of Documents says Yao banished Huandou to Mount Chong.'

sentences.zh: 」孔安國注曰：「崇山，南裔也。

translations.literal: ' Kong Anguo's commentary says: 'Mount Chong is in the southern borderlands.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Anguo identifies Mount Chong with the southern marches.'

sentences.zh: 」山海經曰：「有讙頭之國，帝堯葬焉。

translations.literal: ' The Classic of Mountains and Seas says: 'There is the land of Huantou; Emperor Yao is buried there.
translations.idiomatic: The Shanhaijing places Yao's tomb in the land of Huantou.'

sentences.zh: 」郭璞注云：「讙頭，驩兜也。

translations.literal: ' Guo Pu's commentary says: 'Huantou is Huandou.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Pu equates Huantou with Huandou.'

sentences.zh: 」禮記曰：「舜葬蒼梧之野。」

translations.literal: ' The Record of Rites says: 'Shun was buried in the wilds of Cangwu.'
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Rites says Shun was buried in the wilds of Cangwu.'

sentences.zh: 注[六]鄭玄注禮記云：「近習，天子所親幸者。」

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Zheng Xuan's commentary on the Record of Rites says: 'Intimates are those the Son of Heaven personally favors.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 6: Zheng Xuan defines intimates as those the emperor favors and keeps near.

sentences.zh: 注[七]周禮以八柄詔王馭腢臣，謂爵、祿、予、置、生、奪、廢、誅也。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: The Rites of Zhou uses eight handles to instruct the king in governing assembled ministers—meaning rank, salary, grant, appoint, spare, seize, dismiss, execute.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 7: The Rites of Zhou lists eight handles—nobility, emolument, gift, placement, sparing, confiscation, dismissal, and execution—by which the king governs his ministers.

sentences.zh: 注[八]運，行也。

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: Yun means apply / carry out.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 8: Yun means to dispense or apply.

sentences.zh: 論語曰：「季氏富於周公。」

translations.literal: The Analects says: 'The Ji clan was richer than the Duke of Zhou.'
translations.idiomatic: The Analects says the house of Ji grew wealthier than the Duke of Zhou.'

sentences.zh: 注[九]呼腔，吐納也。

translations.literal: Commentary [nine]: Hu qiang means exhaling and inhaling / issuing orders.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 9: Hu qiang refers to issuing commands—breath in and out.

sentences.zh: 伊尹、顏回、夏桀、盜跖也。

translations.literal: Yi Yin, Yan Hui, Xia Jie, Robber Zhi.
translations.idiomatic: That is, Yi Yin and Yan Hui versus Jie and Robber Zhi.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]攝，持也。

translations.literal: Commentary [ten]: She means hold / maintain.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 10: She means to hold firm or maintain.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]黥首謂鑿額涅墨也。

translations.literal: Commentary [eleven]: Tattooing the forehead means chiseling the forehead and staining with ink.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 11: Forehead tattooing means branding the brow with ink.

sentences.zh: 系趾謂釱其足也，以鐵著足曰釱也。

translations.literal: Fettering toes means shackling the feet; attaching iron to the feet is called zhi.
translations.idiomatic: Fettering the feet means iron shackles called zhi.

sentences.zh: 穆居家數年，在朝諸公多有相推薦者，於是征拜尚書。

translations.literal: Mu dwelt at home several years; many dukes at court recommended him, whereupon he was summoned and appointed director of the secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu lived in retirement for some years until leading ministers repeatedly recommended him; he was then recalled as a director of the secretariat.

sentences.zh: 穆既深疾宦官，及在台閣，旦夕共事，志欲除之。

translations.literal: Mu already deeply hated eunuchs; when he was in the terrace pavilions, morning and evening sharing duties, his intent was to eliminate them.
translations.idiomatic: He loathed the eunuchs bitterly; once inside the secretariat he worked among them daily and meant to root them out.

sentences.zh: 乃上疏曰：「案漢故事，中常侍參選士人。

translations.literal: He submitted a memorial saying: 'According to Han precedent, regular palace attendants were drawn from among gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: He memorialized: Under Han precedent, regular attendants were chosen from scholars.'

sentences.zh: 建武以後，乃悉用宦者。

translations.literal: After Jianwu, then all used eunuchs.
translations.idiomatic: After Guangwu's restoration every such post went to castrates.'

sentences.zh: 自延平以來，浸益貴盛，假貂璫之飾，處常伯之任，[一]天朝政事，一更其手，灌傾海內，寵貴無極，子弟親戚，並荷榮任，故放濫驕溢，莫能禁御。

translations.literal: From Yanping onward, gradually they grew more honored and powerful, borrowing sable-tassel ornaments, holding the regular attendant's charge, [one] court and state affairs pass through their hands alone; they pour and overturn within the seas, favor and honor without limit; sons, younger brothers, kin all bear glorious posts—therefore license and arrogance overflow, none can restrain them.
translations.idiomatic: Since Yanping they have swelled in power, wearing sable and jade pendants and bearing the old 'regular attendant' titles [1]; the whole administration passes through their hands, they flood the empire with influence, their kin load every office, and no one can check their excess.'

sentences.zh: 凶狡無行之徒，媚以求官，恃埶怙寵之輩，漁食百姓，窮破天下，空竭小人。

translations.literal: Vicious cunning men without conduct fawn to seek office; those who rely on power and presume on favor net and devour the hundred surnames, exhaust and break the realm, empty and drain the little people.
translations.idiomatic: Wicked men fawn their way to office; favorites who lean on power strip the people bare and beggar the realm.'

sentences.zh: 愚臣以為可悉罷省，遵復往初，率由舊章，更選海內清淳之士，明達國體者，以補其處。

translations.literal: Your foolish servant thinks they can all be abolished and cut, returning to former beginnings, following the old statutes, again selecting within the seas pure and honest gentlemen who understand state forms, to fill their places.
translations.idiomatic: I urge that they be abolished wholesale, that we return to the old statutes, and that their places be filled with men of integrity who understand government.'

sentences.zh: 即陛下可為堯舜之君，觿僚皆為稷契之臣，兆庶黎萌蒙被聖化矣。

translations.literal: Then Your Majesty can be a Yao or Shun ruler, all your ministers become ministers like Ji and Xie, and the myriad common folk will receive sagely transformation.
translations.idiomatic: Then you may rank with Yao and Shun, your ministers with Hou Ji and Xie, and the people will feel true royal virtue.'

sentences.zh: 」帝不納。

translations.literal: ' The emperor did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor rejected the proposal.'

sentences.zh: 後穆因進見，口復陳曰：「臣聞漢家舊典，置侍中、中常侍各一人，省尚書事，[二]黃門侍郎一人，傳發書奏，[三]皆用姓族。

translations.literal: Later Mu on entering audience orally again stated: 'Your servant has heard Han's old statutes placed one attendant-in-ordinary and one regular palace attendant each, to inspect secretariat business, [two] one gentleman at the yellow gate to transmit and dispatch documents, [three] all using families of pedigree.
translations.idiomatic: Later, at a personal audience, Zhu Mu repeated from memory: 'The Han code set one attendant-in-ordinary and one regular attendant to oversee the secretariat [2], one yellow-gate gentleman to carry memorials [3]—all drawn from great clans.'

sentences.zh: [四]自和熹太后以女主稱制，不接公卿，乃以閹人為常侍，小黃門通命兩宮。

translations.literal: [Four] From Empress Dowager Hexi, as ruling mistress holding the reins, not receiving dukes and ministers, then used castrated men as regular attendants, lesser yellow gates to communicate between the two palaces.
translations.idiomatic: [4] When Empress Dowager Hexi held power as regent and shut out the high ministers, eunuchs became regular attendants and petty yellow gates carried orders between the two palaces.'

sentences.zh: 自此以來，權傾人主，窮困天下。

translations.literal: From this time, power tilted past the lord of men and beggared the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Since then their power has overshadowed the throne and ruined the empire.'

sentences.zh: 宜皆罷遣，博選耆儒宿德，與參政事。

translations.literal: It is fitting all be dismissed and sent away, broadly select aged Confucians of established virtue, to join in governing affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Dismiss them all and fill their offices with venerable scholars of proven character.'

sentences.zh: 」帝怒，不應。

translations.literal: ' The emperor was angry and did not reply.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor flushed with anger and gave no answer.'

sentences.zh: 穆伏不肯起。

translations.literal: Mu prostrated himself and would not rise.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu remained kneeling and refused to rise.

sentences.zh: 左右傳出，[五]良久乃趨而去。

translations.literal: Left and right transmitted him out, [five] after a long while then he hastened and left.
translations.idiomatic: Attendants had to lead him out [5]; only after a long interval did he withdraw.'

sentences.zh: 自此中官數因事稱詔詆毀之。

translations.literal: From this inner officials repeatedly on pretexts in the emperor's name slandered him.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward the eunuchs seized every chance to traduce him in forged edicts.

sentences.zh: 注[一]璫以金為之，當冠前，附以金蟬也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Dang is made of gold, placed before the cap, with a gold cicada attached.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: The dang was a gold ornament before the cap, with a golden cicada.

sentences.zh: 漢官儀曰：「中常侍，秦官也。

translations.literal: Han Official Regulations says: 'Regular palace attendant is a Qin office.
translations.idiomatic: The Han Official Regulations state that the regular attendant was originally a Qin office.'

sentences.zh: 漢興，或用士人，銀璫左貂。

translations.literal: When Han rose, sometimes gentlemen were used, silver dang, left sable.
translations.idiomatic: Early Han sometimes filled the post with gentlemen, who wore silver pendants and sable on the left.'

sentences.zh: 光武已後，專任宦者，右貂金璫。

translations.literal: After Guangwu, solely eunuchs were employed, right sable, gold dang.
translations.idiomatic: After Guangwu only eunuchs held the office, with sable on the right and gold pendants.'

sentences.zh: 」常伯，侍中。

translations.literal: ' Chang bo means attendant-in-ordinary.'
translations.idiomatic: Chang bo is another name for attendant-in-ordinary.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]省，覽也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Sheng means inspect / review.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Sheng means to review or oversee.

sentences.zh: 注[三]傳，通也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Chuan means pass through.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Chuan means to transmit.

sentences.zh: 注[四]引用士人有族望者。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: They employed gentlemen who had clan renown.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: Appointees came from families of high pedigree.

sentences.zh: 注[五]傳聲令出。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Transmitting voice ordered departure.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: That is, they conveyed orders to escort him out.

sentences.zh: 穆素剛，不得意，居無幾，憤懣發疽。

translations.literal: Mu by nature was rigid, did not get his wish; dwelling not long, resentment and vexation brought forth a boil.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu was naturally inflexible; thwarted in office, he soon fell ill with an abscess born of frustration.

sentences.zh: [一]延熹六年，卒，時年六十四。

translations.literal: [One] In the sixth year of Yanxi he died, age sixty-four.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He died in Yanxi 6 at the age of sixty-four.

sentences.zh: 祿仕數十年，蔬食布衣，家無餘財。

translations.literal: For decades on salary and office, vegetable food and cloth garments, his household had no surplus wealth.
translations.idiomatic: After decades in office he still ate plain food and wore homespun; his house held no surplus.

sentences.zh: 公卿共表穆立節忠清，虔恭機密，守死善道，宜蒙旌寵。

translations.literal: Dukes and ministers jointly memorialized that Mu had established integrity, been loyal and pure, reverently careful in confidential matters, guarded death and good way—he ought to receive standard honors.
translations.idiomatic: The high ministers jointly praised his integrity, loyalty, purity, and faithful service in secret matters, and urged posthumous honors.

sentences.zh: 策詔□述，追贈益州太守。

translations.literal: An edict of praise [described] him, posthumously enfeoffing him as grand administrator of Yizhou.
translations.idiomatic: An edict of praise was issued, and he was posthumously titled grand administrator of Yizhou.

sentences.zh: 所著論、策、奏、教、書、詩、記、嘲，凡二十篇。

translations.literal: What he composed—treatises, policy essays, memorials, instructions, letters, poems, records, satires—twenty pieces in all.
translations.idiomatic: His writings—treatises, policy pieces, memorials, instructions, letters, poems, records, and satires—number twenty pieces.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]疽，癰也。

translations.literal: [Two] Commentary [one]: Ju is a boil / abscess.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Commentary 1: Ju means a suppurating sore.

sentences.zh: 注[二]袁山松書曰：「穆著論甚美，蔡邕嘗至其家自寫之。」

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Yuan Shansong's book says: 'Mu's treatises were very fine; Cai Yong once went to his house and copied them himself.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Yuan Shansong records that Cai Yong once visited Zhu Mu's home to copy his essays by hand.'

sentences.zh: 穆前在冀州，所辟用皆清德長者，多至公卿、州郡。

translations.literal: Earlier when Mu was in Ji Province, those he summoned and employed were all men of pure virtue and senior years; many reached dukes and ministers, commanderies and prefectures.
translations.idiomatic: The men Zhu Mu had recruited in Ji were mostly men of sterling character; many rose to ministerial rank or governed provinces.

sentences.zh: 子野，少有名節，仕至河南尹。

translations.literal: His son Ye, in youth had reputation for integrity; in office reached metropolitan governor of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: His son Zhu Ye enjoyed a youthful reputation for integrity and rose to metropolitan governor of Henan.

sentences.zh: [一]初，穆父卒，穆與諸儒考依古義，謚曰貞宣先生。

translations.literal: [One] In the beginning, when Mu's father died, Mu with various Confucians examined according to ancient meaning and gave the posthumous title Master Zhenxuan.
translations.idiomatic: [1] When Zhu Mu's father died, Zhu Mu and other scholars gave him the private posthumous style Master Zhenxuan.

sentences.zh: [二]及穆卒，蔡邕復與門人共述其體行，謚為文忠先生。

translations.literal: [Two] When Mu died, Cai Yong again with disciples jointly narrated his conduct and gave the posthumous title Master Wenzhong.
translations.idiomatic: [2] When Zhu Mu died, Cai Yong and his students summarized his life and styled him Master Wenzhong.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]野字子遼，見荀爽薦文。

translations.literal: [Three] Commentary [one]: Ye's courtesy name is Ziliao; see Xun Shuang's recommendation text.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Commentary 1: Zhu Ye, courtesy name Ziliao, appears in Xun Shuang's letter of recommendation.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謚法曰：「清白守節曰貞，善聞周達曰宣。」

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Posthumous-title law says: 'Pure and white, guarding integrity is called zhen; good fame reaching everywhere is called xuan.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: The canon of posthumous names defines zhen as keeping pure integrity and xuan as a reputation that spreads far.

sentences.zh: 注[三]袁山松書曰：「蔡邕議曰：『魯季文子，君子以為忠，而謚曰文子。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Yuan Shansong's book says: 'Cai Yong argued: The Lu minister Ji Wenzi, gentlemen deemed loyal, yet was titled posthumously Wenzi.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Yuan Shansong cites Cai Yong: Ji Wenzi of Lu was judged loyal by the gentlemen, yet received the posthumous name Wen.'

sentences.zh: 又傳曰：「忠，文之實也。

translations.literal: Again the tradition says: 'Loyalty is the fruit of culture.
translations.idiomatic: The tradition adds that loyalty is the substance of culture.'

sentences.zh: 」忠以為實，文以彰之。

translations.literal: ' Loyalty taken as fruit, culture used to display it.
translations.idiomatic: Loyalty forms the core; culture makes it shine.'

sentences.zh: 』遂共謚穆。

translations.literal: ' Then together they gave Mu a posthumous title.
translations.idiomatic: On that basis they gave Zhu Mu his posthumous title.'

sentences.zh: 荀爽聞而非之。

translations.literal: Xun Shuang heard and rejected it.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Shuang heard of it and objected.

sentences.zh: 故張璠論曰：『夫謚者，上之所贈，非下之所造，故顏、閔至德，不聞有謚。

translations.literal: Therefore Zhang Fan's treatise says: 'As for posthumous titles, they are what superiors bestow, not what inferiors create—therefore Yan and Min, utmost virtue, are not heard to have posthumous titles.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fan therefore argues that posthumous names are gifts from the throne, not labels private scholars may invent; even Yan Hui and Min Ziqian, paragons of virtue, received none from their peers.'

sentences.zh: 朱、蔡各以衰世臧否不立，故私議之。』」

translations.literal: Zhu and Cai each because in a declining age praise-and-blame was not established, therefore privately discussed them.' '
translations.idiomatic: Zhu and Cai took it on themselves only because public judgment had collapsed in a decadent age.'

sentences.zh: 論曰：朱穆見比周傷義，偏黨毀俗，[一]志抑朋游之私，遂著絕交之論。

translations.literal: The treatise says: Zhu Mu saw faction clique injure righteousness, partisan cliques ruin custom, [one] resolved to repress private wandering of friends, then composed the treatise on severing relations.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's judgment: Zhu Mu saw how cliques injured duty and partisan bands ruined morals [1], and meant to curb the selfishness of fashionable networking—hence his Treatise on Severing Friendships.

sentences.zh: 蔡邕以為穆貞而孤，又作正交而廣其致焉。

translations.literal: Cai Yong thought Mu was upright yet isolated, and again composed Correct Friendship to broaden its purport.
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong thought him upright but lonely, and wrote On Correct Friendship to broaden the argument.

sentences.zh: [二]蓋孔子稱「上交不諂，下交不黷」，[三]又曰「晏平仲善與人交」，子夏之門人亦問交於子張。

translations.literal: [Two] Confucius praised 'In dealing with superiors not to flatter, with inferiors not to be familiar,' [three] also said 'Yan Pingzhong was good at dealing with people'; Zixia's disciples also asked Zizhang about friendship.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Confucius praised those who neither flatter their betters nor presume on their inferiors [3], praised Yan Ying's skill in friendship, and heard Zixia's disciples ask Zizhang how to choose friends.'

sentences.zh: [四]故易明「斷金」之義，[五]詩載「燕朋」之謠。

translations.literal: [Four] Therefore the Changes clarifies the meaning of 'severing metal,' [five] the Poetry records the song of 'swallow friends.'
translations.idiomatic: [4] Hence the Changes celebrates friendship strong as metal cut in two [5], while the Songs warn against flocks of fair-weather friends.'

sentences.zh: [六]若夫文會輔仁，直諒多聞之友，時濟其益，[七]紵衣傾蓋，彈冠結綬之夫，遂隆其好，[八]斯固交者之方焉。

translations.literal: [Six] As for literary gatherings aiding humaneness, friends who are straight, trustworthy, and broadly informed—at times they aid one's benefit; [seven] ramie-cloth meeting, tilting-canopy, cap-dusting, cord-tying fellows—thereby they exalt their liking, [eight] this is indeed the method of those who associate.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Literary societies that nurture virtue, friends who are frank, faithful, and learned, can truly help a man [7]; chance meetings on the road, old classmates who dust off caps and share office—these deepen affection [8] and show the right way to friendship.'

sentences.zh: [九]至乃田、竇、□、霍之遊客，[一０]廉頗、翟公之門賓，[一一]進由埶合，退因衰異。

translations.literal: [Nine] As for Tian's, Dou's, [Wei]'s, Huo's retainers, [ten] Lian Po and Zhai Gong's gate guests, [eleven] advance by power's conjunction, retreat because of decline and change.
translations.idiomatic: [9] But the clients of Tian Fen, Dou Ying, Wei Qing, and Huo Guang [10], the hangers-on at Lian Po's and Zhai Gong's gates [11], came when power beckoned and fled when fortune failed.'

sentences.zh: 又專諸、荊卿之感激，[一二]侯生、豫子之投身，[一三]情為恩使，命緣義輕。

translations.literal: Again Zhuan Zhu and Jing Ke's grateful excitement, [twelve] Lord Hou and Yuzi's casting away the body, [thirteen] feeling driven by grace, life weighed light because of duty.
translations.idiomatic: So too the assassins Zhuan Zhu and Jing Ke, moved by favor [12], or Lord Hou and Yurang, who gave their lives [13], acted because emotion answered kindness and they held life cheap beside duty.'

sentences.zh: 皆以利害移心，懷德成節，非夫交照之本，未可語失得之原也。

translations.literal: All shifted heart by gain and harm, harbored virtue to complete integrity—not the root of mutual illumination in friendship; one cannot speak with them of the source of loss and gain in human relations.
translations.idiomatic: Such ties turn on profit and loss, not on the mutual enlightenment that is friendship's root; they do not touch the true source of gain and loss in human relations.'

sentences.zh: 穆徒以友分少全，因絕同志之求；

translations.literal: Mu merely because friendship's lot rarely stays whole, therefore cut off like-minded men's seeking;
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu, because few friendships endure intact, cut himself off from like-minded men;

sentences.zh: 黨俠生敝，而忘得朋之義。

translations.literal: clique knightliness produced harm, and forgot the meaning of obtaining friends.
translations.idiomatic: he saw faction and bravado breed abuse and forgot how true friends complete one another.'

sentences.zh: [一四]蔡氏貞孤之言，其為然也！

translations.literal: [Fourteen] Cai's words on upright isolation—how they are so!
translations.idiomatic: [14] Cai Yong was right to call him upright yet isolated.'

sentences.zh: 古之善交者詳矣。

translations.literal: The ancients' good at friendship are detailed indeed.
translations.idiomatic: The ancients understood friendship in all its complexity.

sentences.zh: 漢興稱王陽、貢禹、陳遵、張竦，[一五]中世有廉范、慶鴻、陳重、賴義雲。

translations.literal: At Han's rise they praised Wang Yang, Gong Yu, Chen Zun, Zhang Song; [fifteen] in the middle age there were Lian Fan, Qing Hong, Chen Zhong, Lai Yiyun.
translations.idiomatic: Early Han celebrated Wang Yang, Gong Yu, Chen Zun, and Zhang Song [15]; mid-Han produced Lian Fan, Qing Hong, Chen Zhong, and Lai Yun.

sentences.zh: 注[一]左傳曰：「頑嚚不友，是與比周。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Zuo Tradition says: 'Stubborn and vicious, not friendly—this is joining in faction.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: The Zuo Commentary condemns the stubborn and unruly who have no true friends but only cliques.'

sentences.zh: 」杜預注云：「比，近也。

translations.literal: ' Du Yu's commentary says: 'Bi means close.
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu glosses bi as 'close association.'

sentences.zh: 周，密也。」

translations.literal: Zhou means secret.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhou means secret collusion.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]邕論略曰：「聞之前訓曰：『君子以朋友講習，而正人無有淫朋。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Yong's treatise outline says: 'I have heard former instruction: The gentleman uses friends for lecture and practice, and the upright man has no excessive clique.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Cai Yong's essay cites the teaching that the gentleman learns with his friends while the upright man keeps no corrupting clique.'

sentences.zh: 』是以古之交者，其義敦以正，其誓信以固。

translations.literal: ' Therefore ancient friendship's meaning was honest and correct, its oaths trustworthy and firm.
translations.idiomatic: Hence ancient friendship rested on integrity and trust.'

sentences.zh: 逮至周德始衰，頌聲既寢，伐木有『鳥鳴』之刺，谷風有『棄予』之怨，其所由來，政之缺也。

translations.literal: When Zhou's virtue began to wane and hymn-sound had fallen silent, Felling Trees had the satire of 'birds calling,' Valley Wind had the resentment of 'discarding me'—whence they arose was the lack in government.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhou power waned and praise fell silent, the poets used Felling Trees and Valley Wind to lament broken friendship—fault lay with bad government.'

sentences.zh: 自此已降，彌以陵□，或闕其始終，或強其比周。

translations.literal: From this downward, increasingly they encroach [and decline], some omitting their beginning and end, some forcing faction cliques.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward the record grew ever more careless—some accounts omit beginnings and endings, others force partisan readings onto the facts.

sentences.zh: 是以搢紳患其然，而論者諄諄如也。

translations.literal: Therefore gentry girdled with tablets worried at this being so, while discussers were earnest and tireless.
translations.idiomatic: Officials therefore fretted over the trend, and essayists never tired of debating it.

sentences.zh: 疾淺薄而攜貳者有之，惡朋黨而絕交遊者有之。

translations.literal: There were those who hated shallowness and grew divided; there were those who hated cliques and cut off socializing.
translations.idiomatic: Some attacked superficiality and withdrew in distrust; others condemned faction and renounced society altogether.

sentences.zh: 其論交也，曰富貴則人爭趣之，貧賤則人爭去之。

translations.literal: In their discussing friendship they said: in wealth and honor then people compete to hasten to them; in poverty and low status then people compete to leave them.
translations.idiomatic: They argued that the world flocks to the rich and powerful and scatters from the poor and obscure.

sentences.zh: 是以君子慎人所以交己，審己所以交人，富貴則無暴集之客，貧賤則無棄舊之賓矣。

translations.literal: Therefore the gentleman is careful how others use friendship toward himself, examines how he himself uses friendship toward others—in wealth and honor then there are no guests who violently gather; in poverty and low status then there are no guests who abandon the old.
translations.idiomatic: The gentleman therefore watches how others approach him and how he approaches others: in prosperity he admits no sudden swarm of flatterers, in adversity he loses no true friends who knew him before.

sentences.zh: 故原其所以來，則知其所以去；

translations.literal: Therefore trace why they come, then know why they go;
translations.idiomatic: Trace why men come to you and you will see why they leave.

sentences.zh: 見其所以始，則鶯其所以終。

translations.literal: Seeing why they begin, then [know] why they end.
translations.idiomatic: See how a friendship begins and you can predict how it will end.

sentences.zh: 彼貞士者，貧賤不待夫富貴，富貴不驕乎貧賤，故可貴也。

translations.literal: As for those upright gentlemen, in poverty they do not wait on wealth and honor; in wealth and honor they are not arrogant toward poverty and low status—therefore they are praiseworthy.
translations.idiomatic: The truly steadfast do not fawn when they are poor nor despise others when they rise; that is why they command respect.

sentences.zh: 蓋朋友之道，有義則合，無義則離。

translations.literal: As for the way of friends, where there is righteousness they join, where there is no righteousness they part.
translations.idiomatic: Friendship rests on duty: share a moral purpose and you unite; lose it and you part.

sentences.zh: 善則久要不忘平生之言，惡則忠告善誨之，否則止，無自辱焉。

translations.literal: If good, then long covenant not forgetting words of a lifetime; if evil, then loyal warning and good instruction—otherwise stop, do not yourself be shamed.
translations.idiomatic: With the worthy, keep lifelong promises; with the straying, offer frank counsel—if they will not hear you, withdraw rather than demean yourself.

sentences.zh: 故君子不為可棄之行，不患人之遺己也。

translations.literal: Therefore the gentleman does not do conduct that may be cast off, and does not worry that others cast him off.
translations.idiomatic: The gentleman avoids conduct that invites desertion, and does not fret when others turn away.

sentences.zh: 信有可歸之德，不病人之遠己也。

translations.literal: Truly having virtue to which others may return, he does not fret that people keep distant from himself.
translations.idiomatic: If your virtue truly draws men, you need not fear their distance.

sentences.zh: 不幸或然，則躬自厚而薄責於人，怨其遠矣；

translations.literal: If unhappily it happens so, then himself thick and thinly blaming others—resentment is far;
translations.idiomatic: Should it happen nonetheless, blame yourself generously and others sparingly—then bitterness stays far off;

sentences.zh: 求諸己而不求諸人，咎其稀矣。

translations.literal: seek in oneself and not seek in others—fault is rare.
translations.idiomatic: look inward, not outward, and reproach will be rare.

sentences.zh: 夫遠怨稀咎之機，鹹在乎躬，莫之能改也。

translations.literal: As for the pivot of keeping resentment far and fault rare, all lies in the person—none can alter it for him.
translations.idiomatic: The secret of escaping blame and rancor lies in oneself alone; no one else can shift it.

sentences.zh: 子夏之門人問交於子張，而二子各有聞乎夫子，然則以交誨也。

translations.literal: Zixia's disciples asked Zizhang about friendship, and the two sons each had heard from the Master—thus they taught friendship.
translations.idiomatic: Zixia's pupils asked Zizhang about friendship; each had heard Confucius differently, and each taught friendship from his own lesson.

sentences.zh: 商也寬，故告之以距人，師也褊，故訓之以容觿，各從其行而矯之。

translations.literal: Shang was broad, therefore was told to keep distance from people; Shi was narrow, therefore was instructed in tolerant breadth—each according to his conduct was corrected.
translations.idiomatic: Zixia was indulgent, so the Master warned him to keep others at arm's length; Zizhang was rigid, so he was taught breadth—each was corrected to suit his temperament.

sentences.zh: 至於仲尼之正教，則泛愛觿而親仁，故非善不喜，非仁不親，交遊以方，會友以文，可無貶也。

translations.literal: As for Zhongni's correct teaching, then broadly loving all and drawing near to humaneness—therefore not good then not delighted, not humane then not intimate; socializing by measure, meeting friends by culture—may go without blame.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius taught universal love grounded in humaneness: delight only in the good, draw close only to the humane, keep company within bounds, and gather friends through learning—then there is nothing to censure.

sentences.zh: 谷梁子亦曰：『心志既通，名譽不聞，友之罪也。

translations.literal: The Guliangzi also says: 'When heart and will are already linked, if fame is not heard, it is the friend's fault.
translations.idiomatic: The Guliangzi adds: 'When hearts already understand each other, failure to win a good name is the friend's fault.'

sentences.zh: 』今將患其流而塞其源，病其末而刈其本，無乃未若擇其正而黜其邪，與其彼農皆黍而獨稷焉。

translations.literal: ' Now if we will worry at the flow and dam the source, ail at the branch and mow the root—is it not yet as good as choosing the straight and banishing the crooked, rather than making all that farmer's millet alone glutinous?
translations.idiomatic: To dam the flood while ignoring the spring, or prune the twigs while ignoring the root, is worse than choosing the right and rejecting the wrong—like sowing nothing but panicled millet when millet and glutinous grain both have their place.'

sentences.zh: 夫黍亦神農之嘉谷，與稷並為粢盛也，使交而可廢，則黍其愆矣。

translations.literal: As for glutinous millet, it too is Shennong's fine grain, together with setaria millet serving as sacrificial grain—if friendship could be abolished, then glutinous millet would be at fault.
translations.idiomatic: Glutinous millet was itself a sacred grain of Shennong, offered beside panicled millet at the altars—if friendship could be abolished, then grain itself would stand condemned.'

sentences.zh: 括二論而言之，則刺薄者博而洽，斷交者貞而孤。

translations.literal: Summing the two treatises and speaking, then those who satirize shallowness are broad and thorough; those who sever friendship are upright yet isolated.
translations.idiomatic: Taken together, the satire on shallowness is comprehensive, while the counsel to break off ties is stern yet lonely.

sentences.zh: 孤有羔羊之節，與其不獲已而矯時也，走將從夫孤焉。」

translations.literal: Isolation has the integrity of the Lamb; rather than being unable not to and correcting the age, I would hasten to follow that isolation.'
translations.idiomatic: Isolation keeps the purity praised in the Lamb ode; if I must choose between trimming my sails to the times and standing alone, I would choose solitude.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]易系辭之言也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Words from the Changes' Appended Remarks.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: A quotation from the Appended Remarks of the Changes.

sentences.zh: 注[四]並見論語。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Both appear in the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: Both passages appear in the Analects.

sentences.zh: 注[五]易系辭曰：「二人同心，其利斷金。」

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: The Changes' Appended Remarks says: 'Two men of one mind, their sharpness cuts metal.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: The Appended Remarks reads, 'Two men of one mind can cut metal with their sharpness.'

sentences.zh: 注[六]詩小雅伐木序云：「燕朋友故舊也。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: The preface to Felling Trees in the Minor Odes says: 'Feasting friends and old acquaintances.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 6: The preface to Felling Trees says the poem is about feasting friends and old companions.'

sentences.zh: 」其詩曰：「伐木滸滸，釃酒有藇。

translations.literal: ' Its poem says: 'Felling trees along the bank, straining wine so rich.
translations.idiomatic: The text runs, 'Along the bank we fell trees; we strain the fragrant wine.'

sentences.zh: 」釃音所宜反。

translations.literal: ' Shi read suo-yi fan.
translations.idiomatic: Shi is read with the same initial as suo (fanqie spelling).

sentences.zh: 藇音序。

translations.literal: Yu read xu.
translations.idiomatic: Yu is read xu.

sentences.zh: 注[七]論語曰：「君子以文會友，以友輔仁。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: The Analects says: 'The gentleman uses culture to meet friends, uses friends to aid humaneness.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 7: The Analects says the gentleman gathers friends through learning and completes humaneness through friendship.'

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「益者三友，友直，友諒，友多聞，益矣。」

translations.literal: ' Again it says: 'Three friendships profit: friendship with the straight, with the trustworthy, with the broadly informed—these profit.'
translations.idiomatic: It also names the three kinds of friends who bring profit: the upright, the faithful, and the learned.'
