sentences.zh: 注[八]左傳曰，吳季札以縞帶贈子產，子產獻紵衣焉。

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: The Zuo Tradition says: Wu Jizha with a white silk sash gifted Zichan; Zichan presented a ramie robe.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 8: The Zuo Commentary tells how Jizha of Wu gave Zichan a white silk sash and received a ramie robe in return.

sentences.zh: 孔叢子曰：「孔子與程子相遇於塗，傾蓋而語。

translations.literal: The Kongcongzi says: 'Confucius and Master Cheng met on the road, tilted canopies and spoke.
translations.idiomatic: The Kongcongzi records that Confucius met Master Cheng on the road and talked with canopies touching.'

sentences.zh: 」傾蓋謂駐車交蓋也。

translations.literal: ' Tilting canopy means halting chariots with canopies crossing.
translations.idiomatic: Tilting canopies means they reined in their chariots until the carriage hoods met.'

sentences.zh: 前書曰，王陽、貢禹相與為友，朱博與蕭育為友，時稱「蕭朱結綬，王貢彈冠」，言其趣捨同，相薦達。

translations.literal: The former Han History says: Wang Yang and Gong Yu were friends to each other; Zhu Bo and Xiao Yu were friends to each other; the age called it 'Xiao and Zhu tying cords, Wang and Gong dusting caps'—meaning their choices matched and they recommended one another.
translations.idiomatic: The History of the Former Han pairs Wang Yang with Gong Yu and Zhu Bo with Xiao Yu, saying 'Xiao and Zhu tied on office sashes, Wang and Gong dusted their caps'—men who shared the same aims and lifted each other into office.

sentences.zh: 注[九]方，道也。

translations.literal: Commentary [nine]: Fang means way.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 9: Fang means the proper measure or path.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]竇嬰，孝文皇后從兄子，封魏其侯，游士賓客爭歸之。

translations.literal: Commentary [ten]: Dou Ying, Empress Xiaowen's elder cousin's son, enfeoffed Marquis of Weiji, wandering scholars and guests competed to join him.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 10: Dou Ying, nephew of Empress Wen, was made marquis of Weiji; clients flocked to him.

sentences.zh: 武帝時為丞相。

translations.literal: In Emperor Wu's time he was chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: He served as chancellor under Emperor Wu.

sentences.zh: 田蚡，*

translations.literal: Tian Fen, *
translations.idiomatic: Tian Fen,

translations.literal: (Martial)
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu's

sentences.zh: **[景]*帝王皇后同產弟，為太尉。

translations.literal: **[Jing]* emperor empress same-birth younger brother, was grand commandant.
translations.idiomatic: younger brother of Empress Wang of Emperor Jing by the same mother—held the post of grand commandant.

sentences.zh: 蚡以太后故親幸，數言事多效，士吏趨埶利者皆去嬰而歸蚡。

translations.literal: Fen because of the empress dowager was intimate and favored, often spoke on affairs with much effect; gentlemen and clerks who hastened to power's profit all left Ying and joined Fen.
translations.idiomatic: Favored because of the empress dowager, Tian Fen's word carried weight, and opportunists deserted Dou Ying for him.

sentences.zh: □青拜大將軍，青姊子霍去病為驃騎將軍，皆為大司馬。

translations.literal: [Wei] Qing was appointed grand general; Qing's elder sister's son Huo Qubing was general of agile cavalry; both were grand marshal.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing became grand general; his nephew Huo Qubing became general of agile cavalry; both held the title of grand marshal.

sentences.zh: 去病秩祿與大將軍等，自是後青日衰而去病益貴，青故人門下多去事去病，輒得官爵也。

translations.literal: Qubing's rank and salary equaled the grand general's; from this afterward Qing daily declined while Qubing grew more honored; Qing's old friends and gate followers mostly went to serve Qubing and promptly obtained offices and noble ranks.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Qubing's stipend soon matched Wei Qing's; Wei Qing waned as his nephew rose, and old followers of Wei Qing transferred to Huo Qubing's service and won titles overnight.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]史記曰，廉頗趙人，封為信平君，假相國。

translations.literal: Commentary [eleven]: The Records says: Lian Po was a man of Zhao, enfeoffed Lord Xinping, acting chancellor of state.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 11: The Records identifies Lian Po of Zhao as Lord Xinping, acting chancellor.

sentences.zh: 長平之免歸也，故客盡去；

translations.literal: When after Changping he was dismissed and returned, former guests all departed;
translations.idiomatic: After his disgrace at Changping his clients all deserted him;

sentences.zh: 及復用為將，客又至。

translations.literal: when again employed as general, guests again arrived.
translations.idiomatic: when he was restored to command, they returned.

sentences.zh: 廉頗曰：「客退矣。

translations.literal: Lian Po said: 'Guests, withdraw.
translations.idiomatic: Lian Po told them, 'Leave.'

sentences.zh: 」客曰：「吁！

translations.literal: ' Guests said: 'Alas!
translations.idiomatic: They replied, 'Ah!'

sentences.zh: 君何見之晚也？

translations.literal: lord, how late you see this?
translations.idiomatic: How slow you are to understand!'

sentences.zh: 夫以市道交，君有埶我即從君，無埶即去，此其理也，又何怨焉？

translations.literal: As for dealing by market ways—when the lord has power I then follow the lord; when there is no power I then depart—this is its principle; why resent?
translations.idiomatic: Friendship is a marketplace: we follow your star while it shines and leave when it sets—that is only natural; why blame us?'

sentences.zh: 」下邽翟公為廷尉，賓客亦填門；

translations.literal: ' Duke Zhai of Xiagui was commandant of justice; guests also filled his gate;
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Gong of Xiagui, as commandant of justice, had a crowded gate;

sentences.zh: 及廢，門外可設爵羅。

translations.literal: when dismissed, outside the gate one could set a net for sparrows.
translations.idiomatic: when dismissed, sparrows could nest in his doorway.

sentences.zh: 後復為廷尉，賓客欲往，翟公大署其門曰「一死一生，乃知交情。

translations.literal: Later again being commandant of justice, when guests wished to come, Duke Zhai wrote large on his gate: 'One death, one life—then you know friendship's truth.
translations.idiomatic: When he was restored, would-be visitors found this inscription: 'One turn through death and life shows the heart of friendship.'

sentences.zh: 一貧一富，乃知交態。

translations.literal: One poor, one rich—then you know friendship's manner.
translations.idiomatic: One spell of wealth and poverty shows its shifting face.'

sentences.zh: 一貴一賤，交情乃見」也。

translations.literal: One honored, one base—friendship's feeling then appears.'
translations.idiomatic: One rise and fall in rank lays friendship bare.'

sentences.zh: 注[一二]史記曰，專諸，堂邑人。

translations.literal: Commentary [twelve]: The Records says: Zhuan Zhu was a man of Tangyi.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 12: The Records names Zhuan Zhu of Tangyi.

sentences.zh: 吳公子光以嫡嗣未得立，請專諸刺吳王僚。

translations.literal: Prince Guang of Wu because the legitimate heir had not obtained the throne, asked Zhuan Zhu to assassinate King Liao of Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Guang of Wu, blocked from the succession, asked Zhuan Zhu to kill King Liao.

sentences.zh: 諸曰：「王僚可殺也，母老子弱，是其無如我何？

translations.literal: Zhu said: 'King Liao can be killed; mother old, sons weak—what can they do to me?
translations.idiomatic: Zhuan Zhu said, 'King Liao can be struck down; his mother is old and his heir a child—what could they do to us?'

sentences.zh: 」光乃置酒請王僚。

translations.literal: ' Guang then set wine and invited King Liao.
translations.idiomatic: Guang then feasted King Liao.'

sentences.zh: 酒酣，專諸置匕首魚炙之中，以刺王僚，立死。

translations.literal: When wine was merry, Zhuan Zhu placed a dagger inside broiled fish, to stab King Liao—immediately dead.
translations.idiomatic: At the height of the banquet Zhuan Zhu hid a dagger in the roast fish and stabbed King Liao dead on the spot.

sentences.zh: 又曰，荊軻，□人也。

translations.literal: Again it says: Jing Ke was a man of [Wei].
translations.idiomatic: The same text names Jing Ke of Wei (or Yan).

sentences.zh: 燕太子丹質於秦，秦王政遇之不善，丹怨亡歸，與軻交結，乃尊為上卿，故謂之荊卿。

translations.literal: Crown Prince Dan of Yan was hostage in Qin; King Zheng of Qin treated him ill; Dan resentful fled home, associated with Ke, then honored him as senior minister—therefore called Jing Qing.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Dan of Yan, ill used as a hostage by the king of Qin, fled home, befriended Jing Ke, and raised him to senior minister—hence the name Jing Qing.

sentences.zh: 軻入秦，刺始皇不遂而死也。

translations.literal: Ke entered Qin, attempted to assassinate the First Emperor, did not succeed and died.
translations.idiomatic: Jing Ke went to Qin to stab the First Emperor, failed, and died.

sentences.zh: 注[一三]史記曰，侯嬴，魏隱士，為大梁夷門門者，魏公子無忌請為上客。

translations.literal: Commentary [thirteen]: The Records says: Hou Ying, Wei recluse, was gatekeeper of Daliang's Yimen gate; Prince Wuji of Wei invited him as chief guest.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 13: Hou Ying of Wei kept the Yimen gate for Lord Xinling, who took him as chief retainer.

sentences.zh: 秦圍邯鄲，嬴教公子竊兵符北救趙，乃自剄。

translations.literal: When Qin besieged Handan, Ying taught the prince to steal the tally and go north to save Zhao, then cut his own throat.
translations.idiomatic: When Qin besieged Handan, he taught the prince to steal the command tally and ride north; then he killed himself.

sentences.zh: 又曰，豫讓，晉人。

translations.literal: Again it says: Yurang was a man of Jin.
translations.idiomatic: The text also names Yurang of Jin.

sentences.zh: 趙襄子滅智伯，讓曰：「士為知己者死。

translations.literal: Zhao Xiangzi destroyed Earl Zhi; Rang said: 'A gentleman dies for one who knows him.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhao Xiangzi destroyed the Zhi clan, Yurang declared, 'A knight dies for a patron who knows his worth.'

sentences.zh: 」乃變名姓，欲刺襄子，襄子令執之，遂伏□而死。

translations.literal: ' Then he changed name and surname, wished to stab Xiangzi; Xiangzi ordered him seized, then submitted [to execution] and died.
translations.idiomatic: He changed his identity and tried to kill Xiangzi; captured, he accepted execution.'

sentences.zh: 注[一四]易曰：「西南得朋。」

translations.literal: Commentary [fourteen]: The Changes says: 'In the southwest obtaining companions.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 14: The Changes speaks of 'finding companions in the southwest.'

sentences.zh: 注[一五]前書曰，陳遵字孟公，杜陵人也。

translations.literal: Commentary [fifteen]: The former Han History says: Chen Zun, courtesy name Menggong, was a man of Duling.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 15: Chen Zun, courtesy name Menggong, came from Duling.

sentences.zh: 張竦字伯松。

translations.literal: Zhang Song, courtesy name Bosong.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Song's courtesy name was Bosong.

sentences.zh: 竦博學通達，以廉儉自守，而遵放縱不拘。

translations.literal: Song was broadly learned and penetrating, guarded himself with integrity and frugality, while Zun was unrestrained and not bound.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Song was learned and austere; Chen Zun was flamboyant and unrestrained.

sentences.zh: 操行雖異，然相親友也。

translations.literal: Though conduct differed, yet they were intimate friends.
translations.idiomatic: Their temperaments differed, yet they were close friends.

sentences.zh: 樂恢字伯奇，京兆長陵人也。

translations.literal: Yue Hui, courtesy name Boqi, was a man of Changling in the capital district.
translations.idiomatic: Yue Hui, courtesy name Boqi, was a native of Changling in the metropolitan region.

sentences.zh: 父親，為縣吏，得罪於令，收將殺之。

translations.literal: His father Qin was a county clerk, offended the magistrate, was seized and about to be killed.
translations.idiomatic: His father Qin served as a county clerk, angered the magistrate, and was arrested for execution.

sentences.zh: 恢年十一，常俯伏寺門，晝夜號泣。

translations.literal: Hui at eleven constantly prostrated at the compound gate, day and night wailing and weeping.
translations.idiomatic: At eleven he lay day and night weeping at the gate of the county compound.

sentences.zh: 令聞而矜之，即解出親。

translations.literal: The magistrate heard and pitied him, then released Qin.
translations.idiomatic: The magistrate, moved, released his father.

sentences.zh: 恢長好經學，事博士焦永。

translations.literal: When grown Hui loved classical learning, served Erudite Jiao Yong.
translations.idiomatic: As a man he devoted himself to the classics under Erudite Jiao Yong.

sentences.zh: 永為河東太守，恢隨之官，閉廬精誦，不交人物。

translations.literal: When Yong became grand administrator of Hedong, Hui followed him to office, closed hut and intently recited, did not associate with people.
translations.idiomatic: When Jiao Yong became grand administrator of Hedong, Yue Hui went with him, shut himself in to study, and shunned society.

sentences.zh: 後永以事被考，諸弟子皆以通關被系，[一]恢獨*

translations.literal: Later Yong because of an affair was investigated; all disciples were bound for having communicated through passes, [one] Hui alone *
translations.idiomatic: Later Jiao Yong fell under investigation; his students were jailed for illicit contact with him [1], but Yue Hui alone

translations.literal: (bright)
translations.idiomatic: stood

sentences.zh: **[曒]*然不□於法，[二] 遂篤志為名儒。

translations.literal: **[bright]*ly not [stained] by the law, [two] then with firm intent became a famed Confucian.
translations.idiomatic: spotless under the law [2] and went on to become a celebrated scholar.

sentences.zh: 性廉直介立，[三]行不合己者，雖貴不與交。

translations.literal: By nature honest, straight, and standing apart, [three] if conduct did not match himself, though noble he did not associate.
translations.idiomatic: He was incorrupt and uncompromising [3]: he would not befriend anyone whose conduct offended him, however exalted.

sentences.zh: 信陽侯陰就數致禮請恢，恢絕不荅。

translations.literal: Marquis of Xinyang Yin Jiu repeatedly sent gifts inviting Hui; Hui utterly did not reply.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Jiu, marquis of Xinyang, courted him with repeated gifts; Yue Hui refused every overture.

sentences.zh: 注[一]為交通關涉也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Means communicating and involving through passes.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: That is, they had illicitly communicated with him.

sentences.zh: 注[二]*

translations.literal: Commentary [two] *
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2:

translations.literal: (bright)
translations.idiomatic: The graph

sentences.zh: **[曒]*，明也，音公鳥反。

translations.literal: **[jiao]* means bright, read gong-niao fan.
translations.idiomatic: jiao means 'bright,' read with the gong initial (fanqie).

sentences.zh: 或從「白」作「皎」，音亦同。

translations.literal: Some from 'white' write jiao; sound also same.
translations.idiomatic: Some write the same word with the 'white' phonetic, using the graph jiao, with the same pronunciation.

sentences.zh: 注[三]介，特也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Jie means singular.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Jie means standing alone or aloof.

sentences.zh: 後仕本郡吏，太守坐法誅，[一]故人莫敢往，恢獨奔喪行服，坐以抵罪。

translations.literal: Later he served as clerk in his home commandery; the grand administrator sat for law and was executed, [one] old friends none dared go; Hui alone rushed to mourning and wore mourning, sat thereby answering for crime.
translations.idiomatic: He later served his home commandery; when the grand administrator was executed for a crime [1], old friends dared not attend the funeral, but Yue Hui alone went in mourning and was punished for it.

sentences.zh: 歸，復為功曹，選舉不阿，請托無所容。

translations.literal: Returning, again became merit assessor; in selection not crooked, requests and entreaties had nowhere to lodge.
translations.idiomatic: Restored as merit assessor, he refused to bend appointments to favor or pull.

sentences.zh: 同郡楊政數觿毀恢，後舉政子為孝廉，由是鄉里歸之。

translations.literal: Yang Zheng of the same commandery repeatedly slandered Hui; later he raised Zheng's son as filial and incorrupt—thereby the village submitted to him.
translations.idiomatic: Though Yang Zheng of the same commandery had often slandered him, he later recommended Yang's son as filial and incorrupt, and the district came to honor him.

sentences.zh: 辟司空牟融府。

translations.literal: He was summoned to Minister of Works Mou Rong's office.
translations.idiomatic: He was summoned to the ministry of Minister of Works Mou Rong.

sentences.zh: 會蜀郡太守第五倫代融為司空，恢以與倫同郡，不肯留，薦穎川杜安而退。

translations.literal: When Shu commandery grand administrator Diwu Lun replaced Rong as minister of works, Hui because he was of the same commandery as Lun would not stay, recommended Yingchuan Du An and withdrew.
translations.idiomatic: When Diwu Lun of Shu succeeded Mou Rong as minister of works, Yue Hui, being from the same commandery as Diwu Lun, declined to remain, recommended Du An of Yingchuan, and withdrew.

sentences.zh: 諸公多其行，連辟之，遂皆不應。

translations.literal: Dukes admired his conduct, repeatedly summoned him—he all did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: High ministers admired his integrity and summoned him repeatedly; he refused every call.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]東觀記京兆尹張恂召恢，署戶曹史。

translations.literal: [Two] Commentary [one]: Eastern Han Records: metropolitan governor Zhang Xun summoned Hui, appointed him clerk of the household section.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Commentary 1: The Eastern Han Records notes that Metropolitan Governor Zhang Xun appointed him clerk of the household section.

sentences.zh: 注[二]華嶠書曰：「安擢為宛令，以病去。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Hua Qiao's book says: 'An was promoted Wan magistrate, left because of illness.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Hua Qiao records that Du An became magistrate of Wan but resigned because of illness.'

sentences.zh: 章帝行過穎川，安上書，召拜御史，遷至巴郡太守。

translations.literal: When Emperor Zhang passed through Yingchuan, Du An submitted a memorial; he was summoned as attendant censor and rose to grand administrator of Ba.
translations.idiomatic: On an imperial progress through Yingchuan, Zhang Di read Du An's memorial, appointed him attendant censor, and eventually promoted him to grand administrator of Ba.

sentences.zh: 而恢在家，安與恢書通問，恢告吏口謝，且讓之曰：『為宛令不合志，病去可也。

translations.literal: Meanwhile Hui was at home; An corresponded with him, but Hui had a clerk convey oral thanks and rebuked him: 'To resign as magistrate of Wan because the post did not suit your aims—on grounds of illness—that can be allowed.
translations.idiomatic: Yue Hui was still at home when Du An wrote; Yue Hui had a clerk thank him orally and scolded him: 'Giving up the magistracy of Wan because it cramped your style—illness is a fair excuse.'

sentences.zh: 干人主以窺覦，非也。

translations.literal: To tug at the sovereign's sleeve from ambition is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Importuning the throne for preferment is another matter entirely.'

sentences.zh: 違平生操，故不報。

translations.literal: It betrays the principles you have kept all your life; therefore I do not answer.
translations.idiomatic: It breaks a lifetime of scruple, so I offer no reply.'

sentences.zh: 』安亦節士也，年十三入太學，號奇童。

translations.literal: ' An too was a man of integrity; at thirteen he entered the Imperial Academy and was hailed a wonder child.
translations.idiomatic: Du An was himself a man of principle: at thirteen he entered the academy and was celebrated as a prodigy.'

sentences.zh: 洛陽令周紆自往候安，安謝不見。

translations.literal: Luoyang Prefect Zhou Xu went in person to call on An; An declined and would not receive him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Xu, prefect of Luoyang, called on him in person; Du An refused to come out.

sentences.zh: 京師貴戚慕其行，或遺之書，安不發，悉壁藏之。

translations.literal: Noble in-laws in the capital admired his conduct and sometimes sent him letters; he never opened them but walled them up for safekeeping.
translations.idiomatic: Powerful families at court admired him and sent letters; he sealed them unopened inside a wall.

sentences.zh: 及後捕案貴戚賓客，安開壁出書，印封如故。」

translations.literal: Later, when those nobles' retainers were arrested and investigated, An broke open the niche and produced the letters—the seals were still intact.'
translations.idiomatic: When the great houses were later investigated, he broke open the cache; every envelope bore its original seal intact.'

sentences.zh: 後征拜議郎。

translations.literal: Later he was summoned as gentleman consultant.
translations.idiomatic: He was later recalled to serve as a gentleman consultant.

sentences.zh: 會車騎將軍竇憲出征匈奴，恢數上書諫爭，朝廷稱其忠。

translations.literal: It happened that General of Chariots and Cavalry Dou Xian was campaigning against the Xiongnu; Hui repeatedly submitted memorials remonstrating, and the court praised his loyalty.
translations.idiomatic: When Dou Xian led the northern campaign, Yue Hui sent memorial after memorial in protest, and the court praised his loyalty.

sentences.zh: [一]入為尚書僕射。

translations.literal: [One] He entered office as vice director of the secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He was brought in as vice director of the secretariat.

sentences.zh: 是時河南尹王調、洛陽令李阜與竇憲厚善，縱捨自由。

translations.literal: At that time the metropolitan governor of Henan, Wang Diao, and the Luoyang prefect, Li Gao, were thick with Dou Xun and did as they pleased.
translations.idiomatic: The metropolitan governor Wang Diao and Luoyang prefect Li Gao were Dou Xian's intimates and abused their power.

sentences.zh: 恢劾奏調、阜，並及司隸校尉。

translations.literal: Hui impeached Diao and Gao, extending the charge to the metropolitan commandant as well.
translations.idiomatic: Yue Hui memorialized against both men and implicated the metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 諸所刺舉，無所迴避，貴戚惡之。

translations.literal: In every accusation he raised he never shrank from powerful targets; the noble houses hated him.
translations.idiomatic: He named the guilty without flinching at rank; the great clans detested him.

sentences.zh: [二]憲弟夏陽侯纓欲往候恢，恢謝不與通。

translations.literal: [Two] Xian's younger brother, Marquis of Xiayang Dou Kui, wished to visit Hui; Hui declined and would not admit him.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Dou Kui, marquis of Xiayang and Dou Xian's brother, tried to pay a call; Yue Hui shut the door.

sentences.zh: 憲兄弟放縱，而忿其不附己。

translations.literal: The Dou brothers gave free rein to their will and resented his refusal to attach himself to them.
translations.idiomatic: The Dou brothers were lawless and bitter that he would not join their faction.

sentences.zh: 妻每諫恢曰：「昔人有容身避害，何必以言取怨？

translations.literal: His wife often remonstrated with Hui: 'Men of old hid their persons to escape harm—why invite hatred with blunt speech?'
translations.idiomatic: His wife urged him, 'The ancients withdrew to save themselves—why court resentment with outspokenness?'

sentences.zh: 」恢歎曰：「吾何忍素餐立人之朝乎！

translations.literal: ' Hui sighed: 'How could I bear to stand in court and eat unearned salary!'
translations.idiomatic: Yue Hui answered, 'I cannot bear to hold office and draw pay I have not earned.'

sentences.zh: 」遂上疏諫曰：「臣聞百王之失，皆由權移於下。

translations.literal: ' Then he submitted a memorial remonstrating: 'Your servant has heard that the hundred kings' failures all come from power slipping downward.
translations.idiomatic: He then memorialized: 'The fall of every dynasty begins when authority drains from the throne.'

sentences.zh: 大臣持國，常以埶盛為咎。

translations.literal: When great ministers hold the state, disaster usually follows swollen power.
translations.idiomatic: When chief ministers grip the government, excess of power is the root of calamity.'

sentences.zh: 伏念先帝，聖德未永，早棄萬國。

translations.literal: I reflect that the late emperor's sacred virtue was not long extended before he abandoned the myriad regions.
translations.idiomatic: Our late emperor's reign was cut short and he left the realm too soon.'

sentences.zh: 陛下富於春秋，纂承大業，[三]諸舅不宜干正王室，以示天下之私。

translations.literal: Your Majesty is still in the spring and autumn of life, inheriting the great enterprise; [three] your maternal uncles ought not to meddle in the royal house and show the realm a private family.
translations.idiomatic: You are young and have inherited the throne [3]; your uncles must not intrude on the Han house and teach the empire that power is a family affair.'

sentences.zh: 經曰：『天地乖互，觿物夭傷。

translations.literal: The classics say: 'When heaven and earth are out of joint, the myriad things wither.
translations.idiomatic: The canon warns: When heaven and earth fall out of harmony, creation suffers.'

sentences.zh: 君臣失序，萬人受殃。

translations.literal: When lord and minister lose their proper order, the myriad people suffer calamity.
translations.idiomatic: When ruler and minister lose their stations, the people pay the price.'

sentences.zh: 』政失不救，其極不測。

translations.literal: ' If misrule is not corrected, the end cannot be foretold.
translations.idiomatic: Unchecked disorder leads to ends no one can predict.'

sentences.zh: 方今之宜，上以義自割，下以謙自引。

translations.literal: What is fitting now is for those above to restrain themselves by duty and for those below to yield place in humility.
translations.idiomatic: The right course is for the throne to cut back out of principle and for your kin to withdraw in modesty.'

sentences.zh: 四舅可長保爵土之榮，[四]皇太后永無籩負宗廟之憂，誠策之上者也。

translations.literal: Then the four uncles may long preserve the honor of their fiefs, [four] and the empress dowager will never bear the shame of failing the ancestral temple—this is the best policy of all.
translations.idiomatic: Your four uncles would keep their noble rank in safety [4], and the empress dowager would never fear disgracing the imperial shrines—that is the supreme strategy.'

sentences.zh: 」書奏不省。

translations.literal: ' The memorial was tabled without response.
translations.idiomatic: The court filed his words away and took no action.'

sentences.zh: 時竇太后臨朝，和帝未親萬機，恢以意不得行，乃稱疾乞骸骨。

translations.literal: At that time Empress Dowager Dou held court and Emperor He had not yet taken the reins; Hui, seeing his counsel ignored, pleaded illness and begged leave to retire.
translations.idiomatic: With the Dou empress dowager regent and the emperor still a minor, Yue Hui saw he could not prevail and asked to retire on grounds of illness.

sentences.zh: 詔賜錢，太醫視疾。

translations.literal: An edict granted him cash and sent the imperial physician to examine him.
translations.idiomatic: The throne sent money and the court physician to tend him.

sentences.zh: 恢薦任城郭均、成陽高鳳，而遂稱篤。

translations.literal: Hui recommended Guo Jun of Rencheng and Gao Feng of Chengyang, then claimed his illness was grave.
translations.idiomatic: He nominated Guo Jun of Rencheng and Gao Feng of Chengyang, then insisted he was mortally ill.

sentences.zh: 拜騎都尉，上書辭謝曰：「仍受厚恩，無以報效。

translations.literal: He was appointed chief of cavalry and submitted a memorial declining: 'I have received deep favor and have no way to repay it.
translations.idiomatic: Named chief of cavalry, he memorialized his refusal: 'I owe too much already to repay with service.'

sentences.zh: 夫政在大夫，孔子所疾；

translations.literal: When government rests with great ministers, that is what Confucius deplored;
translations.idiomatic: Confucius condemned regimes where ministers held real power;'

sentences.zh: [五]世卿持權，春秋以戒。

translations.literal: [five] hereditary ministers clutching authority is what the Spring and Autumn Annals warn against.
translations.idiomatic: [5] the Annals warn against noble houses that monopolize office generation after generation.'

sentences.zh: [六]聖人懇惻，不虛言也。

translations.literal: [six] The sage spoke in earnest—his words were not empty.
translations.idiomatic: [6] The sage meant every word of that warning.'

sentences.zh: 近世外戚富貴，必有驕溢之敗。

translations.literal: In recent generations outer kin who grew rich and honored always met ruin through arrogance and excess.
translations.idiomatic: In our own day maternal relatives who rose to wealth and power have always fallen to pride and license.'

sentences.zh: 今陛下思慕山陵，未遑政事；

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty mourns at the mausoleum and has not yet turned fully to government;
translations.idiomatic: The emperor still grieves for his father and has little time for statecraft;'

sentences.zh: 諸舅寵盛，權行四方。

translations.literal: your uncles enjoy overwhelming favor and wield influence in every quarter.
translations.idiomatic: meanwhile your uncles bask in favor and their orders run through the four directions.'

sentences.zh: 若不能自損，誅罰必加。

translations.literal: If they will not restrain themselves, punishment will surely follow.
translations.idiomatic: If they do not pull back, the axe will fall.'

sentences.zh: 臣壽命垂盡，臨死竭愚，惟蒙留神。

translations.literal: Your servant's life is nearly spent; facing death I exhaust my foolish loyalty—only beg that Your Majesty take heed.
translations.idiomatic: I am near my end; I speak this folly with my last breath—only let the throne consider it.'

sentences.zh: 」詔聽上印綬，乃歸鄉里。

translations.literal: ' An edict permitted him to surrender his seal and cord, and he returned to his village.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor allowed him to yield his seal and return home.'

sentences.zh: 竇憲因是風厲州郡迫脅，恢遂飲藥死。

translations.literal: Dou Xian therefore secretly urged commanderies to threaten him; Hui drank poison and died.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Xian then leaned on local officials to hound him; Yue Hui took poison and died.

sentences.zh: 弟子縗絰挽者數百人，[七]觿庶痛傷之。

translations.literal: Several hundred disciples in mourning sashes pulled his bier; [seven] the common folk grieved for him.
translations.idiomatic: Hundreds of students in mourning drew his hearse [7], and the people mourned him.

sentences.zh: 注[一]東觀記載恢所上書諫曰：「春秋之義，王者不理夷狄。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Eastern Han Records carries Hui's memorial remonstrating: 'By the Spring and Autumn principle, the true king does not busy himself with the barbarians.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: The Eastern Han Records preserves Yue Hui's argument that the sage-kings left the barbarians alone.'

sentences.zh: 得其地不可墾發，得其人無益於政，故明王之於夷狄，紅縻而已。

translations.literal: Their land cannot be opened to cultivation; their people bring no profit to government—therefore the enlightened king holds the barbarians on slack reins only.
translations.idiomatic: Their territory is not worth farming, their tribes not worth governing—the wise king keeps them on a loose tether, nothing more.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『遠人不服，則修文德以來之。

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'If distant peoples will not submit, cultivate civil virtue to attract them.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, 'When the far peoples refuse allegiance, improve culture and virtue to draw them in.'

sentences.zh: 』以漢之盛，不務修舜、禹、周公之*

translations.literal: ' At the height of Han power, instead of cultivating the arts of Shun, Yu, and the Duke of Zhou,
translations.idiomatic: Yet at Han's zenith the court would not polish the ways of Shun, Yu, and the Duke of Zhou'

translations.literal: (arts)
translations.idiomatic: (methods)

sentences.zh: **[德]*，而無故興干戈，動兵革，以求無用之物，臣誠惑之！」

translations.literal: **[virtue]*, it wantonly raises armies and shakes the realm to seek useless things—your servant is truly perplexed!'
translations.idiomatic: but stirs up war without cause to chase worthless gain—I cannot fathom it!'

sentences.zh: 注[二]決錄注曰：「調字叔和，為河南尹。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Notes to the Judgments of the Three Capitals say: 'Diao, courtesy name Shuhe, was metropolitan governor of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: The Judgments of the Three Capitals note that Wang Diao, courtesy name Shuhe, governed Henan.'

sentences.zh: 永和二年，坐買洛陽令同郡任稜竹田及上罷城東漕渠免官。」

translations.literal: In the second year of Yonghe he was dismissed for buying bamboo land from Luoyang prefect Ren Ling of his commandery and for other irregularities concerning the eastern transport canal.'
translations.idiomatic: In Yonghe 2 he lost office for buying bamboo groves from Ren Ling, prefect of Luoyang, and for abuses connected with the eastern canal.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]春秋謂年也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: 'Spring and autumn' here means years of age.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: 'Spring and autumn' means the emperor's youth.

sentences.zh: 言年少，春秋尚多，故稱富。

translations.literal: It means he was still young—many years lay ahead—hence 'rich in spring and autumn.'
translations.idiomatic: The phrase stresses that he was young and had long years before him.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]四舅謂竇憲、弟篤、景、纓也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: The four maternal uncles are Dou Xian and his brothers Dou Du, Dou Jing, and Dou Kui.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: The four uncles are Dou Xian, Dou Du, Dou Jing, and Dou Kui.

sentences.zh: 注[五]論語孔子曰：「天下有道，政不在大夫。」

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: The Analects records Confucius: 'When the Way prevails in the realm, government does not rest with great ministers.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: Confucius said that when the Way prevails, power does not lodge with ministers.

sentences.zh: 注[六]左傳曰：「齊崔氏出奔□。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: The Zuo Tradition says: 'The house of Cui of Qi fled into exile.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 6: The Zuo Commentary tells of the exile of the Cui clan of Qi.'

sentences.zh: 」公羊傳曰：「崔氏者何？

translations.literal: ' The Gongyang Commentary asks: 'What is meant by the house of Cui?'
translations.idiomatic: The Gongyang Commentary asks who the house of Cui were.'

sentences.zh: 齊大夫。

translations.literal: A great officer of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: They were great officers of Qi.'

sentences.zh: 稱崔氏者何？

translations.literal: Why is the clan named?
translations.idiomatic: Why name the whole clan?

sentences.zh: 貶。

translations.literal: To demote.
translations.idiomatic: To mark condemnation.'

sentences.zh: 曷為貶？

translations.literal: Why demote?
translations.idiomatic: Why single them out?

sentences.zh: 譏世卿也。」

translations.literal: To satirize hereditary ministers.'
translations.idiomatic: To rebuke ministers who monopolized office.'

sentences.zh: 注[七]挽，引柩也。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: Wan means to draw the bier.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 7: Wan means to pull the funeral carriage.

sentences.zh: 後竇氏誅，帝始親事，恢門生何融等上書陳恢忠節，除子己為郎中。

translations.literal: After the Dou clan was executed and the emperor began to rule in person, Hui's disciple He Rong and others submitted a memorial praising Hui's loyalty; his son Ji was appointed gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: When the Dou family fell and the emperor took power, student He Rong and others memorialized Yue Hui's loyalty, and his son Yue Ji was made a gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]三輔決錄注曰：「己字伯文，為郎非其好也，去官。」

translations.literal: [One] Notes to the Judgments of the Three Capitals say: 'Ji, courtesy name Bowen, became a gentleman though it was not his wish, and resigned.'
translations.idiomatic: [1] The same work says Yue Ji, courtesy name Bowen, disliked serving as a gentleman and quit.

sentences.zh: 何敞字文高，扶風平陵人也。

translations.literal: He Chang, courtesy name Wengao, was a man of Pingling in Fufeng.
translations.idiomatic: He Chang, courtesy name Wengao, came from Pingling in Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 其先家於汝陰。

translations.literal: His ancestors had dwelt in Ruyin.
translations.idiomatic: His family had earlier lived in Ruyin.

sentences.zh: 六世祖比干，學尚書於朝錯，[一] 武帝時為廷尉正，與張湯同時。

translations.literal: His sixth-generation ancestor Bigan studied the Documents with Chao Cuo; [one] under Emperor Wu he was rectifier for the commandant of justice, serving alongside Zhang Tang.
translations.idiomatic: Six generations back, He Bigan studied the Documents under Chao Cuo [1] and under Emperor Wu served as rectifier under the commandant of justice with Zhang Tang.

sentences.zh: 湯持法深而比干務仁恕，數與湯爭，雖不能盡得，然所濟活者以千數。

translations.literal: Tang enforced the law harshly while Bigan strove for humanity and forgiveness; he often argued with Tang and, though he could not always win, the lives he saved ran to thousands.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Tang was severe; He Bigan pressed for mercy and often crossed him—though he did not always prevail, he saved thousands of lives.

sentences.zh: 後遷丹*

translations.literal: Later he was promoted to *
translations.idiomatic: He was later promoted to

translations.literal: (Dan)
translations.idiomatic: (Dan)

sentences.zh: **[陽]*都尉，因徙居平陵。

translations.literal: **[Yang]* commandant of cavalry and moved his home to Pingling.
translations.idiomatic: commandant of cavalry in Danyang and resettled at Pingling.

sentences.zh: 敞父寵，建武中為千乘都尉，以病免，遂隱居不仕。

translations.literal: Chang's father Chong, in the Jianwu era, was commandant of cavalry in Qiansheng; he retired on grounds of illness and lived in seclusion without taking office.
translations.idiomatic: His father He Chong had been commandant of cavalry at Qiansheng under Guangwu; ill health forced him to retire, and he lived out his days in reclusion.

sentences.zh: 注[一]何氏家傳：「*

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The He family tradition: '
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: The He family tradition records that

sentences.zh: (雲並)

translations.literal: (six generations)
translations.idiomatic: (six generations)

sentences.zh: **[六世]*祖父比干，字少卿，經明行修，兼通法律。

translations.literal: **[six generations]* grandfather Bigan, courtesy name Shaoqing, was clear in the classics and upright in conduct, and also versed in law.
translations.idiomatic: his ancestor Bigan, courtesy name Shaoqing, mastered the classics and law and was a man of upright conduct.'

sentences.zh: 為汝陰縣獄吏決曹掾，平活數千人。

translations.literal: He served as jail clerk and decision-clerk in Ruyin county and spared several thousand lives in verdicts.
translations.idiomatic: As jail clerk and decision clerk in Ruyin he acquitted thousands.

sentences.zh: 後為丹陽都尉，獄無冤囚，淮汝號曰『何公』。

translations.literal: Later he was commandant of cavalry in Danyang; no innocent man sat in his jail, and between the Huai and Ru they called him Lord He.
translations.idiomatic: As commandant of cavalry in Danyang he kept his prisons free of wrongful convictions, and the Huai-Ru region hailed him as Lord He.'

sentences.zh: 征和三年三月辛亥，天大陰雨，比干在家，日中夢貴客車騎滿門，覺以語妻。

translations.literal: On the day xinhai, third month of the third year of Zhenghe, the sky grew dark with rain; at noon Bigan dreamed noble guests' chariots filled his gate; waking, he told his wife.
translations.idiomatic: On a xinhai day in Zhenghe 3 the heavens opened in a downpour; at noon Bigan dreamed noble carriages thronged his gate and told his wife when he woke.

sentences.zh: 語未已，而門有老嫗可八十餘，頭白，求寄避雨，雨甚而衣履不沾清。

translations.literal: Before he finished speaking, an old woman of perhaps eighty, hair white, begged shelter from the rain; though the rain was heavy her clothes and shoes showed no damp stain.
translations.idiomatic: An old woman of eighty with white hair asked shelter; though the storm raged, her garments stayed dry.

sentences.zh: 雨止，送至門，乃謂比干曰：『公有陰德，今天錫君策，以廣公之子孫。

translations.literal: When the rain stopped he escorted her to the gate; she said to Bigan: 'You have hidden virtue; heaven now grants you tallies to enlarge your posterity.
translations.idiomatic: As she left she told him, 'Secret virtue fills your house; heaven sends tallies to bless your descendants.'

sentences.zh: 』因出懷中符策，狀如簡，長九寸，凡九百九十枚，以授比干，子孫佩印綬者當如此筭。

translations.literal: ' She drew from her bosom tallies like slips, nine cun long, nine hundred ninety in all, and gave them to Bigan, saying his descendants who bore seals and cords would match that count.
translations.idiomatic: She produced nine hundred ninety slips from her sleeve, each nine inches long, and said the number of his posterity who would wear seals would match that count.'

sentences.zh: 比干年五十八，有六男，又生三子。

translations.literal: Bigan lived fifty-eight years and had six sons; three more sons were born afterward.
translations.idiomatic: He lived fifty-eight years and had six sons, then three more.

sentences.zh: 本始元年，自汝陰徙平陵，代為名族。」

translations.literal: In the first year of Benshi the family moved from Ruyin to Pingling and became a noted clan.'
translations.idiomatic: In Benshi 1 they moved from Ruyin to Pingling and rose to prominence.'

sentences.zh: 敞性公正。

translations.literal: Chang's nature was fair and upright.
translations.idiomatic: He Chang was fair-minded and principled.

sentences.zh: 自以趣捨不合時務，每請召，常稱疾不應。

translations.literal: Because his choices did not match the temper of the times, whenever summons came he usually pleaded illness and would not respond.
translations.idiomatic: Finding the age uncongenial, he habitually declined summons on grounds of illness.

sentences.zh: 元和中，辟太尉宋由府，由待以殊禮。

translations.literal: In the Yuanhe era he was summoned to Grand Commandant Song You's office; You treated him with special courtesy.
translations.idiomatic: During Yuanhe he entered Grand Commandant Song You's service and was treated with exceptional respect.

sentences.zh: 敞論議高，常引大體，多所匡正。

translations.literal: Chang's discourse was lofty; he constantly cited great principles and corrected much that was wrong.
translations.idiomatic: His debate was elevated; he cited the larger pattern of government and set many errors right.

sentences.zh: 司徒袁安亦深敬重之。

translations.literal: Minister of Education Yuan An also held him in deep esteem.
translations.idiomatic: Minister of Education Yuan An esteemed him equally.

sentences.zh: 是時京師及四方累有奇異鳥獸草木，言事者以為祥瑞。

translations.literal: At that time the capital and the four quarters repeatedly produced strange birds, beasts, and plants; those who spoke on affairs deemed them auspicious omens.
translations.idiomatic: Odd creatures and plants kept appearing in the capital and provinces; memorialists hailed them as good omens.

sentences.zh: 敞通經傳，能為天官，意甚惡之。

translations.literal: Chang was versed in the classics and commentaries and understood astronomy; he deeply disliked such talk.
translations.idiomatic: Well read in the classics and skilled in celestial signs, he detested such flattery.

sentences.zh: 乃言於二公曰：「夫瑞應依德而至，災異緣政而生。

translations.literal: He said to the two dukes: 'Good omens attend virtue; portents of ill follow misrule.
translations.idiomatic: He told the two ministers, 'Lucky signs follow virtue; disasters track misgovernment.'

sentences.zh: 故鈽鵒來巢，昭公有干侯之□；

translations.literal: Therefore when shrikes built nests, Duke Zhao met exile at Ganhou;
translations.idiomatic: When shrikes nested in Lu, Duke Zhao was driven to Ganhou;'

sentences.zh: [一]西狩獲麟，孔子有兩楹之殯。

translations.literal: [one] when a unicorn was taken on the western hunt, Confucius faced his death between two pillars.
translations.idiomatic: [1] when a unicorn was captured in the west, Confucius knew his death was near.'

sentences.zh: [二]海鳥避風，臧文祀之，君子譏焉。

translations.literal: [two] When a sea bird fled the wind, Zang Wenzhong sacrificed to it—and gentlemen mocked him.
translations.idiomatic: [2] When Zang Wenzhong sacrificed to a storm-driven sea bird, wise men laughed.'

sentences.zh: [三]今異鳥翔於殿屋，怪草生於庭際，不可不察。

translations.literal: [three] Now strange birds wheel above the palace halls and freak grass grows in the courtyard—this cannot pass without scrutiny.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Strange birds circle the roof and uncanny plants sprout in the courtyard—these demand investigation, not praise.'

sentences.zh: 」由、安懼然不敢荅。

translations.literal: ' You and Yuan looked stricken and dared not answer.
translations.idiomatic: Song You and Yuan An blanched and had no reply.'

sentences.zh: [四] 居無何而肅宗崩。

translations.literal: [four] Before long Emperor Zhang died.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Soon afterward Emperor Zhang died.

sentences.zh: 注[一]春秋：「有鈽鵒來巢。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Spring and Autumn Annals records: 'Shrikes came to nest.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: The Annals record that shrikes nested in Lu.'

sentences.zh: 」左氏傳魯大夫師已曰：「文、成之世，童謠有之曰：『鈽鵒之羽，公在外野，往饋之馬。

translations.literal: ' The Zuo Tradition has the Lu officer Shi Ji say: 'In the times of dukes Wen and Cheng a children's song ran: The shrike's feathers—the duke abroad in the wilds; we go to feed his horse.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Commentary quotes Shi Ji: In the reigns of Wen and Cheng a children's song said of the shrike, "The duke wanders the field; we feed his horse."'

sentences.zh: 鈽鵒跦跦，公在干侯。

translations.literal: The shrikes hop-hop; the duke is at Ganhou.
translations.idiomatic: The shrikes flit and hop; the exiled duke waits at Ganhou.

sentences.zh: 』」季平子逐昭公，公遜於干侯。

translations.literal: ' ' Jipingzi expelled Duke Zhao; the duke withdrew to Ganhou.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Pingzi drove Duke Zhao out; the duke took refuge at Ganhou.'

sentences.zh: 杜預註：「干侯在魏郡斥丘縣，晉境內邑也。」

translations.literal: Du Yu's commentary: 'Ganhou is in Chiqiu county, Wei commandery—a town within Jin territory.'
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu locates Ganhou at Chiqiu in Wei commandery, on Jin soil.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]公羊傳曰：「西狩獲麟，有以告孔子者曰：『有志而角者何？

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Gongyang Commentary says: 'On the western hunt they caught a unicorn; someone who told Confucius said: What has purpose yet horns?'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: The Gongyang Commentary describes the western hunt that caught a unicorn and the puzzled question put to Confucius.'

sentences.zh: 』孔子曰：『孰為來哉！

translations.literal: ' Confucius said: 'For whom has it come!'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius exclaimed, 'What brings you here!'

sentences.zh: 孰為來哉！

translations.literal: For whom has it come!'
translations.idiomatic: What brings you here!'

sentences.zh: 』反袂拭面，涕下沾袍，曰：『吾道窮矣！

translations.literal: ' He turned his sleeve, wiped his face, tears wetting his robe, saying: 'My way is ended!'
translations.idiomatic: He covered his face with his sleeve and wept, 'My teaching has reached its end.'

sentences.zh: 』」何氏注曰：「麟者，太平之符，聖人之類。

translations.literal: ' ' He Yan's commentary says: 'The unicorn is the token of great peace, the kind of the sage.
translations.idiomatic: He Yan glosses the unicorn as the omen of Great Peace and the sage's counterpart.'

sentences.zh: 時得麟而死，此亦天告夫子將沒之征也。

translations.literal: When it was obtained yet died, this too was heaven telling the Master he would soon perish.
translations.idiomatic: Its capture and death foretold Confucius's own death.

sentences.zh: 」禮記孔子謂子貢曰：「予疇昔夜夢坐奠於兩楹之閒焉。

translations.literal: ' The Record of Rites has Confucius say to Zigong: 'Yesterday I dreamed I sat with offerings between two pillars.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Rites records Confucius telling Zigong he dreamed of sitting with offerings between two pillars.'

sentences.zh: 殷人殯於兩楹之閒，丘即殷人也，予殆將死也。

translations.literal: The Yin people lay out the bier between two pillars; Qiu is a man of Yin—I am about to die.
translations.idiomatic: The Yin laid out their dead between two pillars; I am of Yin stock—I am dying.'

sentences.zh: 」遂寢疾，七日而死。

translations.literal: ' Then he fell ill and died after seven days.
translations.idiomatic: He took ill and died within seven days.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]國語曰，海鳥爰居，止於魯東門之外三日，臧文仲使國人祭之。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The Discourses of the States says: the sea bird Yuanju alighted outside Lu's east gate for three days; Zang Wenzhong had the people sacrifice to it.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: The Discourses of the States tells how a sea bird rested three days outside Lu's east gate and Zang Wenzhong ordered sacrifices.'

sentences.zh: 展禽譏焉，因曰：「今茲海其有風乎？

translations.literal: Zhan Qin mocked this, saying: 'This year will the sea perhaps have great winds?'
translations.idiomatic: Zhan Qin mocked him, asking whether the sea would send great winds.'

sentences.zh: 廣川之鳥恆知避風。

translations.literal: Birds of the broad rivers always know to flee the wind.
translations.idiomatic: Coastal birds always flee storms.'

sentences.zh: 」是歲海多大風，冬暖。

translations.literal: ' That year the sea had many great winds; winter was warm.
translations.idiomatic: That year brought violent winds at sea and a mild winter.'

sentences.zh: 文仲聞之，曰：「吾過矣！」

translations.literal: When Wenzhong heard it, he said: 'I was wrong!'
translations.idiomatic: Wenzhong admitted his error.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]懼音紀具反。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Ju read ji-ju fan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: Ju is read ji-ju (fanqie).

sentences.zh: 時竇氏專政，外戚奢侈，賞賜過制，倉帑為虛。

translations.literal: At that time the Dou clan monopolized government; outer kin were extravagant; rewards exceeded regulation and the treasuries were emptied.
translations.idiomatic: The Dou clan ran the government; imperial in-laws spent wildly; gifts broke precedent and emptied the vaults.

sentences.zh: [一]敞奏記由曰：「敞聞事君之義，進思盡忠，退思補過。

translations.literal: [One] Chang submitted a written memorial to You saying: 'Chang has heard that serving a lord's duty is: advancing, think to exhaust loyalty; withdrawing, think to mend faults.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He Chang wrote Song You: 'A minister owes full loyalty in office and self-correction in retirement.'

sentences.zh: 歷觀世主時臣，無不各欲為化，垂之無窮，然而平和之政萬無一者，蓋以聖主賢臣不能相遭故也。

translations.literal: Surveying rulers and ministers of the age, none lacked the wish to transform the world and leave a name forever—yet a single reign of true peace is rare, because sage lord and worthy minister seldom meet.
translations.idiomatic: Every ruler and minister hopes to reform the age, yet lasting peace is rare because worthy pairs seldom meet.'

sentences.zh: 今國家秉聰明之弘道，明公履晏晏之純德，[二]君臣相合，天下翕然，治平之化，有望於今。

translations.literal: Now the state holds the great way of keen intelligence; the enlightened lord treads the pure virtue of mildness; ruler and minister join in harmony and the realm is at one—an era of order may be hoped for now.
translations.idiomatic: The Han now follows enlightened policy; you embody gentle virtue; court and minister are aligned—the age of good government may be at hand.'

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『如有用我者，三年有成。

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'If someone would use me, within three years there would be results.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, 'Employ me, and within three years there will be results.'

sentences.zh: 』今明公視事，出入再開，宜當克己，以□四海之心。

translations.literal: ' Now the enlightened lord has held office through two openings of the gates; you ought to restrain yourself to [meet] the four seas' hearts.
translations.idiomatic: You have passed two cycles of the palace gates; restrain yourself now and answer the hopes of the realm.'

sentences.zh: 禮，一谷不升，則損服徹膳。

translations.literal: By ritual, if one grade of grain fails to ripen, then reduce garments and cut feasts.
translations.idiomatic: Ritual requires that when one grade of grain fails, the ruler cut wardrobe and table.'

sentences.zh: [三]天下不足，若己使然。

translations.literal: [three] If the realm is insufficient, it is as if oneself had caused it.
translations.idiomatic: [3] When the empire suffers want, the ruler must own it as his fault.'

sentences.zh: 而比年水旱，人不收穫，涼州緣邊，家被凶害，[四]男子疲於戰陳，妻女勞於轉運，老幼孤寡，歎息相依，又中州內郡，公私屈竭，此實損膳節用之時。

translations.literal: Yet year after year flood and drought have left the people without harvest; along Liangzhou's frontier every household has suffered disaster; [four] men grow weary in the lines of battle, wives and daughters labor at transport, old and young, widows and orphans sigh and cling to one another; in the inner commanderies public and private stores are exhausted—this is truly the time to cut feasts and practice economy.
translations.idiomatic: Yet floods and droughts have ruined harvests for years; the Liangzhou marches are devastated [4]; soldiers tire at the front, women haul supplies, the helpless cling together, and the heartland is drained—this is the hour for austerity.'

sentences.zh: 國恩覆載，賞賚過度，但聞臘賜，自郎官以上，公卿王侯以下，至於空竭帑藏，損耗國資。

translations.literal: State favor covers and carries all, yet grants and gifts pass measure; at the year-end distribution, from gentlemen upward through dukes and marquises down to the last rank, the treasuries are drained and state wealth wasted.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial largesse knows no limit: year-end bounty runs from gentlemen to princes until the vaults are bare.'

sentences.zh: 尋公家之用，皆百姓之力。

translations.literal: Search out: public use is all the people's strength.
translations.idiomatic: What the court spends is drawn from the people.'

sentences.zh: 明君賜賚，宜有品制，忠臣受賞，亦應有度，[五]是以夏禹玄圭，周公束帛。

translations.literal: An enlightened ruler's gifts ought to follow rank; a loyal minister's rewards ought to have measure—[five] therefore Xia Yu had the black jade tablet, the Duke of Zhou received silken bundles.
translations.idiomatic: Wise sovereigns give by rule; loyal ministers take by measure [5]—hence Yu's black scepter and the Duke of Zhou's silks.'

sentences.zh: [六]今明公位尊任重，責深負大，上當匡正綱紀，下當濟安元元，豈但空空無違而已哉！

translations.literal: [six] Now the enlightened lord's position is honored and burden heavy, responsibility deep and load great; above you should rectify the net of laws, below bring peace to the common folk—how can it be enough merely to commit no open fault!
translations.idiomatic: [6] You bear high office: set the laws aright above and comfort the people below—mere avoidance of fault is not enough!'

sentences.zh: 宜先正己以率腢下，還所得賜，因陳得失，奏王侯就國，除苑囿之禁，節省浮費，賑恤窮孤，則恩澤下暢，黎庶悅豫，上天聰明，必有立應。

translations.literal: You should first correct yourself to lead those below, return what you have received in gifts, state gains and losses, memorialize kings and marquises to go to their fiefs, lift bans on hunting parks, cut needless expense, relieve the destitute—then favor will flow downward, the people will rejoice, bright heaven will hear, and there will be an immediate response.
translations.idiomatic: Lead by example, return excess gifts, report true conditions, send nobles to their fiefs, open the parks, cut waste, aid the poor—then grace will spread, the people will cheer, and heaven will answer.'

sentences.zh: 使百姓歌誦，史官紀德，豈但子文逃祿，[七]公儀退食之比哉！

translations.literal: Let the hundred surnames sing your praise and scribes record your virtue—would that be only the comparison of Ziwen fleeing salary or Gongyi declining meals!
translations.idiomatic: The people would sing your praise and historians your deed—far more than Ziwen refusing pay or Gongyi skipping meals!'

sentences.zh: 」[八]由不能用。

translations.literal: ' [eight] You did not adopt it.
translations.idiomatic: Song You ignored the advice.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]帑音它朗反。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Tang read ta-lang fan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: Tang is read ta-lang (fanqie).

sentences.zh: 注[二]晏晏，溫和也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Yanyan means mild and gentle.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Yanyan means mild and harmonious.

sentences.zh: 注[三]禮記曰：「歲凶，年谷不登，君膳不祭肺。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The Record of Rites says: 'In a bad year when grain does not ripen, the lord's feast omits the lung offering.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: The Book of Rites says that in famine years the ruler's meat offering is cut.'

sentences.zh: 」損服，減損服御。

translations.literal: ' Reduce garments means cut back wardrobe and equipage.
translations.idiomatic: Reducing garments means cutting court splendor.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]時西羌犯邊為害也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: At that time the western Qiang raided the border and did harm.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: The western Qiang were raiding the frontier.

sentences.zh: 注[五]臘賜大將軍、三公錢各二十萬，牛肉二百斤，粳米二百斛，特進、侯十五萬，卿十萬，校尉五萬，尚書三萬，侍中、將、大夫各二萬，千石、六百石各七千，虎賁、羽林郎二人共三千，以為祀門戶直。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: The La distribution gave the grand general and three dukes two hundred thousand cash each, two hundred jin of beef, two hundred hu of polished rice; specially advanced and marquises one hundred fifty thousand; ministers one hundred thousand; colonels fifty thousand; secretariat directors thirty thousand; attendants-in-ordinary, generals, and grandees twenty thousand each; officials at one thousand and six hundred bushels seven thousand each; pairs of tiger brave and feather guard gentlemen three thousand in all—for gate-guarding sacrifice.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: The Han Official Regulations spell out the year-end gifts in cash, beef, and grain down each rank for gate and household sacrifice.'

sentences.zh: 見漢官儀也。

translations.literal: See Han Official Regulations.
translations.idiomatic: See the Han Official Regulations.

sentences.zh: 注[六]尚書曰：「召公出取幣，入錫周公。」

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: The Documents says: 'The Duke of Shao went out to fetch the gifts and entered to present them to the Duke of Zhou.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 6: The Book of Documents describes the Duke of Shao fetching gifts for the Duke of Zhou.'

sentences.zh: 注[七]國語：「昔楚□子文三登令尹，無一日之積。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: Discourses of the States: 'Formerly Chu [Dou] Ziwen thrice rose to chief minister yet never had a day's surplus.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 7: The Discourses tell how Dou Ziwen of Chu thrice became chief minister without saving a day's grain.'

sentences.zh: 成王聞子文朝不及夕也，於是乎每朝設脯七束，糗一筐，以羞子文。

translations.literal: King Cheng heard that Ziwen went to court hungry by evening; thereupon each court day he set out seven bundles of dried meat and one basket of grain to supply Ziwen.
translations.idiomatic: King Cheng learned he came to court hungry and sent seven bundles of dried meat and a basket of grain each morning.'

sentences.zh: 成王每出子文之祿，必逃，王止而後復。

translations.literal: Whenever the king issued Ziwen's salary, he always fled; the king stopped him and then he returned.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever salary was issued, Ziwen fled until the king forced him to accept.'

sentences.zh: 人謂子文曰：『人生求富，子逃之，何也？

translations.literal: People said to Ziwen: 'Men seek wealth in life; you flee it—why?'
translations.idiomatic: People asked why he fled wealth.'

sentences.zh: 』對曰：『從政者，以庇人也。

translations.literal: ' He answered: 'Those who govern are meant to shelter others.
translations.idiomatic: He replied, 'Office exists to shelter the people.'

sentences.zh: 人多曠者而我取富焉，是勤人以自封也，死無日矣。

translations.literal: When many are destitute and I alone grow rich, I fatten myself on others' toil—death will soon follow.
translations.idiomatic: To enrich myself while the multitude starves is to invite my own end.'

sentences.zh: 我逃死，非逃富也。』」

translations.literal: I flee death, not wealth.' '
translations.idiomatic: I flee ruin, not salary.'

sentences.zh: 注[八]史記：「公儀休相魯，食茹而美，拔園葵而棄之，見布好而逐出其家婦，燔其機，雲『欲令農士女工安得奪其貨乎』？

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: Records of the Historian: 'Gongyi Xiu was chancellor of Lu; he found wild greens delicious, pulled up the garden mallow and cast it away; saw fine cloth and drove out his household wife, burned her loom, saying "I wish farmers and women workers to be able to sell their goods—how can I seize their goods?"'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 8: The Records tells how Gongyi Xiu of Lu refused to compete with farmers and weavers.'

sentences.zh: 」比音庇。

translations.literal: ' Bi read pi.
translations.idiomatic: Bi is read pi.

sentences.zh: 時齊殤王子都鄉侯暢奔吊國憂，上書未報，[一]侍中竇憲遂令人刺殺暢於城門屯□之中，[二]而主名不立。

translations.literal: At that time the prince of Qi, son of Shang, Marquis of Duxiang Liu Chang, had hurried to mourn the state's grief; his memorial was not yet answered, [one] when attendant Dou Xian had men assassinate Chang at the gate guard post, [two] and the principal name was not established.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Liu Chang of Duxiang came to mourn the late emperor; before his petition was answered [1], Dou Xian had him murdered at the city gate watchpost [2], and the culprit went unnamed.'

sentences.zh: 敞又說由曰：「劉暢宗室肺府，茅土藩臣，來吊大憂，上書須報，[三]親在武□，致此殘酷。

translations.literal: Chang again urged You: 'Liu Chang was a royal kinsman, flesh of the altars, a fief-holding prince; he came to mourn the great grief and awaited a reply to his memorial—[three] his kin were at the martial [capital], yet he met this cruelty.
translations.idiomatic: He Chang urged Song You: 'Liu Chang was imperial bone, a feudal prince mourning at court, waiting on a reply [3], yet he was cut down at the capital.'

sentences.zh: 奉憲之吏，莫適討捕，[四]蹤多不顯，主名不立。

translations.literal: The officers charged with Dou Xian's orders had no clear target for arrest; [four] traces were many but not clear, the principal name not established.
translations.idiomatic: Dou's agents had no one to charge [4]; clues multiplied but no culprit was named.'

sentences.zh: 敞備數股肱，職典賊曹，[五]故欲親至發所，以糾其變，而二府以為故事三公不與賊盜。

translations.literal: Chang, though only one among the limbs, holds the office of bandit-catcher, [five] therefore wished to go in person to the scene to investigate the affair, but the two bureaus cited precedent that the three dukes do not handle banditry.
translations.idiomatic: I am but a limb of your office yet hold the bandit section [5]; I wished to examine the scene myself, but the two bureaus cited the rule that the three dukes do not try theft.'

sentences.zh: [六]昔陳平生於征戰之世，猶知宰相之分，云『外鎮四夷，內撫諸侯，使卿大夫各得其宜』。

translations.literal: [six] Formerly Chen Ping, born in an age of war, still knew a chancellor's division of labor, saying 'Without he secures the four barbarians, within he comforts the lords, and lets ministers and grandees each suit their task.'
translations.idiomatic: [6] Even Chen Ping in wartime knew a chancellor's duty: 'Without, pacify the barbarians; within, settle the lords—let each officer fill his role.'

sentences.zh: [七]今二府執事不深惟大義，惑於所聞，公縱奸慝，莫以為咎。

translations.literal: [seven] Now the two bureaus' officers do not ponder the great pattern; misled by what they have heard, they let you indulge wickedness and think it no fault.
translations.idiomatic: [7] Your subordinates ignore the larger duty and let you wink at crime as harmless.'

sentences.zh: 惟明公運獨見之明，昭然勿疑，敞不勝所見，請獨奏案。

translations.literal: Only if the enlightened lord exercises singular insight, clear and without doubt—Chang cannot contain what he sees and begs to investigate alone on memorial.
translations.idiomatic: Only you can see clearly; I beg leave to pursue the case alone by memorial.'

sentences.zh: 」由乃許焉。

translations.literal: ' You then granted it.
translations.idiomatic: Song You agreed.'

sentences.zh: 二府聞敞行，皆遣主者隨之，[八]於是推舉具得事實，京師稱其正。

translations.literal: When the two bureaus heard Chang was going, they each sent supervisors to follow him; [eight] thereupon the investigation obtained the full truth and the capital praised his rectitude.
translations.idiomatic: Both bureaus sent men after him [8]; the truth came out and the capital praised his integrity.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]時章帝崩也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: It was when Emperor Zhang died.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: This was after Emperor Zhang's death.

sentences.zh: 殤王名石，齊武王演之孫也。

translations.literal: The prince of Shang was named Shi, grandson of Prince Wu of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Shang was named Shi, grandson of Prince Wu of Qi.

sentences.zh: 注[二]暢得幸竇太后，故刺殺之。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Chang had won Empress Dowager Dou's favor, therefore he was assassinated.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Liu Chang had won the empress dowager's favor, so Dou Xian had him killed.

sentences.zh: 注[三]須，待也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Xu means wait.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Xu means to await.

sentences.zh: 注[四]適音的。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Di read di.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: Di is read di.

sentences.zh: 謂無指的討捕也。

translations.literal: It means there was no clear target for arrest.
translations.idiomatic: That is, no definite culprit to seize.

sentences.zh: 注[五]股肱謂手臂也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Thigh and arm means arms and legs.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: 'Limb' means a trusted arm of the office.

sentences.zh: 公府有賊曹，主知盜賊也。

translations.literal: The duke's office has a bandit section in charge of knowing bandits.
translations.idiomatic: The high bureau has a bandit section that handles robbery.

sentences.zh: 注[六]敞在太尉府，二府謂司徒、司空。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Chang was in the grand commandant's office; the two bureaus mean minister of education and minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 6: He Chang served the grand commandant; the two bureaus are the ministers of education and works.

sentences.zh: 丙吉為丞相不案事，遂為故事，見馬防傳也。

translations.literal: Bing Ji as chancellor did not try cases, and it became precedent—see Ma Fang's biography.
translations.idiomatic: Bing Ji as chancellor refused to try cases, setting a precedent—see the biography of Ma Fang.

sentences.zh: 注[七]陳平為左丞相，對文帝曰：「宰相者，佐天子理陰陽，順四時，下育萬物之宜，外鎮撫四夷、諸侯，內親附百姓，使卿大夫各得任其職焉。」

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: Chen Ping as left chancellor said to Emperor Wen: 'The chancellor assists the Son of Heaven in ordering yin and yang, harmonizing the four seasons, nurturing the myriad things below, without pacifies and soothes the four barbarians and the feudal lords, within draws near the hundred surnames, and lets ministers and grandees each fulfill his duty.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 7: Chen Ping defined the chancellor's role as harmonizing heaven and earth, ordering the seasons, aiding all creatures, pacifying barbarians and lords abroad, and settling the people at home.'

sentences.zh: 注[八]主者謂主知之盜賊之曹也。

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: Supervisors means the clerks of the bandit-knowing section.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 8: The supervisors were clerks from the bandit section.

sentences.zh: 以高第拜侍御史。

translations.literal: By high grade he was appointed attendant censor.
translations.idiomatic: For high marks he was made attendant censor.

sentences.zh: 時遂以竇憲為車騎將軍，大發軍擊匈奴，而詔使者為憲弟篤、景並起邸第，興造勞役，百姓愁苦。

translations.literal: It happened that Dou Xian was named general of chariots and cavalry and a great army was raised against the Xiongnu, while edicts ordered envoys to build mansions for Xian's younger brothers Du and Jing, stirring corvée and toil until the people groaned.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Xian was made general of chariots and cavalry for a great northern campaign while edicts raised palaces for his brothers Dou Du and Dou Jing, driving the people to exhaustion.

sentences.zh: 敞上疏諫曰：「臣聞匈奴之為桀逆久矣。

translations.literal: Chang submitted a memorial remonstrating: 'Your servant has heard the Xiongnu have been violent rebels for long.
translations.idiomatic: He Chang memorialized: 'The Xiongnu have long been rebels.'

sentences.zh: 平城之圍，嫚書之恥，[一]此二辱者，臣子所為捐軀而必死，高祖、呂後忍怒還忿，捨而不誅。

translations.literal: The siege of Pingcheng, the insult of the lewd letter—[one] these two shames are what ministers would die to avenge; Gaozu and Empress Lü swallowed rage and stored wrath, spared them and did not punish.
translations.idiomatic: The siege at Pingcheng and Modun's lewd letter to Empress Lü [1] were insults ministers would die to avenge—yet Gaozu and the empress bore the shame.'

sentences.zh: 伏惟皇太后秉文母之操，[二]陛下履晏晏之姿，匈奴無逆節之罪，漢朝無可籩之恥，而盛春東作，[三]興動大役，元元怨恨，鹹懷不悅。

translations.literal: I reflect that Empress Dowager holds the conduct of King Wen's mother; [two] Your Majesty bears a mild countenance; the Xiongnu have committed no act of rebellion, the Han has no shame that cannot be borne—yet in the height of spring when eastern work begins, [three] you stir a great levy so the people nurse resentment and none are pleased.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager follows the pattern of Wen's mother [2]; you are gentle of mien; the Xiongnu have not rebelled and the Han need not blush—yet in spring when plowing should begin [3] you launch a vast campaign that embitters the realm.'

sentences.zh: 而猥復為□尉篤、奉車都尉景繕修館第，彌街絕裡。

translations.literal: And rashly again for Colonel Du and chief charioteer Jing you repair mansions, filling whole streets and lanes.
translations.idiomatic: You also rush to build mansions for Dou Du and Dou Jing that swallow whole blocks.'

sentences.zh: 臣雖斗筲之人，[四]誠竊懷怪，以為篤、景親近貴臣，當為百僚表儀。

translations.literal: Your servant, though a man of peck-and-bushel capacity, [four] truly harbors wonder, thinking Du and Jing are intimate honored ministers who ought to model the hundred offices.
translations.idiomatic: I am a man of small measure [4], yet I marvel: Dou Du and Dou Jing are your close ministers and should set the standard for every office.'

sentences.zh: 今觿軍在道，朝廷焦唇，百姓愁苦，縣官無用，而遽起大第，崇飾玩好，非所以垂令德，示無窮也。

translations.literal: Now the hosts are on the road, the court burns at the lips, the people groan, the treasury is empty—yet you suddenly raise great mansions and glorify trifles; this is not how to hand down fine virtue or show endless example.
translations.idiomatic: Armies march while the court frets, the people groan, and the treasury is bare—yet you raise vast houses and dangle baubles; that is no way to teach lasting virtue.'

sentences.zh: 宜且罷工匠，專憂北邊，恤人之困。

translations.literal: It is fitting for the time to halt craftsmen, turn care to the northern border, and pity men's distress.
translations.idiomatic: Stop the builders, worry about the northern frontier, and pity the people's plight.'

sentences.zh: 」書奏不省。

translations.literal: ' The memorial was tabled without response.
translations.idiomatic: The court ignored the memorial.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]匈奴冒頓以精兵三十萬騎，圍高帝於白登七日。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Modun of the Xiongnu with three hundred thousand picked horsemen besieged Gaodi at Baideng for seven days.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: Modun trapped Gaozu at Baideng for seven days with three hundred thousand horsemen.

sentences.zh: 案：白登在平城東南十餘里。

translations.literal: Note: Baideng lies a dozen li southeast of Pingcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Baideng is southeast of Pingcheng.

sentences.zh: 高後時，冒頓遺高後書曰：「陛下獨立，孤僨獨居，兩主不樂，無以自娛，願以所有，易其所無。

translations.literal: Under Empress Gao, Modun sent Empress Gao a letter saying: 'Your Majesty stands alone; I, the orphaned widower, dwell alone; neither ruler is glad and has nothing to amuse himself—I wish to exchange what I have for what you lack.
translations.idiomatic: Under Empress Gao, Modun wrote offering to trade pleasures with the widowed empress.'

sentences.zh: 」孤僨，冒頓自請。

translations.literal: ' Gu fen—Modun speaking of himself.
translations.idiomatic: Gu fen was Modun's self-reference.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]文母，文王之妻大姒也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Wen's mother is King Wen's wife Taisi.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Wen's mother is Taisi, wife of King Wen.

sentences.zh: 詩曰「既有烈考，亦有文母」也。

translations.literal: The Poetry says 'You had a blazing father; you also had a cultured mother.'
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Songs praises both a martial father and a cultured mother.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]歲起於東，人始就耕，故曰東作。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The year begins in the east; people first take up plowing—therefore called eastern work.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Spring work in the east means the season of plowing.

sentences.zh: 注[四]鄭玄注論語：「筲，竹器，容斗二升。」

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Zheng Xuan's commentary on the Analects: 'Shao is a bamboo vessel holding two pecks and two sheng.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: Zheng Xuan defines shao as a small bamboo measure.

sentences.zh: 後拜為尚書，復上封事曰：「夫忠臣憂世，犯主嚴顏，譏刺貴臣，至以殺身滅家而猶為之者，何邪？

translations.literal: Later he was appointed director of the secretariat and again submitted a sealed memorial: 'As for loyal ministers who grieve for the age, who offend the ruler's stern face, who satirize great officers—even unto death and family extinction yet do it—why?
translations.idiomatic: Later as secretariat director he sent a sealed memorial: 'Why do loyal ministers risk death to remonstrate?'

sentences.zh: 君臣義重，有不得已也。

translations.literal: The duty between lord and minister is weighty; there is what cannot be helped.
translations.idiomatic: Because the bond of ruler and minister leaves them no choice.'

sentences.zh: 臣伏見往事，國之危亂，家之將凶，皆有所由，較然易知。

translations.literal: Your servant has examined past affairs: the state's peril and chaos, the family's approaching ill omen, all have their causes—clear and easy to know.
translations.idiomatic: I have studied past crises: every fall of state or house had visible causes.'

sentences.zh: [一]昔鄭武姜之幸叔段，[二]□莊公之寵州吁，[三]愛而不教，終至凶戾。

translations.literal: [one] Formerly Lady Wu of Zheng favored Shuduan, [two] Duke Zhuang of Wei favored Zhouxu—loving yet not teaching, they ended in violence.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Lady Wu of Zheng spoiled Shuduan [2]; Duke Zhuang of Wei spoiled Zhouxu—love without teaching bred monsters.'

sentences.zh: 由是觀之，愛子若此，猶饑而食之以毒，適所以害之也。

translations.literal: From this viewing, to love a son thus is like feeding hunger with poison—it is precisely the means to harm him.
translations.idiomatic: To dote on a son that way is to feed him poison—only harm can follow.'

sentences.zh: [四]伏見大將軍憲，始遭大憂，公卿比奏，欲令典干國事。

translations.literal: [four] Your servant sees Grand General Dou Xian: at first encountering the great grief, dukes and ministers jointly memorialized, wishing to have him manage state affairs.
translations.idiomatic: [4] When the great mourning began, the ministers asked Dou Xian to manage affairs.'

sentences.zh: [五]憲深執謙退，固辭盛位，懇懇勤勤，言之深至，天下聞之，莫不悅喜。

translations.literal: [five] Xian deeply held modest withdrawal, firmly declined the exalted post, earnest and diligent, his words extremely thorough—the realm heard it and none were not glad.
translations.idiomatic: [5] He refused power with earnest words and the empire rejoiced.'

sentences.zh: 今踰年無幾，大禮未終，卒然中改，兄弟專朝。

translations.literal: Now not many years have passed; the great rites are not ended; suddenly the middle course is changed—the brothers monopolize court.
translations.idiomatic: Within a year, before mourning ended, everything changed—the brothers seized the government.'

sentences.zh: 憲秉三軍之重，篤、景總宮□之權，而虐用百姓，奢侈僭偪，誅戮無罪，肆心自快。

translations.literal: Xian holds the three armies' weight; Du and Jing total the palace gates' power; they cruelly use the hundred surnames, are extravagant beyond their rank, execute the guiltless, and follow their hearts for pleasure.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Xian commands the hosts; Dou Du and Dou Jing hold the inner palace; they oppress the people, spend beyond their rank, kill the innocent, and please themselves.'

sentences.zh: 今者論議凶凶，鹹謂叔段、州吁復生於漢。

translations.literal: Now discussion runs fierce; all say Shuduan and Zhouxu are reborn in Han.
translations.idiomatic: Men now whisper that Shuduan and Zhouxu walk again in the Han court.'

sentences.zh: 臣觀公卿懷持兩端，不肯極言者，以為憲等若有匪懈之志，則己受吉甫□申伯之功，[六]如憲等陷於罪辜，則自取陳平、周勃順呂後之權，[七]終不以憲等吉凶為憂也。

translations.literal: Your servant sees dukes and ministers who harbor two minds and will not speak plainly: they think that if Dou Xian and others should show untiring loyalty, then they themselves would receive the merit of Jifu [praising] Shenbo, [six] but if Xian and others sink into crime, then they would themselves take the power Chen Ping and Zhou Bo yielded to Empress Lü—[seven] in the end they do not grieve for Xian and others' good or ill.
translations.idiomatic: High ministers sit on the fence: if Dou Xian thrives they hope to share Jifu-style praise [6]; if he falls they mean to play Chen Ping and Zhou Bo to Empress Lü [7]—they care nothing for Dou's fate either way.'

sentences.zh: 臣敞區區，誠欲計策兩安，絕其撓撓，塞其涓涓，[八]上不欲令皇太后損文母之號，陛下有誓泉之譏，[九]下使憲等得長保其福佑。

translations.literal: Your servant Chang, petty though he is, truly wishes to plan so both sides are safe, cut off their wrangling, dam the trickle, [eight] above not wishing the empress dowager to lose the title of cultured mother, nor Your Majesty incur the reproach of the Yellow Springs oath, [nine] below letting Xian and others long preserve their blessing.
translations.idiomatic: I beg only to secure both throne and kin, stop faction before it swells [8], spare the empress dowager her good name and you the shame of Zheng Zhuang's oath [9], and let the Dou brothers keep their fortune.'

sentences.zh: 然臧獲之謀，上安主父，下存主母，猶不免於嚴怒。

translations.literal: Yet the counsel of bondsmen—above saving the father, below saving the mother—still did not escape fierce anger.
translations.idiomatic: Even a slave-girl's stratagem that saved both master and mistress earned a beating.'

sentences.zh: [一０]臣伏惟累祖蒙恩，至臣八世，[一一]復以愚陋，旬年之閒，歷顯位，備機近，每念厚德，忽然忘生。

translations.literal: [ten] Your servant reflects that successive ancestors received favor down to your servant's eighth generation; [eleven] again by stupidity and coarseness, within a decade's span I have risen to exalted posts and stood near the inner secrets—each time I ponder the deep grace I forget my life.
translations.idiomatic: [10] My line has enjoyed imperial favor for eight generations [11]; in a few short years I rose to the inner court and each time I recall that grace I would gladly die.'

sentences.zh: 雖知言必夷滅，而冒死自盡者，誠不忍目見其禍而懷默苟全。

translations.literal: Though I know speech must mean extinction, yet braving death to exhaust loyalty—truly I cannot bear to watch the disaster with eyes open and hug silence to save myself whole.
translations.idiomatic: I know frank words may destroy me, yet I cannot stand silent while ruin approaches.'

sentences.zh: 駙馬都尉纓，雖在弱冠，有不隱之忠，比請退身，願抑家權。

translations.literal: Chief of cavalry attendant Kui, though still in the weak-crown years, has undisguised loyalty; repeatedly he has asked to withdraw and wishes to check his family's power.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Kui, chief of cavalry for the princess-consort, though young, is loyal: he has asked to retire and curb his clan's power.'

sentences.zh: 可與參謀，聽順其意，誠宗廟至計，竇氏之福。

translations.literal: He may be consulted; heed his intent—this is truly the supreme plan for the altars and the Dou family's fortune.
translations.idiomatic: Hear him out—it is the best policy for state and Dou alike.'

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]較，明。

translations.literal: ' Commentary [one]: Jiao means clear.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: Jiao means clear or evident.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]左傳，鄭武姜愛少子叔段，莊公立，武姜請以京封叔段，謂之京城大叔，後武姜引以襲鄭。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Zuo Tradition: Lady Wu of Zheng loved her younger son Shuduan; when Duke Zhuang succeeded, Lady Wu asked to enfeoff Shuduan at Jing, calling him the great lord of the capital; later Lady Wu led him to raid Zheng.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: The Zuo tells how Lady Wu of Zheng favored Shuduan and helped him rebel.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]左傳，□莊公寵庶子州吁，州吁好兵，公不禁。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Zuo Tradition: Duke Zhuang of Wei favored his bastard son Zhouxu; Zhouxu loved warfare and the duke did not restrain him.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Duke Zhuang of Wei spoiled Zhouxu and let him love arms.'

sentences.zh: 大夫石碏諫曰：「臣聞愛子教之以義方，弗納於邪。

translations.literal: Grandee Shi Que remonstrated: 'Your servant has heard that to love a son is to teach him with the square of righteousness, not to admit him into evil.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Que warned, 'Love a son by teaching duty, not by indulging vice.'

sentences.zh: 」莊公不從。

translations.literal: ' Duke Zhuang did not follow.
translations.idiomatic: The duke refused.'

sentences.zh: 及卒，適子桓公立，州吁乃殺桓公而篡其位。

translations.literal: When he died, the legitimate son Duke Huan succeeded; Zhouxu then killed Duke Huan and usurped his place.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhuang died, Zhouxu murdered Duke Huan and seized the throne.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]史記蘇秦曰：「饑人之所以饑而不食烏喙，為其愈充腹而與餓死同患也。」

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Su Qin in the Records says: 'A starving man will not eat aconite root to fill his belly, for though it fills the belly it brings the same harm as starving to death.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: Su Qin compared poisoned food to fatal favor.'

sentences.zh: 注[五]比，頻也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Bi means frequent.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 5: Bi means repeatedly.

sentences.zh: 干，主也。

translations.literal: Gan means preside / intrude.
translations.idiomatic: Gan here means to meddle in.

sentences.zh: 注[六]申伯，周宣王元舅也，有令德，故尹吉甫作頌以美之。
