translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Shenbo was King Xuan of Zhou's eldest maternal uncle by the same mother; he had fine virtue, therefore Yin Jifu made a hymn praising him.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 6: Shenbo was King Xuan's uncle; Jifu sang his praises.'

sentences.zh: 其詩曰：「維岳降神，生甫及申。

translations.literal: Its poem says: 'The high peak sent down its spirit; it bore Fu and Shen.
translations.idiomatic: The ode runs, 'The sacred peak sent down spirits; it bore Fu and Shen.'

sentences.zh: 申伯之德，柔惠且直。

translations.literal: Shenbo's virtue—gentle, kind, and straight.
translations.idiomatic: Shenbo's virtue was gentle, kind, and upright.'

sentences.zh: 揉此萬邦，聞於四國。」

translations.literal: He harmonized these myriad states, heard in the four realms.'
translations.idiomatic: He brought peace to the states and his fame reached the four quarters.'

sentences.zh: 注[七]呂後欲封呂祿﹑呂產為王，王陵諫不許，陳平﹑周勃順旨而封之。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: Empress Lü wished to enfeoff Lü Lu and Lü Chan as kings; Wang Ling remonstrated and was not permitted; Chen Ping and Zhou Bo followed her intent and enfeoffed them.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 7: Empress Lü wanted the Lü ennobled; Wang Ling opposed but Chen Ping and Zhou Bo acquiesced.'

sentences.zh: 呂後崩，平﹑勃合謀，卒誅產﹑祿也。

translations.literal: When Empress Lü died, Ping and Bo joined in plot and finally executed Chan and Lu.
translations.idiomatic: After her death Chen Ping and Zhou Bo destroyed the Lü clan.'

sentences.zh: 注[八]周金人銘曰「涓涓不壅，終為江河；

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: The Zhou metal-man inscription says: 'If trickles are not dammed, they end as rivers and seas;'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 8: A bronze inscription warns that small streams become floods.'

sentences.zh: 綿綿不絕，或成網羅」也。

translations.literal: if silken threads are not cut, they may become nets.'
translations.idiomatic: Unchecked threads become snares.'

sentences.zh: 注[九]左傳，鄭武姜引大叔段襲莊公，莊公寘姜氏於城穎，誓之曰：「不及黃泉，無相見也。」

translations.literal: Commentary [nine]: Zuo Tradition: Lady Wu of Zheng led Great Lord Duan to raid Duke Zhuang; Zhuang placed Lady Jiang at Yingcheng and swore: 'Until we reach the yellow springs, we shall not meet.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 9: The Zuo cites Duke Zhuang's oath to his mother after Duan's revolt.'

sentences.zh: 注[一０]方言：「臧獲，奴婢賤稱也。

translations.literal: Commentary [ten]: Fangyan: 'Zanghuo is a low term for male and female slaves.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 10: Fangyan glosses zanghuo as bondservants.'

sentences.zh: 」史記曰：「蘇秦謂燕王曰：『客有遠為吏，其妻私人。

translations.literal: ' The Records says: 'Su Qin said to the king of Yan: A guest served far as an official; his wife took a lover.
translations.idiomatic: The Records tells Su Qin's parable of the adulterous wife.'

sentences.zh: 其夫將來，私者憂之，妻曰：「勿憂，吾已為作藥酒待之矣。

translations.literal: When her husband was about to return, the lover worried; the wife said: Do not worry—I have brewed medicinal wine to await him.
translations.idiomatic: The wife brewed poisoned wine for her returning husband.'

sentences.zh: 」居三日，其夫果至，妻使妾舉藥酒而進之。

translations.literal: ' After three days the husband indeed arrived; the wife had a concubine raise the medicinal wine and present it.
translations.idiomatic: Three days later the husband came; she had a maid serve the wine.'

sentences.zh: 妾欲言酒之藥乎，則恐逐其主母也；

translations.literal: The concubine wished to say the wine was poisoned, then feared expelling her mistress;
translations.idiomatic: The maid wanted to warn of poison but feared betraying her mistress;'

sentences.zh: 欲勿言邪，則恐殺其主父。

translations.literal: wished not to speak, then feared killing her master.
translations.idiomatic: to stay silent meant killing her master;'

sentences.zh: 於是佯僵而棄酒。

translations.literal: Thereupon she feigned collapse and spilled the wine.
translations.idiomatic: she feigned a fit and spilled the cup.'

sentences.zh: 主父怒，笞之。

translations.literal: The master was angry and flogged her.
translations.idiomatic: The master beat her.'

sentences.zh: 故妾僵而覆酒，上存主父，下存主母，然猶不免於笞。』」

translations.literal: Thus the concubine, collapsing and overturning the wine, above saved the master, below saved the mistress, yet still did not escape flogging.' '
translations.idiomatic: She saved both yet was whipped all the same.'

sentences.zh: 注[一一]東觀記曰，何修生成，為漢膠東相；

translations.literal: Commentary [eleven]: Eastern Han Records says: He Xiu begat Cheng, who was chancellor of Jiaodong for Han;
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 11: The Eastern Han Records gives the He lineage from Xiu to Chang.'

sentences.zh: 成生果，為太中大夫；

translations.literal: Cheng begat Guo, who was grand palace grandee;
translations.idiomatic: Cheng begat Guo, grand palace grandee;'

sentences.zh: 果生比干，為丹陽都尉；

translations.literal: Guo begat Bigan, who was commandant of cavalry in Danyang;
translations.idiomatic: Guo begat Bigan, Danyang commandant;'

sentences.zh: 比干生壽，蜀郡太守；

translations.literal: Bigan begat Shou, grand administrator of Shu;
translations.idiomatic: Bigan begat Shou, Shu grand administrator;'

sentences.zh: 壽生顯，京輔都尉；

translations.literal: Shou begat Xian, metropolitan commandant of the capital region;
translations.idiomatic: Shou begat Xian, capital commandant;'

sentences.zh: 顯生鄢，光祿大夫；

translations.literal: Xian begat Yan, grandee of brilliant fortune;
translations.idiomatic: Xian begat Yan, grandee of brilliant fortune;'

sentences.zh: 鄢生寵，濟南都尉；

translations.literal: Yan begat Chong, commandant of cavalry in Jinan;
translations.idiomatic: Yan begat Chong, Jinan commandant;'

sentences.zh: 寵生敞：八世也。

translations.literal: Chong begat Chang—eight generations.'
translations.idiomatic: Chong begat He Chang—the eighth generation.'

sentences.zh: 敞數切諫，言諸竇罪過，憲等深怨之。

translations.literal: Chang repeatedly remonstrated sharply, stating the Dou clans' faults; Xian and others deeply resented him.
translations.idiomatic: He Chang's blunt attacks on the Dou enraged them.

sentences.zh: 時濟南王康尊貴驕甚，[一]憲乃白出敞為濟南太傅。

translations.literal: At that time Prince Kang of Jinan was honored and extremely arrogant; [one] Xian therefore had him moved out as grand tutor to Kang.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Prince Kang of Jinan was haughty; Dou Xian had He Chang sent as his grand tutor.'

sentences.zh: 敞至國，輔康以道義，數引法度諫正之，康敬禮焉。

translations.literal: When Chang reached the state, he assisted Kang with the Way and duty, repeatedly citing law to remonstrate and correct him; Kang treated him with respect.
translations.idiomatic: He Chang guided Kang with principle and law; the prince honored him.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]康，光武少子也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Kang was Emperor Guangwu's youngest son.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: Kang was Guangwu's youngest son.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，遷汝南太守。

translations.literal: After more than a year he was transferred to grand administrator of Runan.
translations.idiomatic: A year later he became grand administrator of Runan.

sentences.zh: 敞疾文俗吏以苛刻求當時名譽，故在職以寬和為政。

translations.literal: Chang hated vulgar clerks who sought contemporary fame through harshness; therefore in office he governed with lenience and harmony.
translations.idiomatic: He despised petty officials who built reputations on cruelty and ruled Runan with kindness.

sentences.zh: 立春日，常召督郵還府，[一]分遣儒術大吏案行屬縣，顯孝悌有義行者。

translations.literal: On the day of Beginning of Spring he always summoned supervising secretaries back to headquarters, [one] separately sending Confucian senior clerks to tour the subordinate counties and display filial piety and brotherhood and men who practiced righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Each spring he recalled the supervising secretaries [1] and sent learned clerks to tour the counties and honor the filial and righteous.'

sentences.zh: 及舉冤獄，以春秋義斷之。

translations.literal: When he raised wronged prisoners, he judged them by the Spring and Autumn's meaning.
translations.idiomatic: He cleared unjust jails with principles drawn from the Annals.'

sentences.zh: 是以郡中無怨聲，百姓化其恩禮。

translations.literal: Therefore in the commandery there was no sound of complaint; the hundred surnames were transformed by his grace and ritual.
translations.idiomatic: The commandery fell quiet; the people responded to his grace.

sentences.zh: 其出居者，皆歸養其父母，追行喪服，[二]推財相讓者二百許人。

translations.literal: Those who had lived apart all returned to support their parents and retroactively observed mourning; [two] those who pushed wealth aside and yielded to one another were about two hundred.
translations.idiomatic: Families reunited to care for parents and observe belated mourning [2]; some two hundred households yielded property to neighbors.'

sentences.zh: [三]置立禮官，不任文吏。

translations.literal: [three] He established ritual officers and did not rely on mere clerks.
translations.idiomatic: [3] He appointed ritual specialists instead of leaving rites to clerks.'

sentences.zh: 又修理鮦陽舊渠，百姓賴其利，[四]墾田增三萬餘頃。

translations.literal: He also repaired the old Tongyang canal; the hundred surnames relied on its benefit, [four] opening fields and adding more than thirty thousand qing.
translations.idiomatic: He restored the Tongyang canal so the people profited [4] and opened over thirty thousand qing of new land.'

sentences.zh: 吏人共刻石，頌敞功德。

translations.literal: Clerks and people together carved stone praising Chang's merit and virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Officials and commoners carved a stele praising his rule.

sentences.zh: 注[一]督郵主司察愆過，立春陽氣發生，故召歸。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Supervising secretaries oversee faults; when Beginning of Spring yang qi rises, therefore he summoned them home.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: Supervising secretaries inspect wrongdoing; spring recall was a humane custom.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]出居謂與父母別居者。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: 'Living apart' means those who lived separately from parents.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Those who had left their parents' homes.'

sentences.zh: 其親先亡者自恨喪禮不足，追行喪制也。

translations.literal: Those whose parents had died earlier regretted insufficient mourning rites and retroactively performed mourning.
translations.idiomatic: Some mourned late for parents they had neglected.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]東觀記曰：「高譚等百八十五人推財相讓。」

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Eastern Han Records says: 'Gao Tan and 185 others pushed wealth aside and yielded to one another.'
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: One hundred eighty-five men yielded property, says the Eastern Han Records.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]鮦陽，縣，屬汝南郡，故城在今豫州新蔡縣北。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Tongyang is a county in Runan commandery; the old city is north of Xincai in modern Yuzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: Tongyang lay in Runan, north of present Xincai.'

sentences.zh: 水經注云：「葛陂東出為鮦水，俗謂之三丈陂。」

translations.literal: The Shuijingzhu says: 'East of Ge marsh emerges Tong water, vulgarly called the Three-Fathom dyke.'
translations.idiomatic: The Shuijingzhu identifies the canal east of Ge marsh.'

sentences.zh: 論曰：永元之際，天子幼弱，太后臨朝，竇氏憑盛戚之權，將有呂﹑霍之變。

translations.literal: The treatise says: At the turn of the Yongyuan era the Son of Heaven was young and weak, the empress dowager held court, and the Dou clan, leaning on overwhelming kin power, was heading for a Lü or Huo coup.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's judgment: In Yongyuan the boy emperor and Dou regency threatened a Lü or Huo-style seizure.'

sentences.zh: [一] 幸漢德未衰，大臣方忠，袁﹑任二公正色立朝，[二]樂﹑何之徒抗議柱下，[三] 故能挾幼主*[之]*斷，剿奸回之偪。

translations.literal: [one] Fortunately Han virtue was not yet spent and great ministers were loyal; Yuan and Ren, the two dukes, stood in court with stern countenance, [two] while men like Yue and He remonstrated at the pillar base, [three] therefore they could support the young lord *[in]* judgment and cut off treacherous coercion.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Han virtue still held and loyal ministers stood firm: Yuan An and Ren Kui faced the court [2], Yue Hui and He Chang spoke from the censor's place [3], and together they steadied the boy emperor and broke the Dou grip.'

sentences.zh: [四]不然，國家危矣。

translations.literal: [four] Had it not been so, the state would have been in peril.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Otherwise the dynasty would have fallen.'

sentences.zh: 夫竇氏之閒，唯何敞可以免，而特以子失交之故廢黜，不顯大位。

translations.literal: Among the Dou affair only He Chang could have escaped harm, yet solely because of a son's broken friendship he was dismissed and did not reach high office.
translations.idiomatic: He Chang alone might have survived the Dou purge, yet he lost high rank over a son's quarrel with a friend.'

sentences.zh: 惜乎，過矣哉！

translations.literal: Alas—how excessive the fault!
translations.idiomatic: A pity—and how harsh the irony!'

sentences.zh: 注[一]呂祿、呂產也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Lü Lu and Lü Chan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 1: The Lü partisans Lu and Chan.

sentences.zh: 霍光之子禹。

translations.literal: The son of Huo Guang named Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang's son Huo Yu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]袁安、任隗也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Yuan An and Ren Kui.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Yuan An and Ren Kui.

sentences.zh: 注[三]漢官儀曰：「侍御史，周官也，為柱下史，冠法冠。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Han Official Regulations says: 'Attendant censor was a Zhou office, pillar-base scribe, wearing the law cap.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 3: Attendant censors were pillar-base scribes under Zhou precedent.'

sentences.zh: 」案禮圖注云：「法冠，執法者服之。

translations.literal: ' Note to ritual diagrams says: 'The law cap is worn by those who enforce law.
translations.idiomatic: The law cap was the enforcer's headgear.'

sentences.zh: 」樂恢為司隸，何敞為御史，並彈射糾察之官也。

translations.literal: ' Yue Hui was metropolitan commandant, He Chang was censor—both were impeaching and inspecting officers.
translations.idiomatic: Yue Hui as metropolitan commandant and He Chang as censor both held investigative posts.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]剿，絕也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Jiao means cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 4: Jiao means to extirpate.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：朱生受寄，誠不愆義。

translations.literal: In praise: Zhu Sheng received a trust and truly did not betray duty.
translations.idiomatic: Praise: Zhu Hui kept a friend's trust without fault.

sentences.zh: 公叔辟梁，允納明刺。

translations.literal: Gongshu avoided Liang yet welcomed plain reproof.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Mu shunned Liang Ji but accepted frank counsel.'

sentences.zh: 絕交面朋，崇厚浮偽。

translations.literal: Severing ties with fair-weather friends, honoring thickness against hollow sham.
translations.idiomatic: He renounced hollow friendships and honored true substance.'

sentences.zh: [一] 恢舉謗己，敞非祥瑞。

translations.literal: [one] Hui raised a man who had slandered him; Chang rejected false omens.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Yue Hui recommended his detractor; He Chang dismissed fake prodigies.'

sentences.zh: 永言國偪，甘心強詖。

translations.literal: Ever speaking of the state's peril, he gladly faced the powerful and sycophantic.
translations.idiomatic: Both spoke truth to peril and defied the mighty.'

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]楊雄法言曰：「朋而不心，面朋也。

translations.literal: [two] Yang Xiong's Model Sayings says: 'Companion without heart—surface companion.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Yang Xiong said, 'Friends without inward truth are friends in face only.'

sentences.zh: 友而不心，面友也。

translations.literal: Friend without heart—surface friend.
translations.idiomatic: Friends without heart are friends in name only.'

sentences.zh: 」浮偽者，勸之以崇厚也。

translations.literal: ' 'Hollow sham' urges them toward honoring thickness.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss urges readers toward substance over show.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]詖，佞諂也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Bi means flattering sycophancy.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary 2: Bi means flattery.

sentences.zh: 竇憲兄弟奢僭上偪，敞冒死切諫，是甘心於強詖之人也。

translations.literal: The Dou brothers' extravagance overstepped and pressed upward; Chang braved death to remonstrate sharply—this was willingly facing the powerful sycophants.
translations.idiomatic: He Chang risked death remonstrating against the Dou brothers' arrogance—that was courage before flatterers.'

sentences.zh: 校勘記

translations.literal: Collation notes
translations.idiomatic: Collation notes

sentences.zh: 一四五七頁三行朱暉字文季袁宏紀作「文秀」。

translations.literal: Page 1457 line 3: Zhu Hui's courtesy name Wenji—in Yuan Hong's annals written 'Wenxiu.'
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Yuan Hong records Zhu Hui's style as Wenxiu.'

sentences.zh: 按：下云「強直自遂，南陽朱季」，則作「文季」是。

translations.literal: Note: Below it says 'firm and straight, Zhu Ji of Nanyang'—therefore Wenji is correct.
translations.idiomatic: The text favors Wenji over Wenxiu.'

sentences.zh: 一四五七頁一二行太守阮況嘗欲巿暉*

translations.literal: Page 1457 line 12: Grand Administrator Ruan Kuang wished to buy from Hui *
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ruan Kuang wished to buy from Zhu Hui

translations.literal: (ox)
translations.idiomatic: (ox)

sentences.zh: **[婢]*據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: **[maidservant]* according to Ji and Palace editions changed.
translations.idiomatic: and a maidservant per Ji and Palace editions.'

sentences.zh: 按：注引東觀記「欲買暉婢」，則作「婢」是。

translations.literal: Note: The commentary quotes Eastern Han Records 'wished to buy Hui's maidservant'—therefore maidservant is correct.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary quotes the Eastern Han Records as wishing to buy Zhu Hui's maidservant, so the reading maidservant is correct.

sentences.zh: 一四五八頁三行是時陰就為府卿按：御覽八０六引「府卿」作「少府卿」。

translations.literal: Page 1458 line 3: At that time Yin Jiu was 'fu qing'—Imperial Readings 806 cites 'privy treasurer' instead.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Taiping yulan reads privy treasurer for fu qing.'

sentences.zh: 一四五八頁九行暉為*

translations.literal: Page 1458 line 9: Hui was *
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Zhu Hui was

translations.literal: (clerk)
translations.idiomatic: (clerk)

sentences.zh: *督郵據汲本、殿本刪。

translations.literal: *supervising secretary deleted per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: The words supervising secretary are omitted in Ji and Palace.'

sentences.zh: 按：聚珍本東觀記作「暉為郡督郵」。

translations.literal: Note: The rare Eastern Han Records reads 'Hui was commandery supervising secretary.'
translations.idiomatic: A variant reads commandery supervising secretary.'

sentences.zh: 一四五八頁一四行臣觀大王無償趙城色汲本、殿本「無」下有「意」字，「色」作「邑」。

translations.literal: Page 1458 line 14: Your servant observes the great king has no intent to compensate Zhao's cities—Ji and Palace add 'intent' and read yi for se.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: The Records reads 'no intent to yield Zhao's towns.'

sentences.zh: 今按：史記作「臣觀大王無意償趙王城邑」。

translations.literal: Now checked: Records reads 'Your servant observes the great king has no intent to compensate King of Zhao's towns.'
translations.idiomatic: The Shiji line uses yi and yi (towns).

sentences.zh: 一四六０頁六行居儉難之時汲本、殿本「儉」作「險」。

translations.literal: Page 1460 line 6: Dwelling in straitened difficulty's time—Ji and Palace read 'danger' for 'straitened.'
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Some editions read xian (danger) for jian.'

sentences.zh: 按：易否卦「君子以儉德辟難」，為此語所本。

translations.literal: Note: The Changes' Pi hexagram says 'The gentleman with straitened virtue avoids difficulty'—this is the source.
translations.idiomatic: The line echoes the Changes Pi hexagram.'

sentences.zh: 儉與險通。

translations.literal: Jian interchanges with xian.
translations.idiomatic: Jian and xian are used interchangeably here.'

sentences.zh: 一四六二頁一五行嚴鮪謀立清河王蒜按：集解引沉宇說，謂清河王、李固、杜喬傳皆作「劉鮪」。

translations.literal: Page 1462 line 15: Yan Wei plotted to set up Prince Suan of Qinghe—Collected Explanations cites Shen Yu: the biographies of Prince Qinghe, Li Gu, and Du Qiao all read Liu Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Shen Yu notes the conspirator should read Liu Wei, not Yan Wei, matching parallel texts.

sentences.zh: 一四六三頁四行郡中瞻望明府謂如仲尼非顏回不敢以迎孔子按：汲本、殿本「謂」字在「非顏回」上。

translations.literal: Page 1463 line 4: The commandery looks up to the enlightened prefect as to Confucius—only Yan Hui would dare meet Confucius—note: Ji and Palace editions place wei before fei Yan Hui.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji and Palace editions move the graph wei to a different position in the sentence.

sentences.zh: 一四六五頁一一行此老子*[道]*德經之詞也據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1465 line 11: this Laozi classic wording—supplemented per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: The text dao was restored from Ji and Palace editions.

sentences.zh: 一四六五頁一二行行*[之]*有失據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1465 line 12: conduct having fault—supplemented per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: The graph zhi was restored in xing zhi you shi.

sentences.zh: 一四六六頁一０行*

translations.literal: Page 1466 line 10: *
translations.idiomatic: Collation fragment:

translations.literal: (Martial)
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu

sentences.zh: **[景]*帝王皇后據陳景雲說改。

translations.literal: **[Jing]* emperor empress—emended per Chen Jingyun.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Emended per Chen Jingyun to the Jing emperor's empress.

sentences.zh: 一四六七頁六行然*[後]*知薄者之不足刊誤謂案文「然」字下不可少「後」字，明脫之。

translations.literal: Page 1467 line 6: only then knowing the thin's insufficiency—Errata says ran cannot lack hou below; clearly dropped.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Errata requires hou after ran; the graph was missing.

sentences.zh: 今據補。

translations.literal: Now supplemented accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Supplied in modern editions.

sentences.zh: 一四六七頁一四行世之務交遊也久矣敦千乘不忌於君按：御覽四一０引作「世之務交遊也甚矣，不惇於業，不忌於君」。

translations.literal: Page 1467 line 14: the age's pursuit of socializing has been long—sincerely with a thousand chariots not fearing toward the lord—note: Imperial Readings 410 cites 'the age's pursuit of socializing is extreme; not earnest in duty, not fearing toward the lord.'
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Taiping yulan gives a longer variant of Zhu Mu's sentence.

sentences.zh: 一四六八頁五行我豈足下部*[民]*據汲本補。

translations.literal: Page 1468 line 5: how could I be your subordinate *[people]*—supplemented per Ji edition.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: The graph min after bu was restored from Ji.

sentences.zh: 按：刊誤謂「部」下應有「民」字。

translations.literal: Note: Errata says min should follow bu in bu xia.
translations.idiomatic: Errata notes min missing after department.

sentences.zh: 一四六九頁一行馬免之徒按：集解引惠棟說，謂蔣杲雲帝紀「免」作「勉」。

translations.literal: Page 1469 line 1: followers of Ma Mian—note: Collected Explanations cites Hui Dong: Jiang Gao's Annals writes mian as mian (homophone variant).
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Some texts read Ma Mian as Ma Mian with a different graph.

sentences.zh: 一四七０頁八行漂害人庶數十萬戶按：校補引錢大昭說，謂續漢五行志注引此傳作「數千萬戶」。

translations.literal: Page 1470 line 8: drifted and harmed commoners in several hundred thousand households—note: Collation supplement cites Qian Dazhao: the Continued Han Treatise on Five Phases cites this passage as several ten million households.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: A variant gives a far larger household count for the flood victims.

sentences.zh: 一四七０頁一０行奏劾諸郡按：汲本、殿本「郡」作「部」。

translations.literal: Page 1470 line 10: memorial-impeached various commanderies—note: Ji and Palace read bu for jun.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji and Palace read department instead of commandery.

sentences.zh: 一四七二頁三行系趾謂釱其足也以鐵著足曰釱也按：兩「釱」字原並斗「□」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1472 line 3: fettering toes means shackling the feet; attaching iron to the feet is called zhi—note: both zhi characters were corrupted to lacunae and are emended here.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Two corrupted zhi graphs were restored.

sentences.zh: 一四七三頁七行追贈益州太守集解引沉欽韓說，謂袁紀作「益州刺史」為是。

translations.literal: Page 1473 line 7: posthumously enfeoffed grand administrator of Yizhou—Collected Explanations cites Shen Qinhan: Yuan Hong's Annals reads inspector of Yizhou, which is correct.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Shen Qinhan prefers posthumous title inspector over grand administrator.

sentences.zh: 按：校補謂蔡邕朱公叔碑首云「忠文公益州太守朱君」，則固可為贈太守之一證。

translations.literal: Note: Collation supplement says Cai Yong's stele for Zhu opens 'Loyal and cultured lord Zhu, grand administrator of Yizhou'—so posthumous grand administrator is supported.
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong's stele supports the grand reading.

sentences.zh: 漢制刺史雖巡行所部各郡，以六條問事，而秩僅六百石，遠不逮太守，故太守轉為刺史遷途，贈官亦例以太守為重也。

translations.literal: Han regulations: though inspectors toured commanderies on the six articles, rank was only six hundred bushels, far below grand administrators; therefore promotion from grand administrator to inspector was normal, and posthumous gifts likewise favored grand administrator.
translations.idiomatic: Note explains why posthumous rank used grand administrator rather than inspector.

sentences.zh: 一四七五頁四行否則止按：刊誤謂「否」當作「不可」。

translations.literal: Page 1475 line 4: otherwise stop—note: Errata says fou should read bu ke.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Errata emends fou to bu ke.

sentences.zh: 一四七五頁六行莫之能改也按：殿本無「能」字，王先謙謂無「能」字是。

translations.literal: Page 1475 line 6: none can alter it—note: Palace edition lacks neng; Wang Xianqian says omission of neng is correct.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Wang Xianqian prefers the text without neng.

sentences.zh: 一四七五頁一一行走將從夫孤焉按：「夫」原斗「失」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1475 line 11: I would hasten to follow that isolation—note: fu was corrupted to shi and is emended here.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: fu was miswritten as shi.

sentences.zh: 一四七六頁三行*

translations.literal: Page 1476 line 3: *
translations.idiomatic: Collation:

translations.literal: (Martial)
translations.idiomatic: Martial

sentences.zh: **[景]*帝王皇后據陳景雲說改。

translations.literal: **[Jing]* emperor empress—emended per Chen Jingyun.
translations.idiomatic: Same emendation as above for the empress line.

sentences.zh: 一四七六頁一三行為大梁夷門門者按：汲本、殿本下「門」字作「監」。

translations.literal: Page 1476 line 13: as gatekeeper of Daliang's Yimen gate—note: Ji and Palace read overseer for the second men.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Second men read jian in some editions.

sentences.zh: 一四七七頁一行恢年十一按：校補引柳從辰說，謂袁宏紀「一」作「二」。

translations.literal: Page 1477 line 1: Hui age eleven—note: Collation supplement cites Liu Congchen: Yuan Hong's Annals writes two.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Yuan Hong gives Yue Hui's age as twelve.

sentences.zh: 一四七七頁三行事博士焦永按：集解引惠棟說，謂袁宏紀作「焦貺」。

translations.literal: Page 1477 line 3: served Erudite Jiao Yong—note: Collected Explanations cites Hui Dong: Yuan Hong writes Jiao Huang.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Some texts read Jiao Huang instead of Jiao Yong.

sentences.zh: 案鄭宏傳，宏師河東太守焦貺，袁紀稱貺嘗為博士，後為河東太守，則「永」當為「貺」也。

translations.literal: Case: Zheng Hong's biography says Hong studied under grand administrator of Hedong Jiao Huang; Yuan Hong says Huang was once erudite then Hedong grand administrator—therefore Yong should be Huang.
translations.idiomatic: Scholars identify Yong as Huang from Zheng Hong's biography.

sentences.zh: 一四七七頁四行恢獨*

translations.literal: Page 1477 line 4: Hui alone *
translations.idiomatic: Collation on jiao:

translations.literal: (bright)
translations.idiomatic: bright

sentences.zh: **[曒]*然不污於法據殿本改，注同。

translations.literal: **[jiao]*ran not stained by law—emended per Palace edition; commentary likewise.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Palace reads wu for a corrupted graph in not stained by law.

sentences.zh: 一四七七頁一０行同郡楊政按：校補引柳從辰說，謂袁紀作「杜陵人楊正」。

translations.literal: Page 1477 line 10: Yang Zheng of same commandery—note: Collation supplement cites Liu Congchen: Yuan Hong reads Yang Zheng of Duling.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Yuan Hong gives a different name and origin for Yang.

sentences.zh: 一四七八頁二行干人主以窺覦按：「覦」原斗「踰」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 1478 line 2: tugging at the sovereign's sleeve from ambition—note: yu was corrupted to yu (step) and is emended per Ji and Palace.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Graph for covet was corrected.

sentences.zh: 一四七八頁三行年十三入太學按：集解引沉欽韓說，謂書鈔引先賢行狀作「年十五」。

translations.literal: Page 1478 line 3: age thirteen entered Imperial Academy—note: Collected Explanations cites Shen Qinhan: Shuchao cites Exemplary Conduct at fifteen.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Some sources give fifteen for Du An's entry to the academy.

sentences.zh: 一四七八頁一一行觿物夭傷按：汲本「夭」作「大」。

translations.literal: Page 1478 line 11: myriad things wither young—note: Ji reads da for yao.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji edition reads great harm instead of premature death.

sentences.zh: 一四七八頁一四行成陽高鳳集解引錢大昕說，謂案逸民傳，高鳳南陽葉人，此「成陽」恐是「南陽」之鬥，或別有同姓名者。

translations.literal: Page 1478 line 14: Chengyang Gao Feng—Collected Explanations cites Qian Daxin: the Recluses biography places Gao Feng as a man of Ye in Nanyang; this Chengyang is probably a miswriting for Nanyang, unless another man of the same name existed.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Qian Daxin doubts Chengyang for Gao Feng.

sentences.zh: 按：張森楷校勘記謂南陽高鳳以建初元年為任隗所薦，尋卒，此在永元之時，則卒已久矣，蓋非一人。

translations.literal: Note: Zhang Senkai's collation says the Nanyang Gao Feng recommended by Ren Sui in Jianchu 1 soon died; this is Yongyuan times—so not one person.
translations.idiomatic: Collation argues two different men named Gao Feng.

sentences.zh: 錢說疑尚未審。

translations.literal: Qian's theory may not yet be settled.
translations.idiomatic: The identification remains disputed.

sentences.zh: 一四七九頁五行不務修舜禹周公之*

translations.literal: Page 1479 line 5: the phrase 'without cultivating the ways of Shun, Yu, and the Duke of Zhou' breaks off at the graph marked with an asterisk; the lacuna is supplied in the following collation line.
translations.idiomatic: Collation note for page 1479 line 5: the sentence about emulating Shun, Yu, and the Duke of Zhou was damaged mid-word; the missing wording is restored in the next entry.

translations.literal: (methods)
translations.idiomatic: Methods (the gloss supplied for the lacuna).

sentences.zh: **[德]*據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: **[virtue]*—emended per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Virtue restored from Ji and Palace.

sentences.zh: 按：今東觀記亦作「德」。

translations.literal: Note: present Eastern Han Records also reads virtue.
translations.idiomatic: The Eastern Han Records agrees on virtue.

sentences.zh: 一四七九頁一一行左傳曰齊崔氏出奔□按：校補謂此春秋宣公十年經文，「左傳」二字乃「春秋」之誤，各本皆未正。

translations.literal: Page 1479 line 11: Zuo Tradition says the house of Cui of Qi fled into exile—note: Collation supplement says this is Annals Duke Xuan year 10 text; Zuo Tradition should read Spring and Autumn; all editions wrongly retain Zuo.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: The citation should be Spring and Autumn, not Zuo.

sentences.zh: 一四八０頁三行後遷丹*

translations.literal: Page 1480 line 3: the clause 'later promoted to Dan...' ends with an asterisk because the place-name was corrupted; the correct reading is completed in the following collation line.
translations.idiomatic: Collation note for page 1480 line 3: the promotion line was split across entries after a corrupted graph in the place-name.

translations.literal: (Yang)
translations.idiomatic: Yang

sentences.zh: **[陽]*都尉據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: **[Yang]* commandant of cavalry—emended per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Danyang emended to Yang per editions.

sentences.zh: 一四八０頁四行何氏家傳*

translations.literal: Page 1480 line 4: the heading 'He family tradition' ends with an asterisk where the manuscript was damaged; the missing characters are supplied in the following collation line.
translations.idiomatic: Collation note for page 1480 line 4: the He genealogy quotation was broken at the asterisk and continues in the next fragment.

sentences.zh: (雲並)

translations.literal: (six generations)
translations.idiomatic: Six generations (the gloss supplied for the lacuna).

sentences.zh: **[六世]*祖父比干據汲本改。

translations.literal: **[six generations]* grandfather Bigan—emended per Ji edition.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Six generations restored from Ji.

sentences.zh: 按：「雲並」與「六世」形近而鬥。

translations.literal: Note: yun bing and liu shi are graphically similar and were confused.
translations.idiomatic: Collation explains a copying error between phrases.

sentences.zh: 一四八０頁一五行文成之世汲本﹑殿本「成」作「武」。

translations.literal: Page 1480 line 15: times of Wen and Cheng—Ji and Palace read Wu for Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Some editions read martial for Cheng in the ode context.

sentences.zh: 按：今本左傳亦作「文武之世」，汲本﹑殿本殆據今本左傳改也。

translations.literal: Note: present Zuo also reads times of Wen and Wu; Ji and Palace probably followed modern Zuo.
translations.idiomatic: Modern Zuo text favors Wen and Wu.

sentences.zh: 然阮元校勘記謂石經﹑宋本﹑岳本「武」作「成」，謂文公﹑成公也，則作「文成之世」是。

translations.literal: Yet Ruan Yuan's collation notes stone classic and Song and Yue editions read Cheng for Wu—meaning Dukes Wen and Cheng—so reading times of Wen and Cheng is correct.
translations.idiomatic: Ruan Yuan argues Cheng is correct for Dukes Wen and Cheng.

sentences.zh: 一四八一頁一二行治平之化按：「治」原斗「洽」，逕據汲本﹑殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 1481 line 12: transformation of good order—note: zhi was corrupted to qia and is emended per Ji and Palace.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Order graph restored from Ji and Palace.

sentences.zh: 一四八二頁二行豈但空空無違而已哉按：集解引通鑒胡注，謂「空」當作「悾」，悾悾，謹□也。

translations.literal: Page 1482 line 2: how can it be enough merely to commit no open fault—note: Collected Explanations cites Hu commentary to Tongjian: kong should read kong kong, diligent and cautious.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Hu Sanxing reads kong kong as diligent care.

sentences.zh: 一四八二頁一五行欲令農士女工安得奪其貨乎汲本「奪」作「售」。

translations.literal: Page 1482 line 15: wishing farmers and women workers to be able to seize their goods—Ji reads shou for duo.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji edition reads sell instead of seize.

sentences.zh: 刊誤謂案文「奪」當作「售」，「得」當作「所」。

translations.literal: Errata says duo should read shou and de should read suo.
translations.idiomatic: Errata proposes shou and suo.

sentences.zh: 按：史記循吏傳作「欲令農士女工安所讎其貨乎」。

translations.literal: Note: Records of the Exemplary Officials reads wishing farmers and women workers where would they vend their goods.
translations.idiomatic: Shiji parallel uses a different wording for market goods.

sentences.zh: 一四八三頁一行齊殤王按：刊誤謂「殤」當作「煬」。

translations.literal: Page 1483 line 1: Prince Shang of Qi—note: Errata says Shang should read Yang.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Prince Yang, not Shang.

sentences.zh: 一四八四頁二行嫚書之恥按：「嫚」原斗「慢」，逕據汲本﹑殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 1484 line 2: insulting letter's shame—note: man was corrupted to slow and is emended per Ji and Palace.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Insult graph restored from slow.

sentences.zh: 一四八五頁二行伏見大將軍憲按：汲本﹑殿本「憲」上有「竇」字。

translations.literal: Page 1485 line 2: your servant sees Grand General Xian—note: Ji and Palace add Dou before Xian.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji and Palace add the Dou surname.

sentences.zh: 一四八五頁一四行鄭武姜愛少子叔段按：「少」原斗「小」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1485 line 14: Lady Wu of Zheng loved younger son Shuduan—note: shao was corrupted to xiao and is emended.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Younger son graph corrected.

sentences.zh: 一四八六頁一二行比干生壽按：張森楷校勘記謂案漢書百官表及何武傳，壽是盧江人，與比干居郡絕遠，東觀記乃以為比干生壽，恐非也。

translations.literal: Page 1486 line 12: Bigan begat Shou—note: Zhang Senkai says checking Han tables and He Wu's biography, Shou was a man of Lujiang, far from Bigan's commandery; Eastern Han Records making Shou Bigan's son is probably wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Collation doubts the He genealogy on Shou.

sentences.zh: 一四八六頁一二行壽生顯按：張森楷校勘記謂案前書何武傳，壽子不見名字，名顯者乃武弟，非壽子也。

translations.literal: Page 1486 line 12: Shou begat Xian—note: Zhang Senkai says He Wu's biography does not give Shou's son's name; the one named Xian is Wu's younger brother, not Shou's son.
translations.idiomatic: Collation doubts Xian as Shou's son.

sentences.zh: 一四八七頁一０行三遷五官中郎將按：校補引錢大昭說，謂張酺傳作「左中郎將」。

translations.literal: Page 1487 line 10: thrice promoted to gentleman of the five offices—note: Collation supplement cites Qian Dazhao: Zhang Fu's biography reads left gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Zhang Fu's parallel reads left gentleman instead of five offices.

sentences.zh: 一四八七頁一四行故能挾幼主*[之]*斷據刊誤補。

translations.literal: Page 1487 line 14: therefore able to support the young lord *[in]* judgment—supplemented per Errata.
